# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "visual representation" in the context of data visualization. State the formal mapping function used to describe it.**

**Model Answer:**
A visual representation is a graphical encoding of data that translates abstract values into perceptible visual marks (points, lines, bars, areas, colors) to enable pattern recognition and decision-making through the human visual system. Formally, it is expressed as:

$$V = f(D, E, C)$$

where $V$ is the visual output, $D$ is the dataset, $E$ is the encoding (mapping of data to visual channels), and $C$ denotes contextual constraints (audience, medium, design grammar). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the function, 1 for explaining each term.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the five steps involved in creating a visual representation.**

**Model Answer:**
The five steps (Card-Mackinlay-Shneiderman reference model) are:
1. **Data Collection & Understanding** — identify variables, types, scales.
2. **Data Transformation** — filter, aggregate, normalize, derive.
3. **Visual Encoding** — map each variable to a visual channel respecting perceptual accuracy.
4. **View Transformation** — apply axes, scales, sorting, faceting.
5. **Interaction & Interpretation** — enable zoom, filter, brush, annotate.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for the list, 1.5 for the brief explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain **Bertin's seven visual variables** with their suitability for nominal, ordinal, and quantitative data. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Cleveland-McGill hierarchy of perceptual accuracy**. Using this hierarchy, justify why a **bar chart** is preferred over a **pie chart** for comparing quantities across categories. *(7 marks)*

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-stage pipeline for creating a visual representation** ($D_{raw} \to T \to E \to V_T \to I \to V_{final}$). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the following dataset of monthly sales (in ₹ lakhs) for a KTU-registered startup, design a suitable visual representation. Justify your choice using the perceptual accuracy ranking and the pre-attentive processing principle. *(7 marks)*

| Month | Sales (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|
| Jan | 12 |
| Feb | 18 |
| Mar | 15 |
| Apr | 22 |
| May | 28 |
| Jun | 25 |

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a) — Bertin's Visual Variables

Jacques Bertin (1967) identified **seven retinal visual variables** used to encode data in visual representations:

| # | Variable | Nominal | Ordinal | Quantitative | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Position** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | X-Y coordinates of a point |
| 2 | **Size** | Limited | ✓ | ✓ | Bubble radius |
| 3 | **Shape** | ✓ | Limited | ✗ | Circle, triangle, square |
| 4 | **Value (lightness)** | Limited | ✓ | Limited | Light gray to dark gray |
| 5 | **Color (hue)** | ✓ | Limited | ✗ | Red, blue, green |
| 6 | **Orientation** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Tilted lines |
| 7 | **Texture** | ✓ | ✓ | Limited | Dotted, dashed, solid |

- **Position** is the most powerful — it is the *only* variable that can simultaneously encode nominal, ordinal, and quantitative data with high accuracy.
- **Size and Value** support ordinal and quantitative perception.
- **Hue** is best for nominal categories (it has no inherent order — "red is not greater than blue").
- **Shape and Orientation** are good for distinguishing nominal groups but not for ordered or quantitative readings.

*(7 marks: 2 for the list, 3 for the data-type suitability table, 2 for the comparative summary.)*

### Solution to Question A (b) — Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy and Bar vs Pie

**Cleveland-McGill Perceptual Accuracy Ranking (1984):**

1. Position along a common scale *(most accurate)*
2. Position along non-aligned scales
3. Length, direction, angle
4. Area
5. Volume, curvature
6. Shading, color saturation
7. Color hue *(least accurate)*

**Why a Bar Chart is preferred over a Pie Chart:**

- A **bar chart** uses **length on a common scale** (the y-axis) — ranked **#1** in perceptual accuracy. Humans are exceptionally good at comparing lengths aligned to a common baseline.
- A **pie chart** uses **angle** — ranked **#3** (jointly with length), but in practice angle estimation is **much worse** than length estimation because there is no common baseline to align angles. Research shows that angle judgments can be off by **±10° to ±25°**, while length judgments are accurate within **±2%**.
- Additionally, a pie chart can only encode **one variable** (proportions of a whole), while a bar chart can encode **two or more** through grouping, stacking, or small multiples.
- A bar chart supports **pre-attentive comparison** (the eye snaps to the tallest bar in < 250 ms) whereas a pie chart requires the viewer to mentally compute angles and convert to percentages.

**Conclusion:** A bar chart is the perceptually superior choice whenever the goal is to compare quantities across categories.

*(7 marks: 2 for the ranking, 3 for the comparison, 1 for the quantitative accuracy claim, 1 for the pre-attentive justification.)*

### Solution to Question B (a) — Five-Stage Pipeline

The five-stage pipeline for creating a visual representation, also called the **Information Visualization Reference Model**, is:

**Stage 1 — Data Collection:** Identify the raw dataset $D_{raw}$ with its variables, types, and scales.

**Stage 2 — Data Transformation (T):** Filter, aggregate, normalize, bin, and derive. Output: $D_{tidy} = T(D_{raw})$.

**Stage 3 — Visual Encoding (E):** Map each variable to a visual channel (position, length, color, shape, etc.) respecting perceptual accuracy (Cleveland-McGill, Bertin). Output: $E(D_{tidy})$.

**Stage 4 — View Transformation ($V_T$):** Apply scales (linear, log), axes, legends, sorting, faceting (small multiples).

**Stage 5 — Interaction (I):** Zoom, pan, filter, brush, link, details-on-demand, annotations.

**Composed pipeline:**

$$V_{final} = I \circ V_T \circ E \circ T(D_{raw})$$

```
Raw Data  -->  Transform  -->  Encode  -->  View Transform  -->  Interact  -->  Final Visual
   D_raw        T(D)           E(T(D))      V_T(E(T(D)))        I(...)        V_final
```

*(7 marks: 1.5 per stage, 0.5 for the composed equation and a neat diagram.)*

### Solution to Question B (b) — Designing a Visual for the Sales Dataset

**Step 1 — Data understanding:**
- 1 temporal variable: Month (Jan–Jun).
- 1 quantitative variable: Sales in ₹ Lakh (ratio scale, range 12–28).
- Small dataset ($N = 6$), single series.

**Step 2 — Recommended visual: Line chart with annotated data points.**

**Step 3 — Justification using the principles:**

1. **Time-series structure** — A line chart is the canonical choice when the independent variable is *temporal* because the line preserves *temporal continuity* and makes *trends* (rising, falling, peaks) immediately visible. A bar chart would also work, but a line emphasizes the continuous flow of time.

2. **Perceptual accuracy** — The line chart uses **position-on-common-scale** for both the x-axis (months) and y-axis (sales), placing it at **rank #1** in Cleveland-McGill's hierarchy.

3. **Pre-attentive cues** — Adding a **highlight color** (e.g., red for the peak month May = ₹28 Lakh) leverages **color hue** as a pre-attentive feature, drawing the eye to the maximum in < 250 ms. Adding **data labels** above each point provides details-on-demand.

4. **Avoid pie chart** — There is no "whole" being partitioned, and angle perception (rank #3) is inferior to position (rank #1).

5. **Avoid 3D** — 3D introduces occlusion, perspective distortion, and violates the *data-ink ratio* principle of Tufte.

**Final answer description:**

```
Sales (Lakh)
  30 |
  28 |                          ●  ← peak (highlighted)
  25 |                  ●  ●
  22 |              ●
  18 |      ●
  15 |  ●  ●
  12 |  ●
      |________________________________
        Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May  Jun
                     Month
```

*(7 marks: 1 for recommending line chart, 1 for time-series justification, 2 for perceptual accuracy + pre-attentive justification, 1 for ruling out pie/3D, 2 for the neat sketch with annotations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Listing Bertin's variables without mapping to data types** — full marks require the suitability column.
> 2. **Confusing Bertin (1967) with Cleveland-McGill (1984)** — they are different frameworks; Bertin is about *what variables exist*, Cleveland-McGill is about *how accurately they are perceived*.
> 3. **Recommending pie charts for non-parts-of-a-whole data** — automatic 1–2 mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the data-transformation step** — many students jump straight to chart choice. Always mention $T$ explicitly.
> 5. **Using 3D effects** — examiners treat 3D charts as chartjunk and deduct marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to label axes and units** — KTU strictly requires axis labels with units (e.g., "Sales in ₹ Lakh").
> 7. **Not citing pre-attentive processing (< 250 ms)** — this is a *high-yield keyword* that examiners scan for.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Creating Visual Representations:**

- **Definition:** A visual representation is a graphical encoding of data, $V = f(D, E, C)$.
- **5-step pipeline:** $D_{raw} \to T \to E \to V_T \to I \to V_{final}$.
- **Bertin's 7 visual variables:** Position, Size, Shape, Value, Color, Orientation, Texture.
- **Position is the most accurate channel** — it is the only one that works for all data types.
- **Cleveland-McGill ranking (best → worst):** Position-on-scale > Position-non-aligned > Length/Direction/Angle > Area > Volume/Curvature > Shading/Saturation > Hue.
- **Miller's Law:** $7 \pm 2$ chunks — limit visual elements to maintain comprehension.
- **Pre-attentive threshold:** < **250 ms** — use color, size, orientation for instant detection.
- **Color rules:** Hue for nominal (≤ 12 colors), Saturation/Value for ordinal, sequential ramps for quantitative.
- **Chart-to-data-type mapping:** 1 quant → histogram/box; 1 cat + 1 quant → bar; 2 quant → scatter; time → line; parts-of-whole → stacked bar/treemap (use pie sparingly).
- **Tufte's principles:** Maximize data-ink ratio; Lie Factor = 1 means honest.
- **Avoid:** 3D effects, pie charts with > 5 slices, dual axes, truncated y-axes, chartjunk.
- **Always include:** axis labels with units, legend, title, source citation, and a one-sentence takeaway.
- **High-yield keywords for KTU answers:** *perceptual accuracy*, *pre-attentive*, *visual channel*, *encoding*, *preattentive features*, *Cleveland-McGill*, *Bertin*, *information visualization reference model*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visualization Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visualization Reference Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Visualization Reference Model** is a formal, generalized abstraction of the entire data visualization pipeline. It is a *blueprint* that explicitly decomposes the transformation of raw, abstract data into a meaningful, perceivable visual image into a sequence of well-defined mathematical and perceptual stages. The most academically influential and KTU-prescribed reference model is the one proposed by **Card, Mackinlay, and Shneiderman (1999)** in their seminal work *"Readings in Information Visualization: Using Vision to Think"*.

The model formally represents the visualization process as a sequence of four cascading data transformations, where the output of one stage becomes the structured input of the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECPT745):**
> The reference model is the foundational architecture of *every* visualization system. Examiners frequently ask students to **draw, label, and explain the four stages** and the operators acting between them. Memorizing the exact stage names and operator notations is mandatory for **Part B (14 mark)** questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are a **cartographer (map-maker)** creating a map of a city from raw municipal data:

1. **Stage 1 – Data Table Creation (T₁):** You first take a chaotic pile of handwritten survey notes and organize them into a neat spreadsheet with columns like `Population`, `Latitude`, `Longitude`, `Area`. This is converting raw data into a structured data table.
2. **Stage 2 – Data Analysis (T₂):** You then perform calculations — e.g., computing population density = `Population / Area` — generating a *new derived column* that wasn't in the original data.
3. **Stage 3 – Visual Mapping (M):** You decide that "high density" will be shown as "red color" and "low density" as "blue color." This binds abstract data attributes to visual channels.
4. **Stage 4 – View Transformation (T₃ / V):** You project the spherical Earth onto a flat 2D paper map using mathematical projection techniques (like Mercator projection) so your audience can see it.

**That entire four-step journey — from messy notes to a finished map on paper — is the Visualization Reference Model.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The reference model is essentially a *conveyor belt* in a factory. Raw material (data) enters at one end, gets progressively shaped (transformed, filtered, mapped) at each workstation, and finally emerges as a finished product (the visual image seen on screen).

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Constants

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Transformation (T)** | Operations that change the structure or form of data without altering its meaning. | Stage 1 & Stage 2 |
| **Visual Mapping (M)** | The binding of data attributes to visual variables (position, color, size). | Stage 3 |
| **View Transformation (V)** | Operations that alter the geometry/projection of the visual structure. | Stage 4 |
| **Derived Data** | New data values computed from raw data (e.g., ratios, aggregations). | Output of Stage 2 |
| **Visual Structure** | A collection of visual marks, their attributes, and spatial relationships. | Output of Stage 3 |
| **View** | The final rendered image on the display device. | Output of Stage 4 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Stages of the Reference Model

The Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman model defines visualization as a *composition of mappings*. The pipeline proceeds as follows:

**Stage 1: Raw Data → Data Tables (Data Transformation)**
- Operator: $T_{1}$ (a *Data Transformation* function)
- The unstructured, often semi-formatted raw data (logs, CSV fragments, sensor outputs) is normalized into a relational structure: rows = records, columns = attributes.
- Sub-operations include **cleaning, normalization, schema-mapping, and type-casting**.

**Stage 2: Data Tables → Derived Data (Data Transformation)**
- Operator: $T_{2}$ (another *Data Transformation*, often implemented as analytical functions)
- Operations here are computational: aggregations (mean, median, count), correlations, regressions, cluster identification.
- Output is a *new* data table that contains computed/derived attributes not originally present.

**Stage 3: Derived Data → Visual Structures (Visual Mapping)**
- Operator: $M$ (a *Visual Mapping* function)
- This is the **semantic heart** of the model. Each data attribute (quantitative, ordinal, nominal) is bound to a specific **visual variable** (channel).
- Examples: `latitude` → x-position; `temperature` → color hue; `magnitude` → size of a circle.

**Stage 4: Visual Structures → Views (View Transformation)**
- Operator: $V$ (a *View Transformation* function)
- Performs geometric operations: zooming, panning, clipping, perspective projection, 2D/3D rendering.
- Output is the final pixels rendered on the output device.

### 2.2 Why the Model Matters in Engineering

In real-world engineering — from **medical imaging (CT scans)** to **CFD simulations of aircraft wings** — the reference model ensures that:
- **Reproducibility:** Any visualization can be mathematically specified by composing its transformations.
- **Modularity:** Each stage can be independently optimized (e.g., a faster projection algorithm at Stage 4).
- **Validation:** Errors in data (Stage 1–2) versus errors in rendering (Stage 4) can be localized.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The visualization reference model itself is **descriptive**, not numerical. However, the *visual mapping function* is mathematical, and the KTU board expects familiarity with the following notations.

| Symbol / Notation | Definition | Stage | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D_{\text{raw}}$ | Raw, unstructured input data | Input | Domain-specific (sensor units, text, etc.) |
| $D_{\text{table}}$ | Structured relational table | After $T_{1}$ | Rows $\times$ Columns |
| $D_{\text{derived}}$ | Data with computed attributes | After $T_{2}$ | Same as $D_{\text{table}}$, augmented |
| $S$ | Visual structure (set of marks) | After $M$ | Geometric domain (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$) |
| $V_{f}$ | Final rendered view | After $V$ | Pixel buffer (2D array) |
| $M : D_{\text{derived}} \rightarrow S$ | Visual mapping function | Stage 3 | Maps attribute values to visual channels |
| $T_{1} : D_{\text{raw}} \rightarrow D_{\text{table}}$ | Data transformation 1 | Stage 1 | Functional mapping |
| $T_{2} : D_{\text{table}} \rightarrow D_{\text{derived}}$ | Data transformation 2 | Stage 2 | Functional mapping |
| $V : S \rightarrow V_{f}$ | View transformation | Stage 4 | Geometric / projective mapping |
| $\mathcal{C} = \{c_{1}, c_{2}, \dots, c_{k}\}$ | Set of $k$ visual channels (e.g., position, hue) | Stage 3 inputs | Categorical set |
| $A = \{a_{1}, a_{2}, \dots, a_{n}\}$ | Set of $n$ data attributes | Stage 3 inputs | Domain-specific |

### 2.4 Composite Function Form

The entire visualization process can be expressed as a single composite function:

$$V_{f} = V \circ M \circ T_{2} \circ T_{1} (D_{\text{raw}})$$

This compact form is a high-yield KTU answer point: it proves the student understands that the model is a *pipeline composition*.

### 2.5 Visual Channel Hierarchy (Bertin's Semiology, integrated by Card et al.)

Card et al. extended Bertin's classic visual variable framework. The following ordering is the standard high-yield table for KTU:

| Visual Channel | Best For (Data Type) | Perceptual Accuracy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position (x, y) | Quantitative, Nominal | **Highest** |
| Length | Quantitative | High |
| Angle / Slope | Quantitative | Moderate |
| Area | Quantitative | Moderate |
| Color Hue | Nominal / Categorical | High (for groups) |
| Color Intensity (Saturation) | Ordinal / Quantitative | Moderate |
| Shape | Nominal | High |
| Motion (Flicker) | All types | Very High (attention-grabbing) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Mapping *quantitative* data to *hue* is a common mistake students make. Hue is **categorical**, not ordinal. Quantitative data should be mapped to position, length, or intensity — this distinction can fetch full marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Pipeline

We now formally derive the four-stage reference model step-by-step. This derivation is the kind of structured argument KTU examiners expect for full marks on a 7-mark sub-part.

**Step 1 — Define the raw data universe.**
The input to any visualization system is a collection of unstructured, often heterogeneous observations. We define this as a set $D_{\text{raw}}$, which may contain mixed types (numerical, textual, temporal, spatial). In practice, $D_{\text{raw}}$ may be ill-formed (missing values, inconsistent encodings).

**Step 2 — Apply the first data transformation $T_{1}$.**
$T_{1}$ is an operator that converts $D_{\text{raw}}$ into a structured relational form. Formally:

$$D_{\text{table}} = T_{1}(D_{\text{raw}}) = \{r_{i} \in A \times \dots \times A \mid i = 1, \dots, n\}$$

where each record $r_{i}$ is a tuple over a fixed schema of attributes $A = \{a_{1}, a_{2}, \dots, a_{n}\}$, and $n$ is the total number of records. Each attribute $a_{j}$ has a defined type: $\text{type}(a_{j}) \in \{\text{quantitative}, \text{ordinal}, \text{nominal}, \text{temporal}\}$.

*Why this step?* Without a tabular structure, downstream analytical and visual operations cannot be applied uniformly. $T_{1}$ is therefore the **schema-enforcement** step.

**Step 3 — Apply the second data transformation $T_{2}$.**
$T_{2}$ operates on the table $D_{\text{table}}$ and produces a *new* table with derived attributes. Formally:

$$D_{\text{derived}} = T_{2}(D_{\text{table}}) = D_{\text{table}} \cup \{a_{n+1}, a_{n+2}, \dots, a_{n+m}\}$$

where $a_{n+k}$ for $k = 1, \dots, m$ are *derived attributes* computed by analytical functions $f_{k}$. For example:

$$a_{n+1} = f_{1}(a_{1}, a_{2}) = \frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}$$

This represents a *density-like* computation. The derived table preserves all original records and adds new attribute columns.

*Why this step?* Many visualizations convey *insights* (ratios, trends, clusters) that are not present in raw data. $T_{2}$ is the **insight-generation** step.

**Step 4 — Apply the visual mapping $M$.**
$M$ binds each data attribute to a visual channel. Formally:

$$S = M(D_{\text{derived}}, \mathcal{C})$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \{c_{1}, c_{2}, \dots, c_{k}\}$ is the set of available visual channels. A typical assignment is:

$$a_{1} \mapsto c_{1} \quad (\text{e.g., latitude} \rightarrow x\text{-position})$$
$$a_{2} \mapsto c_{2} \quad (\text{e.g., longitude} \rightarrow y\text{-position})$$
$$a_{n+1} \mapsto c_{3} \quad (\text{e.g., density} \rightarrow \text{color intensity})$$

The output $S$ is the *visual structure*: a set of marks (points, lines, areas) with assigned visual properties.

*Why this step?* This is the **semantic translation** step — converting abstract data into perceptual variables the human eye can decode efficiently.

**Step 5 — Apply the view transformation $V$.**
$V$ maps the visual structure $S$ into a rendered image $V_{f}$:

$$V_{f} = V(S)$$

$V$ includes operations like viewport selection (zoom level), projection (orthographic, perspective), anti-aliasing, and rasterization. The output is a 2D pixel buffer suitable for display on a monitor or print.

*Why this step?* The visual structure $S$ exists in a continuous mathematical space; $V$ discretizes it for the finite-resolution display device.

**Step 6 — Compose the full pipeline.**
Chaining Steps 1 through 5 via function composition:

$$V_{f} = V(M(T_{2}(T_{1}(D_{\text{raw}}))))$$

which is read right-to-left: raw data is first tabularized ($T_{1}$), then analytically enriched ($T_{2}$), then visually encoded ($M$), and finally rendered ($V$). This single expression captures the entire reference model.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Visualizing City Climate Data

To ground the derivation, consider a small dataset of 3 cities with attributes: `City`, `Latitude`, `Longitude`, `Population`, `Area`. We trace the pipeline:

**Input $D_{\text{raw}}$:** Unstructured text/CSV fragment:
```
Kochi, 9.97 N, 76.28 E, 677k, 94 km^2
Trivandrum, 8.52 N, 76.94 E, 957k, 200 km^2
Kozhikode, 11.25 N, 75.78 E, 609k, 118 km^2
```

**After $T_{1}$ — the structured data table:**

| City | Latitude | Longitude | Population | Area |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Kochi | 9.97 | 76.28 | 677000 | 94 |
| Trivandrum | 8.52 | 76.94 | 957000 | 200 |
| Kozhikode | 11.25 | 75.78 | 609000 | 118 |

**After $T_{2}$ — the derived data table (density added):**

For each record, compute $\text{Density} = \text{Population} \div \text{Area}$. For Kochi: $677000 \div 94 \approx 7202$. Adding the column:

| City | Latitude | Longitude | Density |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Kochi | 9.97 | 76.28 | 7202 |
| Trivandrum | 8.52 | 76.94 | 4785 |
| Kozhikode | 11.25 | 75.78 | 5161 |

**After $M$ — visual structure assignments:**
- `Latitude` $\rightarrow$ `x-position`
- `Longitude` $\rightarrow$ `y-position`
- `Density` $\rightarrow$ `circle area` (size of point)
- `City` $\rightarrow$ `text label` (shape channel)

**After $V$ — final view:** A 2D scatter map on screen where each city appears as a circle of varying size at its geographic coordinates.

This end-to-end trace is a typical KTU 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Reference Model Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw["RAW DATA"] -->|T1 Data Transform| table["DATA TABLES"]
    table -->|T2 Data Transform| derived["DERIVED DATA"]
    derived -->|M Visual Mapping| visual["VISUAL STRUCTURE"]
    visual -->|V View Transform| view["FINAL VIEW ON DISPLAY"]
    table -.->|Selection Filter S| derived
    derived -.->|Selection Filter S| visual
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows represent the *primary pipeline*. The dashed arrows represent **Selection (filtering) operations** that can be applied *between* stages to focus on subsets of data — a critical extension mentioned in the KTU syllabus.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        nodeA["Sensors, Logs, Databases, CSVs"]
    end
    subgraph stage1["STAGE 1: DATA TRANSFORM T1"]
        nodeB["Cleaning, Normalization, Schema Mapping"]
    end
    subgraph stage2["STAGE 2: DATA TRANSFORM T2"]
        nodeC["Aggregation, Correlation, Regression, Clustering"]
    end
    subgraph stage3["STAGE 3: VISUAL MAPPING M"]
        nodeD["Attribute to Channel Binding"]
        nodeE["Channels: Position, Color, Size, Shape"]
    end
    subgraph stage4["STAGE 4: VIEW TRANSFORM V"]
        nodeF["Projection, Zoom, Pan, Clipping"]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        nodeG["Rendered Image on Display"]
    end
    inputLayer --> stage1
    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 --> outputLayer
```

### 4.3 Mapping Data Types to Channels (Decision Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q["Quantitative Data"] -->|Best Match| ch1["Position or Length"]
    o["Ordinal Data"] -->|Best Match| ch2["Ordered Hue or Intensity"]
    n["Nominal Data"] -->|Best Match| ch3["Distinct Hue or Shape"]
    t["Temporal Data"] -->|Best Match| ch4["Time Axis Position"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram is high-yield:** KTU frequently tests whether students know the *correct* channel for a given data type. The wrong mapping (e.g., nominal data → length) is a common pitfall and is explicitly penalized in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** Define the term **Visualization Reference Model**. Mention the authors and the year it was proposed.
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Visualization Reference Model is a generalized, formal abstraction of the data visualization pipeline that decomposes the transformation of raw data into a perceivable image into a sequence of well-defined stages. It was proposed by **Card, Mackinlay, and Shneiderman in 1999** in their work *"Readings in Information Visualization"*. The model consists of four canonical stages: Raw Data → Data Tables → Derived Data → Visual Structures → Views. *(Full 3 marks if the year and all four stages are mentioned.)*

> **Q2.** List the four stages of the Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman visualization reference model and state the operator that acts between each consecutive pair of stages.
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. Raw Data → Data Tables : operator **$T_{1}$ (Data Transformation)**
> 2. Data Tables → Derived Data : operator **$T_{2}$ (Data Transformation)**
> 3. Derived Data → Visual Structures : operator **$M$ (Visual Mapping)**
> 4. Visual Structures → Views : operator **$V$ (View Transformation)**
> *(Each correctly labeled transition fetches approximately 0.75 marks. Full 3 marks require all four.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman Visualization Reference Model**. Describe each of its four stages in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **role of data transformation** ($T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$) in the reference model. Illustrate with a real-world example showing how raw data is progressively converted to derived data. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman reference model, proposed in 1999, formalizes visualization as a four-stage pipeline:

1. **Stage 1 — Raw Data to Data Tables ($T_{1}$):** The operator $T_{1}$ normalizes heterogeneous, often messy raw inputs (e.g., text logs, mixed-format CSVs) into a well-defined relational table with rows as records and columns as attributes. Operations include data cleaning, type-casting, and schema mapping. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Stage 2 — Data Tables to Derived Data ($T_{2}$):** The operator $T_{2}$ applies analytical functions to compute new attributes (e.g., density, ratios, rolling averages). The output $D_{\text{derived}}$ augments the original table with these computed columns. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Stage 3 — Derived Data to Visual Structures ($M$):** The visual mapping function $M$ binds each data attribute to a visual channel drawn from a set $\mathcal{C} = \{$position, length, hue, size, shape, $\dots\}$. The output $S$ is a set of marks with assigned visual properties. *(2 marks)*

4. **Stage 4 — Visual Structures to Views ($V$):** The view transformation $V$ applies geometric and rendering operations (projection, zoom, clipping, anti-aliasing) to produce the final rendered image $V_{f}$ on the display device. *(1.5 marks)*

The composite form $V_{f} = V \circ M \circ T_{2} \circ T_{1} (D_{\text{raw}})$ summarizes the entire pipeline. *(0.5 marks for the composite expression.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Data transformation encompasses Stages 1 and 2, and is responsible for converting raw observations into a form suitable for visual encoding.

- **$T_{1}$ ensures *structural integrity*:** Without $T_{1}$, downstream stages cannot assume a consistent schema. Example: a hospital's patient records arriving in different formats (PDF, free text, HL7 messages) are normalized into a single table with attributes like `PatientID`, `Age`, `BP_Systolic`, `BP_Diastolic`, `Date`. *(2 marks)*

- **$T_{2}$ ensures *semantic enrichment*:** The raw table is insufficient for insight — one wants to see "average BP per age group" or "trend over time." $T_{2}$ computes these: $a_{n+1} = \text{Mean Pressure} = \frac{BP_{\text{Systolic}} + 2 \cdot BP_{\text{Diastolic}}}{3}$. The derived table now contains this new column. *(2 marks)*

- **Real-world example trace (3 marks):**
  - *Input:* 1000 raw BP readings in unstructured text.
  - *After $T_{1}$:* Tabular form with columns `PatientID`, `BP_Systolic`, `BP_Diastolic`, `Date`.
  - *After $T_{2}$:* Table augmented with `Mean Arterial Pressure` and `Age_Group` (derived via binning).
  - *Final insight:* This derived table is what Stage 3 will visually encode — e.g., a scatter plot of `Age_Group` vs `Mean Pressure` with `Date` as a temporal axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A frequent mistake is **conflating $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$** into a single "data transformation" step. The model *explicitly* separates them: $T_{1}$ is structural, $T_{2}$ is analytical. Failing to distinguish costs at least **1.5 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part. Also, do not describe Stage 3 or Stage 4 inside your answer to part (b) — examiners reward strict stage-wise focus.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternate]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **visual mapping ($M$)** in the reference model. Discuss Bertin's visual variables and explain how data types determine channel selection with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **view transformation ($V$)** stage. Discuss at least three operations performed at this stage and their effect on the final rendered view. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **visual mapping $M$** is the operator at Stage 3 of the reference model. It is a function that binds data attributes to visual channels (also called visual variables), producing a *visual structure* $S$ as output:

$$S = M(D_{\text{derived}}, \mathcal{C})$$

**Bertin's Visual Variables (Jacques Bertin, 1967, extended by Card et al.):** Bertin identified seven primitive visual variables that the human visual system can perceive and decode: **position, size, shape, value (lightness), color (hue), orientation, and texture.** Card et al. later added **motion** as an eighth. *(2 marks)*

**Data-type to channel selection — the core rule:** The *type* of a data attribute determines the *appropriate* channel:

- **Quantitative data** (e.g., temperature, population) is best mapped to **position** or **length** because the human visual system can compare these channels with the highest perceptual accuracy. Example: temperature on the x-axis. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Ordinal data** (e.g., education level: low/medium/high) is best mapped to **ordered hue** (light-to-dark) or **size** because the channel must preserve rank ordering. Example: education level → grayscale intensity. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Nominal data** (e.g., country name, blood group) is best mapped to **distinct hue** or **shape** because the channel has no inherent order. Example: blood group → different colored markers. *(2 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The **view transformation $V$** is the operator at Stage 4 of the reference model. It maps the visual structure $S$ (an abstract geometric arrangement) into the final rendered view $V_{f}$ (a 2D pixel buffer on the display device):

$$V_{f} = V(S)$$

$V$ performs several categories of operations:

1. **Projection (2 marks):** Converts higher-dimensional visual structures into a 2D displayable form. For 3D scatter plots, a *perspective projection* or *orthographic projection* is applied. For geographic visualizations, map projections (e.g., Mercator, Robinson) translate spherical coordinates onto a flat plane.

2. **Viewport / Zoom / Pan (2 marks):** Selects the visible region of the visual structure and scales it to the display dimensions. Zooming magnifies a subregion; panning shifts the viewport. These are *navigation* operations that do not change the underlying visual structure $S$, only the rendered view.

3. **Clipping and Culling (1.5 marks):** Removes parts of the visual structure that fall outside the viewport or are occluded by foreground objects. This reduces rendering load and improves visual clarity.

4. **Anti-aliasing and Rasterization (1.5 marks):** Discretizes the continuous geometric primitives (lines, polygons) into pixels with smoothing to reduce jagged edges.

**Illustrative example:** In a 3D architectural visualization of a building, $M$ binds wall positions to 3D coordinates and door positions to colored marks. $V$ then applies a perspective projection (so the building looks 3D), a zoom operation (so a specific room is enlarged), and clipping (so walls behind the camera are not drawn). The final $V_{f}$ is the rendered image displayed on screen.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> A very common error in part (a) is listing *all* of Bertin's variables as equally suitable for all data types. Examiners deduct marks if the student fails to articulate that **position is the most perceptually accurate** for quantitative data and that **hue has no inherent order**, making it unsuitable for ordinal data. For part (b), students often forget to mention that $V$ is a *purely geometric* operation that does not change the underlying data semantics — losing a critical conceptual mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Visualization Reference Model**

- **Origin & Year:** Proposed by **Card, Mackinlay, and Shneiderman in 1999** in *"Readings in Information Visualization."* Frequently asked in 1-mark sub-questions.
- **The Four Stages (Memorize the Order):**
  1. Raw Data → Data Tables ($T_{1}$)
  2. Data Tables → Derived Data ($T_{2}$)
  3. Derived Data → Visual Structures ($M$)
  4. Visual Structures → Views ($V$)
- **The Composite Form:** $V_{f} = V \circ M \circ T_{2} \circ T_{1}(D_{\text{raw}})$ — write this in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer to earn an *extra* conceptual mark.
- **Distinguish $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$:** $T_{1}$ is *structural normalization*; $T_{2}$ is *analytical enrichment*. Never merge them.
- **Visual Mapping is the Semantic Core:** $M$ binds data attributes to visual channels (Bertin's variables). This is where the choice of *position vs hue vs size* determines whether the visualization is perceptually effective.
- **Data Type to Channel Rule:**
  - Quantitative → Position / Length
  - Ordinal → Ordered Intensity / Size
  - Nominal → Distinct Hue / Shape
  - Temporal → Time-axis Position
- **View Transformation is Geometric, Not Semantic:** $V$ performs projection, zoom, pan, clipping — it does *not* change the meaning of the data.
- **Selection / Filtering Operator ($S$):** Mention this as a *cross-stage* operation that can be applied between any two consecutive stages to focus on data subsets. Many students forget this and lose a mark.
- **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Mapping nominal data to *length* (a quantitative channel) or quantitative data to *hue* (a categorical channel) — both are perceptually incorrect and are penalized.
- **KTU's Most Repeated Question Pattern:** "Explain the visualization reference model with a neat diagram and describe each stage with an example." — practice writing a clean, labeled diagram plus 3–4 lines per stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Mapping (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> **Visual Mapping** is the systematic process of transforming abstract, raw data attributes (variables) into perceivable visual attributes (channels) such as position, length, size, color, shape, and orientation on a two-dimensional display surface. It is the *core cognitive-to-perceptual bridge* in any data visualization system, governed by the fundamental equation:
> $$\text{Visualization} = f(\text{Data}) \rightarrow \text{Visual Encoding} \rightarrow \text{Visual Image}$$

The discipline of visual mapping sits at the intersection of **computer graphics, perceptual psychology, and information design**. According to the KTU 2024 PECPT745 syllabus (Module 1), visual mapping is treated as the foundational engine that determines *how accurately, efficiently, and intuitively* a viewer can extract meaning from a graphical representation. The mapping must respect the **semantics of the data type** (Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio) and the **capabilities of the human visual system**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for B.Tech Students**
> Think of visual mapping as a **skilled translator at the United Nations**. The data (in its native language of numbers and categories) cannot be understood by the *human eye* directly. The visual mapping acts as the translator who converts these raw numbers into a language the *human visual cortex* understands fluently — **shapes, sizes, colors, and positions**.

| Element in the Analogy | Real-World Counterpart | Visualization Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| The Speaker | Raw dataset (Excel rows) | Data variables |
| The Translator | Mapping algorithm | Visual encoding function |
| The Audience | Human viewer's eyes & brain | Perceptual system |
| The Speech (output) | Verbal sentences in audience's language | Final visual image |
| Accuracy of translation | Quality of the message conveyed | **Effectiveness of the chart** |

> **Just like a bad translator can distort the original message (e.g., turning "I agree" into "I disagree"), a poor visual mapping can mislead the audience — making the same dataset tell two completely opposite stories!**

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Visual Mapping

Every effective visual mapping must balance three competing concerns, often visualized as a triangle:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Data Integrity** — The mapping must not distort, lie, or hide the underlying data truth.
> 2. **Perceptual Accuracy** — The mapping must exploit how the human eye *actually* perceives (e.g., we judge **length** far more accurately than **area**).
> 3. **Aesthetic Clarity** — The mapping must be clean, uncluttered, and pre-attentively parseable.

## 1.4 What is NOT a Visual Mapping?

It is equally important to delineate the boundary:

- **Visual mapping is NOT drawing** — Drawing is the rendering of geometry. Mapping is the *decision* of which geometry to use.
- **Visual mapping is NOT data analysis** — Analysis (mean, median, regression) happens *before* mapping.
- **Visual mapping is NOT chart selection alone** — Chart selection is one *output* of the mapping decision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Visual Mapping

A complete visual mapping operation consists of three logical stages, executed in sequence:

### Stage 1 — Data Decomposition
The raw dataset $D$ is broken into a set of attributes (variables) $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\}$. Each attribute $a_i$ belongs to a specific **measurement level** in S. S. Stevens' classification:

| Level | Mathematical Operators Allowed | Example | Examples in Engineering |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal** | $=, \neq$ | Blood group, Material type | Steel, Concrete, Wood |
| **Ordinal** | $=, \neq, <, >$ | Hardness scale (Mohs), Ranks | Low/Medium/High stress |
| **Interval** | $=, \neq, <, >, +, -$ | Temperature (°C), pH | Calibrated sensor reading |
| **Ratio** | $=, \neq, <, >, +, -, \times, \div$ | Length, Mass, Voltage | Beam length (m) |

### Stage 2 — Channel Selection
For each attribute $a_i$, the designer selects a **visual channel** $c_j$ from a pre-defined set. The selection is constrained by the attribute's measurement level. A mismatch (e.g., mapping a *ratio* variable to a *hue* channel) results in **perceptual falsehood**.

### Stage 3 — Encoding & Rendering
The selected channel is parameterized with a specific value drawn from the attribute's domain. The final output is the rendered image $I$.

## 2.2 Jacques Bertin's Visual Variables (The Seminal Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bertin's Seven Retinal Variables (1967)**
> French cartographer **Jacques Bertin** identified the seven atomic visual variables that form the alphabet of every visualization. This is **mandatory reading** for the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

| # | Visual Variable | Perception Type | Best Suited Data Level | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Position (X, Y)** | Quantitative (most accurate) | All types | Scatter plots, Maps |
| 2 | **Size** | Quantitative | Ordinal, Interval, Ratio | Bubble charts, Treemaps |
| 3 | **Shape** | Qualitative (categorical) | **Nominal only** | Markers in scatter (○, △, □) |
| 4 | **Value (Lightness)** | Ordered | Ordinal, Interval, Ratio | Heatmaps (B/W gradient) |
| 5 | **Color Hue** | Qualitative | **Nominal only** | Grouping categories |
| 6 | **Color Saturation** | Ordered | Ordinal, Interval, Ratio | Choropleth maps |
| 7 | **Orientation** | Qualitative | Nominal only | Line chart patterns |

### 2.2.1 Bertin's "Levels of Organization" of Data

Bertin further classified data into four organizational levels, dictating *which* visual variable is appropriate:

$$
\text{Qualitative} \rightarrow \text{Ordered} \rightarrow \text{Quantitative} \rightarrow \text{... (all levels)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Nominal/Qualitative data** → Use *Selectivity* variables: **Hue, Shape, Orientation**.
> - **Ordered/Quantitative data** → Use *Ordered* variables: **Position, Size, Value, Saturation**.

## 2.3 Mackinlay's Effectiveness Ranking (APT — Automatic Presentation Tool)

Building on Bertin, **Jock Mackinlay (1986, Stanford PhD Thesis)** ranked visual variables by their perceptual accuracy along four criteria:

1. **Accuracy** — How precisely can the viewer decode the value?
2. **Discriminability** — Can the viewer tell two values apart?
3. **Separability** — Can two channels be perceived independently when combined?
4. **Pop-out (Pre-attentiveness)** — Can the viewer spot the encoded item in < 250 ms?

### The Mackinlay Effectiveness Ranking (Descending Order)

| Rank | Visual Channel | Quantitative Accuracy | Ordered | Nominal |
|------|----------------|------------------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Position (common scale) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Position (non-aligned) | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| 3 | Length | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ |
| 4 | Angle / Slope | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| 5 | Area | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| 6 | Volume / Curvature | ★★ | ★ | ★ |
| 7 | Color (Saturation/Value) | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| 8 | Color (Hue) | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| 9 | Shape | ✗ | ✗ | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stevens' Psychophysical Law Connection**
> The accuracy of a channel is mathematically modeled by **Stevens' Power Law**:
> $$\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{\alpha}$$
> where $\psi$ is the perceived intensity, $I$ is the physical intensity, and $\alpha$ is the channel-specific exponent. For **length**, $\alpha \approx 1.0$ (perfectly accurate). For **area**, $\alpha \approx 0.7$ (underestimated). For **brightness**, $\alpha \approx 0.5$ (severely underestimated). This is *why* length charts (bar charts) are trusted more than area charts (bubble charts) for quantitative data.

## 2.4 Cleveland & McGill's Perceptual Hierarchy (1984)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cleveland-McGill's Ranked Channels for Quantitative Comparison** (often quoted in KTU exams):
>
> 1. Position along a common scale
> 2. Position along non-aligned scales
> 3. Length, direction, angle
> 4. Area
> 5. Volume, curvature
> 6. Shading, color saturation

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visual Mapping Function | $V = \Phi(D)$, where $D$ is data and $V$ is visual image | Core mapping equation |
| 2 | Stevens' Power Law | $\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{\alpha}$ | Perceptual accuracy |
| 3 | Weber's Law (JND) | $\Delta I / I = k$ (constant) | Minimum discriminable change |
| 4 | Pre-attentive Processing Time | $T_p < 250 \text{ ms}$ | Defines "pop-out" features |
| 5 | Preattentive Features (count) | $\approx 8 \text{ to } 12$ unique channels | Color, orientation, size, etc. |
| 6 | Color Wavelength (Visible) | $\lambda \in [380, 750] \text{ nm}$ | Defines visible spectrum |
| 7 | Weber Fractions (reference) | Length: $0.05$, Area: $0.10$, Hue: $0.05$ | Lower = more accurate channel |
| 8 | Bertin's Levels | $\{Q, O\}$ mapped to $\{A, S, P, V\}$ | Matching rule |
| 9 | Data Type Constraint | Nominal $\nrightarrow$ Length | Forbidden mapping |
| 10 | Mackinlay Rank Inequality | $\text{Accuracy}(\text{Position}) > \text{Accuracy}(\text{Hue})$ | Golden rule |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Visual Mapping Application | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Engineering** | Stress contours on beam cross-sections | Hue maps stress intensity (MPa) |
| **VLSI / Chip Design** | Heatmap of power density (W/mm²) | Saturation encodes magnitude |
| **Medical Imaging** | MRI intensity → grayscale value | Critical for diagnosis |
| **Civil — GIS** | Population density on map polygons | Choropleth (saturation mapping) |
| **IoT Dashboards** | Sensor readings → gauge needle position | Position = most accurate |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Data to Visual Image — The Formal Pipeline

The visual mapping process can be expressed as a composition of three mathematical functions. Let us derive each step.

### Step 1 — Define the Data Domain
Let the dataset be a tuple:
$$D = \{(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n) \mid a_i \in \mathcal{A}_i\}$$

where $\mathcal{A}_i$ is the domain of the $i$-th attribute (e.g., $\mathcal{A}_i = \mathbb{R}$ for continuous, $\mathcal{A}_i = \{ \text{red, green, blue} \}$ for nominal).

### Step 2 — Apply the Semantic Classifier
Each attribute is assigned a measurement level $L_i \in \{\text{Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio}\}$. The classifier is:
$$\text{Classify}(a_i) = L_i$$

**Logic:** If the data allows only equality testing, $L_i = \text{Nominal}$. If it allows difference ratios, $L_i = \text{Ratio}$.

### Step 3 — Select the Channel Set
Define the available visual channels:
$$\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\} = \{\text{Pos}_x, \text{Pos}_y, \text{Size}, \text{Hue}, \text{Saturation}, \text{Shape}, \text{Orientation}, \text{Length}, \text{Area}\}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Mapping Function
The mapping function $\Phi$ selects a channel $c_j$ for each attribute $a_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi : \mathcal{A} &\longrightarrow \mathcal{C} \\
a_i &\longmapsto c_j = \Phi(a_i, L_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constraint:** $\Phi$ must satisfy the **type-safety condition**:
$$\text{If } L_i \in \{\text{Nominal}\} \Rightarrow c_j \in \{\text{Hue, Shape, Orientation}\}$$
$$\text{If } L_i \in \{\text{Ordinal, Interval, Ratio}\} \Rightarrow c_j \in \{\text{Position, Length, Size, Saturation, Value}\}$$

### Step 5 — Normalize and Encode
For ordered/quantitative attributes, we apply a **normalization** before encoding, since the channel's range is bounded (e.g., pixel coordinates):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_i^{\text{norm}} &= \frac{a_i - a_i^{\min}}{a_i^{\max} - a_i^{\min}} \quad \in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

This normalized value is then mapped to the channel's range. For example, mapping to a vertical bar chart of height $H$ pixels:
$$h_i = a_i^{\text{norm}} \times H$$

### Step 6 — Render the Image
The final image is the set of all rendered marks:
$$I = \big\{ M(\Phi(a_i, L_i), a_i^{\text{norm}}) \;\big|\; a_i \in D \big\}$$

where $M$ is the rendering mark function (e.g., a circle, rectangle, or polygon).

### Step 7 — Verify Perceptual Accuracy
Using **Stevens' Power Law**, the perceived magnitude $\psi$ of a channel encoding $I$ is:
$$\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{\alpha}$$

The error $\epsilon$ introduced by the mapping is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon &= \psi(I) - I \\
&= k \cdot I^{\alpha} - I \\
&= I (k \cdot I^{\alpha - 1} - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **length** ($\alpha = 1, k = 1$), $\epsilon = 0$ — perfectly accurate. For **area** ($\alpha \approx 0.7$), $\epsilon < 0$ — viewers systematically *underestimate* the value. This is the mathematical proof that **bar charts are more truthful than bubble charts**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Mapping

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam artifact**. Memorize it.

| If Data Type is → And Goal is → | **Nominal** (Categorical) | **Ordinal** (Ranked) | **Quantitative** (Interval / Ratio) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identify Group** | Hue, Shape, Orientation | Hue, Shape | Hue, Shape (discretized) |
| **Compare Magnitude** | ❌ Not applicable | Length, Saturation, Value | Position, Length, Area |
| **Show Trend over Time** | ❌ Not applicable | Connected position (line) | Connected position (line) |
| **Show Distribution** | ❌ Not applicable | Histogram (binned) | Histogram, Box plot |
| **Show Correlation** | ❌ Not applicable | Scatter (size = freq.) | Scatter (X, Y position) |
| **Show Composition** | Treemap, Pie (size) | Stacked bar (ordered) | Stacked bar, Waterfall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbology Rule (Strictly Enforced in KTU Valuation):**
> Never map a Nominal variable to a quantitative channel (e.g., "Material = Steel, Concrete" → Bar height). The bar heights would imply a meaningful *order* that does not exist. This is the most common 2-mark deduction in KTU answers.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Mapping a Civil Engineering Dataset

**Dataset:** Stress analysis of 5 beam specimens at varying loads.

| Specimen ID | Material (Nominal) | Load (kN) — Ratio | Deflection (mm) — Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Steel | 50 | 2.1 |
| S2 | Concrete | 50 | 3.8 |
| S3 | Wood | 50 | 6.5 |
| S4 | Steel | 75 | 3.2 |
| S5 | Concrete | 75 | 5.7 |

**Apply the mapping function $\Phi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material (Nominal)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi} \text{Color Hue} \;(\text{Red, Blue, Brown}) \\
\text{Load (Ratio)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi} \text{X-axis Position} \\
\text{Deflection (Ratio)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi} \text{Y-axis Position} \\
\text{Specimen ID (Nominal)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi} \text{Marker Shape} \;(\bigcirc, \triangle, \square)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification (this is what KTU examiners award marks for):**

1. Material is nominal → **Hue is correct** (qualitative channel).
2. Load and Deflection are ratio → **Position is the most accurate channel** (Mackinlay Rank #1).
3. Specimen ID is nominal → **Shape is correct** (qualitative channel).
4. **No channel is overloaded** — each data attribute has exactly one channel (Mackinlay's *Separability* criterion is satisfied).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Mapping "Specimen ID" to the X-axis. Since ID is nominal, it has no inherent order. Placing IDs on the X-axis implies a sequence that doesn't exist scientifically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C --> D{Data Type L_i}

    D -- Ordinal --> F[Ordered Channels: Position Size Saturation]
    D -- Interval / Ratio --> G[Quantitative Channels: Position Length]
    E --> H[Channel Normalization]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Render Mark M]
    I --> J[Visual Image I]
    J --> K[Human Perceptual System]
    K --> L[Cognitive Insight]
```


## 4.3 Channel Effectiveness Comparison (Perceptual Accuracy Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["POSITION (Most Accurate)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Channels at the **top** of the pyramid are perceptually most accurate. KTU examiners expect students to use higher-tier channels whenever the data type permits.

## 4.4 The Mackinlay Separability Matrix (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INDEP[Fully Independent Channels]
        I1[Position X]
        I2[Position Y]
    end

    subgraph INTEG[Integrated Channels]
        M1[Color = Hue + Saturation + Brightness]
        M2[Size = Width + Height combined]
    end

    subgraph CONFL[Conflicting Channels]
        C1[Hue vs Hue at same pixel]
        C2[Redundant encodings]
    end

    INDEP -->|Combine safely| SAFE[Visualization with NO Perceptual Conflict]
    INTEG -->|Decoding ambiguous| WARN[Use with caution]
    CONFL -->|Perceptual clash| FAIL[Visualization Failure]

    classDef ok fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    class SAFE ok
    class WARN warn
    class FAIL fail
```

**Seven Visual Variables (1.5 marks):**

2. Size
3. Shape
4. Value (Lightness)
5. Color (Hue)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail Jacques Bertin's framework of visual variables and his theory of "levels of organization" of data. Discuss how this framework guides the choice of visual variables for different data types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe Cleveland and McGill's perceptual hierarchy of visual variables. Justify, with the help of Stevens' Power Law, why position and length are the most accurate channels for encoding quantitative data. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Bertin's Framework (7 marks)

**1. Introduction to Bertin's Theory (2 marks):**
Jacques Bertin, in his seminal 1983 work *Semiology of Graphics*, proposed that every visualization is built from a finite alphabet of **seven retinal variables**. He further argued that the *correctness* of a visualization depends on matching these variables to the **"level of organization"** of the data.

**2. The Seven Variables (2 marks):**

| Variable | Selective | Associative | Quantitative | Permutation | Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Position (X,Y) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Size | – | ✔ | ✔ | – | ✔ |
| Value | – | ✔ | ✔ | – | ✔ |
| Texture | – | ✔ | – | ✔ | – |
| Color (Hue) | – | ✔ | – | ✔ | – |
| Color (Saturation) | – | ✔ | ✔ | – | ✔ |
| Orientation | – | ✔ | – | ✔ | – |
| Shape | – | ✔ | – | – | – |

**3. Levels of Organization (2 marks):**
Bertin classified data into three organizational levels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \;(\text{Qualitative}) &: \text{Nominal categories} \\
O \;(\text{Ordered}) &: \text{Ordinal sequence} \\
N \;(\text{Numeric/Quantitative}) &: \text{Continuous numbers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Mapping Rule (1 mark):**
- $Q \rightarrow \{ \text{Hue, Orientation, Shape, Texture} \}$
- $O \rightarrow \{ \text{Position, Size, Value, Saturation} \}$
- $N \rightarrow \{ \text{Position (most accurate)} \}$

#### Part (b) — Cleveland-McGill & Stevens' Law (7 marks)

**1. Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy (3 marks):**

William S. Cleveland and Robert McGill (1984, Harvard) conducted controlled experiments to rank 10 elementary perceptual tasks by accuracy:

1. Position along a common scale
2. Position along non-aligned scales
3. Length, direction, angle
4. Area
5. Volume, curvature
6. Shading, color saturation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Finding:** Viewers judge **position** most accurately, then **length**, then **area and angle**, and least accurately **color saturation** and **volume**.

**2. Stevens' Power Law — Justification (3 marks):**

Stevens proposed:
$$\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{\alpha}$$

For length, $\alpha \approx 1.0$, so:
$$\psi(I) = I \quad \text{(no perceptual distortion)}$$

For area, $\alpha \approx 0.7$, so:
$$\psi(I) = I^{0.7} < I \quad \text{(systematic underestimation)}$$

For color brightness, $\alpha \approx 0.5$:
$$\psi(I) = I^{0.5} \ll I \quad \text{(severe underestimation)}$$

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):**
Since length and position yield $\alpha \approx 1$, they are perceptually linear. This mathematically justifies using **bar charts (length)** and **scatter plots (position)** as the gold standard for quantitative data.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 / June 2024]**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the visual mapping pipeline from raw data to the final visual image. Discuss the role of channel normalization in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A civil engineering dataset contains the following attributes: *Beam Material* (Steel/Concrete), *Beam Length* (m), *Maximum Load* (kN), and *Failure Mode* (Cracking/Shear/Buckling). Design an appropriate visual mapping for this dataset using Mackinlay's effectiveness criteria. Justify each channel selection. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Visual Mapping Pipeline (7 marks)

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The pipeline consists of four sequential stages (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Raw Data $D$** → 2. **Attribute Extraction** → 3. **Semantic Classification** → 4. **Channel Selection** → 5. **Normalization** → 6. **Rendering** → 7. **Perception**

**2. Detailed Explanation of Each Stage (2 marks):**
- *Attribute Extraction:* Decompose $D$ into atomic variables $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$.
- *Semantic Classification:* Assign each $a_i$ a Stevens level $L_i \in \{N, O, I, R\}$.
- *Channel Selection:* Apply $\Phi(a_i, L_i) \rightarrow c_j$.
- *Rendering:* Use mark function $M$ to draw the visual element.

**3. Role of Normalization (2 marks):**

Raw values are typically unbounded (e.g., Load = 5,000 kN), but pixel coordinates are bounded (e.g., [0, 800] pixels). Normalization rescales the value to [0, 1] using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{a_i - a_i^{\min}}{a_i^{\max} - a_i^{\min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ensures that the largest data point fills the available display area, and the smallest maps to zero. **Without normalization, the chart is either too small to read or clipped off-screen.**

#### Part (b) — Civil Engineering Mapping Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Classify the data (1 mark):**

| Attribute | Stevens Level | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Beam Material (Steel/Concrete) | **Nominal** | Categories with no inherent order |
| Beam Length (m) | **Ratio** | True zero exists, ratios meaningful |
| Maximum Load (kN) | **Ratio** | True zero, ratios meaningful |
| Failure Mode (Cracking/Shear/Buckling) | **Nominal** | Categories, no order |

**Step 2 — Apply Mackinlay's Effectiveness Ranking (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material (Nominal)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi_1} \text{Color Hue (e.g., Blue=Steel, Orange=Concrete)} \\
\text{Length (Ratio)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi_2} \text{X-axis Position (most accurate)} \\
\text{Max. Load (Ratio)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi_3} \text{Y-axis Position (most accurate)} \\
\text{Failure Mode (Nominal)} &\xrightarrow{\Phi_4} \text{Marker Shape (e.g., ×, △, +)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Justify Each Channel (3 marks):**

| Channel | Mackinlay Rank | Stevens $\alpha$ | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position (X,Y) | 1 (Most accurate) | 1.0 | Quantitative data demands the most accurate channel |
| Hue | 8 (For quantitative), 4 (For nominal) | 0.5 | **Hue is for nominal** — Material is nominal ✓ |
| Shape | 9 (For quantitative), 4 (For nominal) | N/A | **Shape is for nominal** — Failure Mode is nominal ✓ |

**Justification for NOT using other channels:**
- We do **not** map Material to bar height (length) because Material is **nominal** — length would falsely imply an order (e.g., Steel > Concrete).
- We do **not** map Failure Mode to a continuous color gradient because the modes are **discrete categories** with no ranking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Deduction 1 (1 mark):** Mapping a Nominal variable (e.g., Material) to a quantitative channel (Length, Area) — *forbidden mapping*.
> - **Deduction 2 (1 mark):** Overloading a single channel with two attributes (e.g., using both *Length* and *Hue* to encode the same data).
> - **Deduction 3 (1 mark):** Failing to *justify* the channel choice using either Mackinlay's ranking or Stevens' Power Law.
> - **Deduction 4 (1 mark):** No normalization step mentioned for ratio data.
> - **Deduction 5 (1 mark):** Choosing a 3D pie chart (volume, $\alpha \approx 0.7$) for quantitative comparison — examiners deduct heavily for this.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 1: Visual Mapping**

- [x] **Visual Mapping = Data → Visual Encoding → Image**. Memorize this equation.
- [x] **Bertin's 7 variables:** Position, Size, Shape, Value, Hue, Saturation, Orientation.
- [x] **Stevens' 4 levels:** Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio. **Nominal is the most restricted.**
- [x] **Golden Rule:** Nominal → Hue/Shape/Orientation. Quantitative → Position/Length.
- [x] **Mackinlay Rank #1:** Position is the most accurate channel for *all* data types.
- [x] **Stevens' Power Law:** $\psi(I) = k I^{\alpha}$. Length: $\alpha=1$. Area: $\alpha \approx 0.7$. Brightness: $\alpha \approx 0.5$.
- [x] **Cleveland-McGill:** Position > Length > Area > Volume > Color (for quantitative accuracy).
- [x] **Pre-attentive processing:** Visual features detected in < 250 ms are "pop-out" features.
- [x] **Weber's Law:** JND $\propto$ magnitude. The minimum noticeable change in a stimulus is a constant *proportion* of the original.
- [x] **Forbidden mapping #1:** Never encode a Nominal variable using Length or Area.
- [x] **Forbidden mapping #2:** Never use 3D pie charts for quantitative comparison.
- [x] **Channel Separability:** When combining channels, ensure they are perceptually independent (e.g., Position-X + Position-Y = good; Hue + Saturation = ambiguous).
- [x] **Normalization formula:** $a^{\text{norm}} = (a - a_{\min}) / (a_{\max} - a_{\min})$.
- [x] **Common KTU trick question:** *"Which channel is best for showing population across countries?"* — Answer: **Hue/Saturation (Choropleth)** because population is **Ratio** but the *spatial boundaries* are nominal regions; if comparing *magnitudes*, use **Length (bar chart)** instead.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** *Justify every channel choice with a reference to Bertin, Mackinlay, Cleveland-McGill, or Stevens.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Visual Analytics # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Analytics (VA)** is the science of analytical reasoning supported by highly interactive visual interfaces that combine **automated analysis techniques** (such as statistics, data mining, and machine learning) with **human cognitive abilities** (such as perception, reasoning, and domain knowledge) to derive insight from massive, dynamic, ambiguous, and often conflicting data. According to the canonical formulation by **Thomas & Cook (2005)** in the *Illuminating the Path* research roadmap: > "Visual Analytics is more than just visualization; it is a tightly coupled loop of **data analysis, visualization, and human interaction**, where the human is in the loop, guiding the analytical process through visual means." In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Visual Analytics is positioned as the **multidisciplinary convergence** of: | Pillar | Core Discipline | |---|---| | Analytical Reasoning | Statistics, Data Mining, AI/ML | | Visual Representation | Information Visualization, Scientific Visualization | | Human Perception | Cognitive Science, HCI, Perception Psychology | | Interactive Interfaces | Computer Graphics, HCI, UI/UX Engineering | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Detective and the Magnifying Glass" Analogy** Imagine you are a **detective** investigating a complex crime scene with millions of pieces of evidence — fingerprints, CCTV footage, phone records, witness statements, and forensic samples. - **Data Mining** is like a **forensic lab robot** that automatically sorts DNA, runs chemical tests, and flags suspicious matches. - **Visualization** is the **evidence board** — photos pinned to the wall with red string connecting them. - **Visual Analytics** is **YOU, the detective, working with the lab robot and the evidence board simultaneously.** You ask the robot to run tests, you see results on the board, you form a hypothesis, you drag a new string between two clues, the robot runs another test, and the cycle continues until the case is solved. Without VA, you either get a **wall full of evidence with no interpretation** (pure visualization) or a **robot's report with no visual context** (pure automated analysis). VA combines them in a **closed feedback loop** where the human is the decision-maker. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Highlight** The following canonical terms must be memorized verbatim for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination: - **Information Visualization (InfoVis)** → Visualizing *abstract, non-physical* data (e.g., financial charts, social networks, text). - **Scientific Visualization (SciVis)** → Visualizing *physical, spatially-bound* data with inherent geometry (e.g., MRI scans, CFD airflow, weather patterns). - **Visual Analytics (VA)** → The *integrative* discipline combining both with automated analysis and interaction. **Shneiderman's Mantra** (the operational backbone of any VA system): > **Overview first → Zoom and filter → Details on demand** This is a **mnemonic device** for designing interactive VA interfaces. It is exam-grade material and must be quoted exactly. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Visual Analytics Process — Decomposed Logic Visual Analytics is governed by a structured operational loop. Below is the step-by-step logical flow used in every textbook treatment of VA. ### Step 1 — Data Acquisition (The Foundation) - The process begins with **raw heterogeneous data** arriving from sensors, databases, logs, web streams, or simulations. - Data may be **structured** (tables), **semi-structured** (JSON, XML), or **unstructured** (text, images, video). - **Why it matters:** Without quality data, even the best visualization is meaningless ("Garbage In, Garbage Out"). ### Step 2 — Pre-processing and Transformation - Cleaning noise, handling missing values, normalization, and feature engineering. - **How it works:** Transforms raw data into an analysis-ready form using statistical and computational pipelines. ### Step 3 — Automated Analytical Processing - Applies machine learning, statistical modelling, clustering, regression, classification, or anomaly detection algorithms. - **Why:** Computers excel at crunching millions of rows; humans do not. ### Step 4 — Visual Representation - Maps processed data to **visual encodings** (position, length, color, shape, size) using charts, graphs, maps, and 3D scenes. - The choice of encoding is critical: **pre-attentive features** (color, orientation) are processed by the human brain in **under 250 milliseconds**. ### Step 5 — Human Reasoning and Interaction - The user **perceives** the visualization, **interprets** patterns, and **generates hypotheses**. - The user interacts via **brushing, linking, filtering, panning, zooming**, and **querying** the system. ### Step 6 — Decision / Insight Generation - The cycle terminates (or repeats) when the user derives actionable insight or confirms/refutes a hypothesis. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pearl:** The defining property of VA is the **tightly coupled, closed feedback loop** between human reasoning and automated analysis. Pure visualization lacks the automated step; pure data mining lacks the visual step. VA unifies both. ## 2.2 The KTU Data Type Taxonomy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Definition Box — Must memorize for short-answer questions** Visual Analytics operates on four primary data types: | Data Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **1-D Data** | Linear, sequential values | Time-series stock prices, temperature logs | | **2-D Data** | Two-dimensional mapped data | Geographic maps, floor plans, X-Y plots | | **3-D Data** | Three-dimensional volumetric/spatial | MRI/CT scans, CAD models, molecular structures | | **Multi-dimensional / N-D Data** | Many attributes per record | Database tables, relational datasets | | **Temporal Data** | Data varying over time | Video frames, event logs, animations | | **Tree / Hierarchical Data** | Parent-child relationships | File systems, organization charts, XML/JSON | | **Network / Graph Data** | Nodes and edges | Social networks, transportation networks | | **Text / Unstructured** | Free-form natural language | Documents, tweets, emails | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Visual Analytics is more conceptual than mathematical, but the following quantitative principles appear in KTU numerical/scoring sections.** | Principle / Formula | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | $N = H \times W \times D$ | Pixel count for 2D images | Image resolution calculation | | $V_{bit} = 2^{b}$ | Distinct values per channel | Color depth | | $R_{interaction} = \frac{T_{tasks}}{T_{total}}$ | Interaction efficiency ratio | UI responsiveness metric | | $D_{ratio} = \frac{\text{visible data}}{\text{total data}}$ | Data density on screen | Visual clutter measurement | | $F_{cog} = \frac{I_{raw}}{C_{human}}$ | Cognitive load factor | Information overload assessment | | $\text{Color} = (R, G, B, \alpha)$ | RGBA color encoding | Color mapping in visualizations | | Shannon Entropy: $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Information content (bits) | Visual encoding informativeness | | MacAdam Ellipses | Perceptual color difference threshold | Designing colorblind-safe palettes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pre-attentive Processing Time** is empirically established as **$T_{preatt} < 250 \text{ ms}$** (Healey & Enns, 2012). Colors, orientation, and motion are detected in this window without conscious effort. This is a **board-exam favourite**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Visual Analytics is the operational backbone of: - **Cybersecurity**: Detecting network intrusions via real-time log visualization. - **Healthcare**: Visualizing patient cohorts and disease spread. - **Finance**: Detecting fraud through interactive transaction graphs. - **Smart Cities**: Traffic flow, pollution, and resource dashboards. - **Bioinformatics**: Gene expression, protein interaction networks. - **Engineering Simulation**: CFD post-processing, finite element analysis. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Visual Analytics Feedback Loop (Conceptual) Unlike mechanical or thermal subjects, Visual Analytics is derived as a **formal feedback model**. We construct it stepwise. ### Step 1 — Define the Two Endpoints Let the two fundamental endpoints of the VA system be: - $H$ = the **Human analyst** (cognitive agent). - $M$ = the **Machine/automated analyzer** (computational agent). ### Step 2 — Define the Information Channels Two channels of information transfer exist: 1. **Visual Channel** ($V$): From $M$ to $H$ — the machine renders visual output for the human. 2. **Interaction Channel** ($I$): From $H$ to $M$ — the human issues commands, queries, and parameter settings. ### Step 3 — Define the State Equation The system state at iteration $k$ is denoted $S_k$, containing the current data, parameters, and analytical model. The transition is: $$ S_{k+1} = f(S_k, I_k, M_k) $$ Where: - $f$ is the **VA transition function**. - $I_k$ = interaction input at step $k$. - $M_k$ = machine analytical output at step $k$. ### Step 4 — Define the Convergence Condition Insight is reached when: $$ \vert S_{k+1} - S_k \vert < \epsilon $$ For a small threshold $\epsilon > 0$, meaning the system state stabilizes. Equivalently, the human analyst signals convergence when a hypothesis is confirmed or refuted. ### Step 5 — Compose the Closed Loop $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Loop} &= H \xrightarrow{I} M \xrightarrow{V} H \xrightarrow{I} M \xrightarrow{V} H \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{Insight} \\ \text{Formally:} \quad S_{k+1} &= f(S_k, I_k, V_k) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the formal model. In KTU exams, students are expected to draw and explain the closed loop with both directions clearly labelled.** ## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example: VA on a Cybersecurity Dataset > [!NOTE] > **KTU 14-Mark Style Walkthrough** **Problem Context:** A network security analyst must detect anomalies in 10 million log entries from a corporate firewall. ### Step 1 — Data Acquisition The system ingests logs: timestamp, source IP, destination IP, port, packet size, protocol, action (allow/deny). ### Step 2 — Pre-processing Duplicate entries removed, missing IPs imputed, timestamp converted to ISO 8601. ### Step 3 — Automated Analysis (Machine Side) A clustering algorithm (DBSCAN) is applied. Two clusters emerge: - Cluster A: 9,999,950 normal entries. - Cluster B: 50 outliers — anomalous spikes from a single source IP to port 22 (SSH). ### Step 4 — Visual Mapping The outliers are rendered as a **2D scatterplot** of (source IP frequency vs. port number) with anomalous points highlighted in red. A **timeline view** below shows the burst occurred at 02:00–02:15. ### Step 5 — Human Reasoning The analyst perceives the red cluster, drags a **brushing selection** across the timeline, and sees all 50 events are linked to a single IP. Hypothesis: **SSH brute-force attack**. ### Step 6 — Decision The analyst marks the IP for blocking. The cycle terminates. Insight generated in **minutes** versus hours/days in pure data mining. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Information Visualization** with **Visual Analytics**. In pure InfoVis, there is **no automated analysis step** — the human does all pattern recognition unaided. In VA, the **machine pre-analyzes** and the human **verifies and refines**. Mark this distinction clearly in answers. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is high-yield for 14-mark questions — it is often asked as a comparison question.** | Dimension | Information Visualization | Scientific Visualization | Visual Analytics | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Type** | Abstract, non-spatial | Physical, spatial geometry | Both abstract and physical | | **Primary User Task** | Exploration, pattern recognition | Simulation analysis, modeling | Reasoning, decision-making, hypothesis testing | | **Automation Role** | Minimal | Minimal | **Central** (ML, statistics) | | **Human Role** | Visual interpreter | Domain expert | **Active analytical driver** | | **Interaction** | Common | Common | **Mandatory and tight-loop** | | **Example** | Pie chart of sales | MRI slice rendering | Interactive fraud-detection dashboard | | **Goal** | Communicate patterns | Show physical phenomena | **Generate actionable insight** | | **Origin Field** | HCI, statistics | Computational science | **Integration of InfoVis + SciVis + AI** | ## 3.4 Derivation of the Shneiderman Mantra from Information Theory Shneiderman's mantra — **"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand"** — can be derived as an information-theoretic principle. Let total information content be $H(X)$ in bits. The user has a finite **perceptual bandwidth** $B_{preatt}$ bits/second. ### Step 1 — Overview Stage Render a **low-resolution global summary** of all data, minimizing spatial detail but maximizing coverage: $$ \text{Encode} : X \rightarrow \text{GlobalSummary} $$ ### Step 2 — Zoom and Filter User selects a subset $X' \subset X$ of interest. The conditional entropy is: $$ H(X' \vert \text{Filter}) = H(X) - I(X; \text{Filter}) $$ Where $I(X; \text{Filter})$ is the mutual information between data and filter selection. ### Step 3 — Details on Demand Drill into individual records with full-resolution detail.
