Each detail item carries: $$ H_{\text{detail}} = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i $$ This three-stage flow ensures the human's cognitive load is **distributed across perceptual stages**, never overwhelming a single channel. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pearl:** This is rarely asked in full derivation, but the **three stages** must be defined and exemplified in any 14-mark answer on VA design. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Visual Analytics Closed-Loop Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagrams the canonical VA feedback loop. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels are plain text without markdown formatting. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Data Sources
Sensors Databases Streams"] --> nodeB["Pre Processing
Cleaning Transformation"] nodeB --> nodeC["Automated Analysis
ML Statistics Mining"] nodeC --> nodeD["Visual Mapping
Encoding Rendering"] nodeD --> nodeE["Visual Display
Screens Dashboards 3D"] nodeE --> nodeF["Human Perception
Cognitive Reasoning"] nodeF --> nodeG["Hypothesis and
Decision Formation"] nodeG --> nodeH["Interaction Layer
Brushing Filtering Zoom"] nodeH --> nodeC nodeH --> nodeB nodeG --> nodeI["Insight and
Action"] nodeI --> nodeA ``` ## 4.2 VA vs. Pure Visualization vs. Pure Data Mining — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUB1["Pure Information Visualization"] nodeP1["Data"] --> nodeP2["Visual Mapping"] nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Human Eyes"] nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Interpretation"] end subgraph SUB2["Pure Data Mining"] nodeQ1["Data"] --> nodeQ2["Algorithm"] nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3["Statistical Output"] nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4["Report"] end subgraph SUB3["Visual Analytics Integrated"] nodeR1["Data"] --> nodeR2["Pre Processing"] nodeR2 --> nodeR3["Automated Analysis"] nodeR3 --> nodeR4["Visual Mapping"] nodeR4 --> nodeR5["Human Analyst"] nodeR5 --> nodeR6["Interaction"] nodeR6 --> nodeR3 nodeR5 --> nodeR7["Insight and Action"] end ``` ## 4.3 Shneiderman Mantra — Three-Stage User Interaction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS1["Stage 1 Overview
Show Entire Dataset
Low Resolution Global View"] --> nodeS2["Stage 2 Zoom and Filter
Select Region of Interest
Reduce Visual Clutter"] nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Stage 3 Details on Demand
Click to Reveal Full Info
Drill Down to Records"] nodeS3 --> nodeS4["New Hypothesis Formed"] nodeS4 --> nodeS2 ``` ## 4.4 VA System Stack — Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB layerA["Layer 5 Application and Domain
Fraud Detection Public Health Smart City"] layerB["Layer 4 Visual Interface
Dashboards Charts Maps 3D Scenes"] layerC["Layer 3 Analytical Engine
Clustering Classification Anomaly Detection"] layerD["Layer 2 Data Management
Databases Data Lakes Streaming Pipelines"] layerE["Layer 1 Infrastructure
Servers GPUs Cloud Networks"] layerA --> layerB layerB --> layerC layerC --> layerD layerD --> layerE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Students:** In the examination, you will often be asked to "draw and label the VA process." The simplest and most marks-earning diagram is the **closed loop** (4.1). Always label the **two directions**: machine-to-human (visual) and human-to-machine (interaction). Add brief annotations next to each arrow. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1 **Define Visual Analytics. How does it differ from Information Visualization?** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** Visual Analytics is the science of analytical reasoning supported by interactive visual interfaces, integrating **automated analysis techniques** (statistics, data mining, machine learning) with **human cognitive abilities** to derive insight from complex data. **Difference from Information Visualization:** - **Information Visualization** focuses on representing **abstract data** through visual encodings (charts, graphs) and relies primarily on human perception to detect patterns. It has **minimal or no automated analysis**. - **Visual Analytics** explicitly **integrates automated computational analysis** with the visual interface in a closed feedback loop. The human is an **active analytical driver**, not merely a passive viewer. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation]** --- ### Question 2 **State and explain Shneiderman's Mantra for visual information seeking.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** Shneiderman's Mantra defines the three-stage operational flow for designing interactive visual interfaces: 1. **Overview First** — Display a global, low-resolution summary of the entire dataset to establish context. 2. **Zoom and Filter** — Allow the user to interactively focus on regions of interest and reduce visual clutter. 3. **Details on Demand** — Provide drill-down access to individual records or high-resolution details on user request. This mantra ensures that **cognitive load is distributed across perceptual stages** and supports efficient information seeking in large datasets. **[3 marks: 1 for each stage with brief explanation]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Style]** --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **(a) Explain the Visual Analytics process in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of automated analysis and human reasoning in the loop.** `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The Visual Analytics process is a **closed, iterative feedback loop** comprising six sequential stages that integrate machine computation with human cognition. **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:** Raw data is collected from heterogeneous sources — databases, sensors, web streams, simulations. Data may be structured, semi-structured, or unstructured. **Stage 2 — Pre-processing and Transformation:** The data is cleaned (noise removal, missing-value handling), normalized, and transformed into an analysis-ready form. Feature engineering and dimensionality reduction may be applied. **Stage 3 — Automated Analytical Processing:** Statistical, data mining, and machine learning algorithms process the data to extract patterns, clusters, anomalies, and predictive models. Examples include DBSCAN clustering, linear regression, and neural network classification. **Stage 4 — Visual Representation:** The processed data is mapped to **visual encodings** — position, length, color, shape, size — using charts, graphs, maps, or 3D scenes. The choice of encoding leverages **pre-attentive processing** (sub-250ms detection) to maximize human comprehension. **Stage 5 — Human Reasoning and Interaction:** The analyst perceives the visualization, forms hypotheses, and interacts with the system via brushing, linking, filtering, panning, zooming, and querying. **Stage 6 — Insight and Decision:** A hypothesis is confirmed or refuted, generating actionable insight. The cycle may restart with refined parameters. **The Role of Automated Analysis:** - Excels at high-volume, high-dimensional computation. - Pre-filters data, surfaces patterns, and reduces cognitive load. - Cannot interpret **context, semantics, or domain knowledge**. **The Role of Human Reasoning:** - Provides **domain expertise, intuition, and creativity**. - Recognizes patterns the machine may miss. - **Drives the analytical process** through interaction. The two are **complementary**; VA leverages the strengths of both in a tight feedback loop. **Diagram to be drawn (mandatory):** Closed loop with machine side (Stages 1–4) and human side (Stages 5–6) connected by bidirectional arrows (visual output → human, interaction → machine). --- **(b) Compare and contrast Information Visualization, Scientific Visualization, and Visual Analytics across data types, automation role, and primary user task.** `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** | Dimension | InfoVis | SciVis | VA | |---|---|---|---| | **Data** | Abstract (non-spatial) | Physical (spatial geometry) | Both abstract and physical | | **Primary Task** | Communicate, explore | Simulate, model physical phenomena | Reason, decide, generate insight | | **Automation** | Minimal | Minimal | **Central and integral** | | **Human Role** | Visual interpreter | Domain expert | **Active analytical driver** | | **Example** | Pie chart of sales | CFD airflow simulation | Real-time fraud-detection dashboard | | **Origin** | HCI, statistics | Computational science | **Convergence of all three** | **Key Differentiator:** Only Visual Analytics explicitly fuses **automated analysis** (ML, statistics) with **interactive visualization** in a **closed loop** for hypothesis-driven decision-making. InfoVis and SciVis are largely **read-only**; VA is **write-and-read** with continuous feedback. **[7 marks: 2 for each comparison dimension + 1 for the summary]** --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks **(a) Define Visual Analytics. Discuss the major data types that VA systems handle with one real-world example for each.** `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Visual Analytics is the science of analytical reasoning supported by interactive visual interfaces, combining **automated analysis** (statistics, ML, data mining) with **human cognition** (perception, reasoning, domain knowledge) to generate insight from complex, large-scale, and ambiguous data. **Major Data Types:** 1. **One-Dimensional (1-D) Data** — Linear sequences of values. - *Example:* Time-series stock prices displayed as candlestick charts; VA adds automated trend detection and anomaly flagging. 2. **Two-Dimensional (2-D) Data** — Mapped to a plane. - *Example:* Geographic information systems (GIS) with crime-hotspot overlays; VA adds spatial clustering and predictive risk modeling. 3. **Three-Dimensional (3-D) Data** — Volumetric or geometric. - *Example:* MRI brain scans rendered as 3D volumes; VA adds automated tumor segmentation and interactive exploration. 4. **Multi-Dimensional Data** — Many attributes per record. - *Example:* Customer databases visualized via parallel coordinates or scatterplot matrices; VA adds automated clustering and drill-down analytics. 5. **Temporal Data** — Time-varying. - *Example:* Cybersecurity event logs over 24 hours; VA adds anomaly detection and timeline brushing. 6. **Hierarchical / Tree Data** — Parent-child relationships. - *Example:* Organizational charts or phylogenetic trees; VA adds automated branch similarity scoring. 7. **Network / Graph Data** — Nodes and edges. - *Example:* Social network interaction maps; VA adds community detection and influence scoring. 8. **Text / Unstructured Data** — Natural language. - *Example:* Twitter feeds visualized as word clouds and topic graphs; VA adds sentiment analysis and trend extraction. --- **(b) Discuss the role of interaction in Visual Analytics. Explain the Shneiderman Mantra with an example of a fraud-detection dashboard.** `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **Role of Interaction:** Interaction is the **defining and indispensable element** of Visual Analytics. Without interaction, the system degenerates into static visualization. Interaction enables: - **User-driven exploration** — analysts steer the analytical process. - **Dynamic query and filtering** — the dataset is reshaped in real time. - **Hypothesis testing** — the user can drill down, link views, and verify patterns. - **Bridging human intuition and machine computation** — interaction is the channel through which human reasoning feeds back into automated analysis. **Shneiderman's Mantra Applied to a Fraud-Detection Dashboard:** **Stage 1 — Overview First:** The dashboard opens with a **global map view** showing 1 million transactions per day across all regions, with **red hotspots** indicating regions of unusually high volume. This gives the analyst immediate context. **Stage 2 — Zoom and Filter:** The analyst **zooms into Europe**, applies a **filter for transactions over $10,000**, and **brushing-selects** the 03:00–04:00 window where a spike appears. The map and timeline views are **linked** — selecting a region highlights its corresponding timeline slice. **Stage 3 — Details on Demand:** The analyst clicks on a specific outlier transaction. A **detail panel** reveals: sender account, receiver account, IP address, device fingerprint, and a **machine-generated risk score** (e.g., 0.94). The automated model has already flagged it as suspicious. The analyst now has **actionable insight** — block the receiver account, escalate to investigation. The closed loop is complete: **machine analysis → visual display → human reasoning → interaction → refined machine analysis → ... → insight**. **[7 marks: 3 for role of interaction + 4 for mantra application]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Conflating Visual Analytics with Visualization:** Many students describe VA as just "visualization with charts." This loses 2–3 marks. Always emphasize the **closed loop with automated analysis**. > 2. **Forgetting the Diagram:** In 14-mark questions on the VA process, a **labeled closed-loop diagram is mandatory**. Without it, expect a **2-mark deduction**. > 3. **Missing the "Why":** Stating "ML is used" is incomplete. Always explain **what role it plays** (pattern extraction, anomaly flagging, pre-filtering). > 4. **Skipping Examples:** Generic definitions without concrete engineering or scientific examples lose marks. Always include at least **one real-world scenario**. > 5. **Confusing Pre-attentive Processing:** Some students state pre-attentive time as "less than 1 second." The **canonical value is < 250 ms** — use the precise figure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU exam.** - **Visual Analytics (VA)** = Automated Analysis + Interactive Visualization + Human Reasoning in a **closed feedback loop**. - **Origin:** Thomas & Cook, *Illuminating the Path* (2005). - **Three Pillars of VA:** Analytical Reasoning, Visual Representation, Human Cognition. - **Difference from InfoVis and SciVis:** VA explicitly integrates **machine learning / statistics** with visualization; InfoVis and SciVis do not. - **Shneiderman's Mantra (3 stages):** **Overview first → Zoom and filter → Details on demand**. - **Data Types in VA:** 1-D, 2-D, 3-D, N-D, Temporal, Hierarchical, Network, Text. - **Pre-attentive Processing Time:** **< 250 ms** (color, orientation, motion detected without conscious effort). - **VA Process Stages:** Data Acquisition → Pre-processing → Automated Analysis → Visual Mapping → Human Reasoning → Interaction → Insight. - **Key Algorithms Used in VA Pipeline:** DBSCAN (clustering), k-NN (classification), PCA (dimensionality reduction), Isolation Forest (anomaly detection), LSTM (temporal modeling). - **Engineering Applications:** Cybersecurity, Healthcare, Finance, Smart Cities, Bioinformatics, CFD post-processing. - **System Stack Layers:** Infrastructure → Data Management → Analytical Engine → Visual Interface → Application/Domain. - **Mnemonic for VA Loop:** **"D-P-A-V-H-I"** = Data, Pre-process, Analyze, Visualize, Human-thinks, Interact, then loop. - **Convergence Condition:** $\vert S_{k+1} - S_k \vert < \epsilon$ — system stabilizes when insight is reached. - **MacAdam Ellipses:** Perceptual thresholds for color difference — essential for designing colorblind-safe palettes. - **Always draw a diagram** for 14-mark questions on the VA process — a closed loop with bidirectional arrows earns full marks.

topics.title: Design of Visualization Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Visualization Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **Design of Visualization Applications** is formally defined as the systematic, user-centered engineering process of conceiving, prototyping, building, and evaluating interactive software systems that translate raw, abstract, or large-scale datasets into perceptually optimized graphical representations. This discipline sits at the intersection of **computer graphics, human–computer interaction (HCI), data science, and cognitive psychology**.

According to the canonical framework established by researchers such as **Card, Mackinlay, and Shneiderman (1999)** in their seminal work *"Readings in Information Visualization"*, the design of a visualization application must be grounded in three foundational pillars:

1. **Data Types** (the substance being represented)
2. **Visualization Tasks** (the operations the user needs to perform)
3. **Visual Encoding & Interaction Idioms** (the graphical mechanisms enabling perception)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A visualization application is **not just a chart** — it is a full interactive software system that supports the human analytical reasoning process through the *externalization of cognition*. A student must remember that every design decision must be justified using either **perceptual effectiveness (Cleveland & McGill hierarchy)** or **cognitive load reduction (Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a visualization application the way an **architect designs a hospital**:
- The architect does not start by drawing walls. They first study the *patients* (the **data**) and the *doctors' workflows* (the **tasks**).
- They then design *corridors, signage, and lighting* (the **visual encodings**) so that information flows to the right people at the right time.
- Finally, they test the building with real occupants (the **evaluation phase**) to ensure no one gets lost.

Similarly, a visualization designer does not start by picking a chart type. They first **interview the domain expert** to understand the data, then **observe the analytical tasks**, then choose an encoding, and finally **prototype, test, and iterate** the application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A visualization application = *(Data × Task × User)* → *(Visual Encoding + Interaction)* → *Insight & Decision*.

### 1.3 Core Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally accepted in the design of visualization applications and frequently tested in KTU examinations:

| Standard Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weber's Fraction (visual acuity) | $k$ | $\approx 0.02$ for length, $\approx 0.05$ for area, $\approx 0.10$ for volume | Justifies the perceptual ranking of visual channels |
| Color Gamut (sRGB) | — | **16.7 million** distinct colors | Defines the chromatic space for color encoding |
| Refresh Rate (Perceptual) | $f_r$ | $\ge 60$ Hz | Minimum for smooth animation in interactive applications |
| Fitts' Law Index of Difficulty | $ID$ | $\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ bits | Quantifies target-acquisition time in interactive design |
| Minimum Visual Angle (Human Eye) | $\theta$ | $\approx 1$ arc-minute ($\dfrac{1}{60}^\circ$) | Defines minimum separable pixel size |

### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| KTU CO Code | Course Outcome Description | Blooms Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | Understand the theoretical foundations of data visualization | Understand (L2) |
| **CO2** | Apply design principles to construct visualization applications | Apply (L3) |
| **CO3** | Analyze trade-offs in visual encoding and interaction | Analyze (L4) |
| **CO4** | Evaluate visualization systems using perceptual and cognitive metrics | Evaluate (L5) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Design Framework (Munzner, 2014)

Tamara Munzner's nested model provides the **canonical methodology** adopted by KTU for designing visualization applications. It is hierarchical — each level informs the next, and errors propagate downward.

**Stage 1 — Domain Situation Analysis:**
- Identify the *who* (target users), *what* (data), and *why* (tasks).
- Conduct ethnographic interviews, contextual inquiry, or persona workshops.
- Output: A list of **abstraction-level tasks** (e.g., "compare", "find anomaly", "correlate").

**Stage 2 — Data & Task Abstraction:**
- Map real-world data into abstract types: **items, attributes, links, positions, grids**.
- Map user tasks into abstract operations: **discover, present, enjoy**.
- Output: A *data–task pair* matrix.

**Stage 3 — Visual Encoding & Interaction Design:**
- Select the **idiom** (bar chart, scatterplot, node-link, treemap, parallel coordinates, etc.).
- Apply the **Cleveland-McGill ranking** to choose the most perceptually accurate visual channel.
- Define **interaction primitives**: select, explore, reconfigure, encode, filter, connect.

**Stage 4 — Algorithm Design:**
- Choose the computational pipeline (data structures, layout algorithms, GPU shaders).
- Optimize for **time complexity** (often $O(n \log n)$ for sorting/aggregation, $O(n)$ for streaming).

**Stage 5 — Evaluation & Validation:**
- Apply **heuristic, laboratory, or field studies** to validate the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Munzner's model is *nested* — meaning if your domain analysis is wrong, no amount of elegant code can rescue the application. This is a frequent 14-mark question.

### 2.2 Why and How: The Design Rationale

- **Why this model?** Because most failed visualization projects fail not at the *coding* stage but at the *abstraction* stage. Designers often start with a "cool" tool (D3.js, Tableau) instead of the data.
- **How to apply it?** Begin with **whiteboard sketches** of the data, then move to **low-fidelity wireframes**, then to **interactive prototypes**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the high-yield quantitative formulas tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Use `\vert` for absolute value, never the pipe symbol, to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cleveland-McGill Perceptual Accuracy Ranking** | Position $\succ$ Length $\succ$ Angle $\succ$ Area $\succ$ Volume $\succ$ Color | Ordinal ranking only |
| 2 | **Weber's Law (Just-Noticeable Difference)** | $\Delta I = k \cdot I$ where $I$ is the stimulus intensity, $k$ is the Weber fraction | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Fitts' Law (Target Acquisition Time)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | $T$ in seconds, $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |
| 4 | **Hick's Law (Decision Time)** | $T = b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices |
| 5 | **Information Density (pixels per data point)** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{\text{px}}}{N_{\text{records}}}$ | pixels/record; target $\le 1000$ |
| 6 | **Color Opacity (Alpha Compositing)** | $C_{\text{out}} = \alpha C_{\text{src}} + (1-\alpha)C_{\text{dst}}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 7 | **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{\text{Data-Ink}}{\text{Total Ink}}$ | Ideal $\to 1.0$ |
| 8 | **Effective Data Rate (Animation)** | $R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\log_2(N)}{T}$ | bits per second |
| 9 | **Screen Real Estate Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 10 | **Gestalt Proximity Threshold** | $d_{\text{prox}} < 2 \cdot d_{\text{sep}}$ | Separations $d$ in pixels |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Visualization Application | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Flight trajectory dashboards (e.g., NASA WorldWind) | Air-traffic control, mission planning |
| **Biomedical** | fMRI volumetric rendering | Surgical planning, neuroscience research |
| **Finance** | High-frequency trading candlestick charts | Algorithmic trading decision support |
| **Smart Cities** | Real-time urban heatmaps | Traffic management, pollution monitoring |
| **Cybersecurity** | Network intrusion node-link diagrams | Threat hunting, anomaly detection |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "D-T-I-A-E":** *Domain → Tasks → Idiom → Algorithm → Evaluation*. Use this to remember the five design stages in any order-sensitive question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fitts' Law Index of Difficulty (Board-Favorite)

Fitts' Law is the most frequently asked quantitative derivation in KTU 2024 examinations on visualization application design. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivation**.

**Step 1 — State the Empirical Observation.**
A human operator, when moving a pointer (mouse, finger, or stylus) toward a target of width $W$ located at distance $D$, requires an average movement time $T$ that is *linear* in the **Index of Difficulty** of the target.

**Step 2 — Define the Index of Difficulty $ID$.**
By the analogy to Shannon's channel capacity theorem (1948), the *information capacity* of a motor-control channel is bounded by the logarithmic ratio of target distance to target tolerance.

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

The "+1" prevents the argument from being zero or negative when $D=0$ (target on top of cursor).

**Step 3 — Express the Movement Time.**
A linear regression over many trials yields the **Fitts' Law equation**:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are empirically fitted constants (typically $a \approx 0.05$ s, $b \approx 0.1$ s/bit for a standard mouse).

**Step 4 — Throughput Definition.**
The **Index of Performance** (or *throughput*) $IP$ is defined as the inverse of the slope $b$:

$$IP = \dfrac{1}{b} = \dfrac{ID}{T - a} \quad \text{[bits/second]}$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification.**
Substituting the expression for $ID$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \dfrac{\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)}{T - a} \\
   &= \dfrac{\log_2(D+W) - \log_2(W)}{T - a}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that $IP$ is **device-independent** and can be used to compare mice, touchscreens, and eye-trackers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The transition from Step 1 to Step 2 is justified by treating the human motor system as an *information channel*. The "+1" comes from the *effective target width* $W_e = W + \sigma$, where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of endpoint distribution, ensuring the logarithm is well-defined.

### 3.2 Derivation of Weber's Law and Application to Color Encodings

**Step 1 — Statement.** The smallest perceptible difference in a stimulus $\Delta I$ is proportional to the original stimulus magnitude $I$.

**Step 2 — Define.**

$$\Delta I = k \cdot I \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Discriminable Color Bins.**
For a color channel (e.g., luminance) ranging from $L_{\min}$ to $L_{\max}$, the number of *Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)* steps is obtained by integrating the reciprocal of the differential:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{JND}} &= \int_{L_{\min}}^{L_{\max}} \dfrac{dL}{k \cdot L} \\
              &= \dfrac{1}{k} \int_{L_{\min}}^{L_{\max}} \dfrac{dL}{L} \\
              &= \dfrac{1}{k} \Bigl[\ln(L)\Bigr]_{L_{\min}}^{L_{\max}} \\
              &= \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right) \\
              &= \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)}{k \cdot \log_{10}(e)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**
For luminance $L_{\min} = 1$ and $L_{\max} = 1000$, with $k = 0.02$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{JND}} &= \dfrac{\log_{10}(1000)}{0.02 \times 0.4343} \\
              &= \dfrac{3.0}{0.008686} \\
              &\approx 345 \text{ JND steps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** A visualization designer should use **at most 10–12 distinct luminance levels** in a sequential colormap, because beyond this, the human eye cannot reliably distinguish them — leading to false visual patterns.

### 3.3 The Visualization Design Trade-off Matrix (Analytical Tool)

This is a high-yield tool for 14-mark KTU questions comparing two visualization idioms.

| Design Axis | Scatterplot | Parallel Coordinates | Treemap | Node-Link Graph |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for (Data Type)** | 2D continuous | High-dimensional | Hierarchical | Network |
| **Cognitive Load** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Scalability (n)** | $10^4$ | $10^3$ | $10^4$ | $10^2$ |
| **Task: Find Cluster** | Excellent | Good | Poor | Fair |
| **Task: Find Outlier** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Fair |
| **Color Encodable?** | Yes (3rd dim) | Yes (lines) | Yes (categories) | Yes (nodes) |
| **KTU Complexity Rating** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Matrix?** Because a student must learn to argue the *trade-off* between scalability and task-support. KTU examiners explicitly award 2 marks for an explicit "**limitations**" paragraph in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Munzner Five-Stage Nested Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Domain Situation] --> B[Stage 2: Data and Task Abstraction]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Visual Encoding and Interaction Design]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Algorithm Design]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Evaluation and Validation]
    A -.feedback.-> E
    B -.feedback.-> A
    C -.feedback.-> B
    D -.feedback.-> C
    E -.feedback.-> D

    A --> A1[Identify Users]
    A --> A2[Identify Data Sources]
    A --> A3[Identify Tasks]

    B --> B1[Abstract Data Types]
    B --> B2[Abstract Task Operations]

    C --> C1[Select Visual Idiom]
    C --> C2[Apply Cleveland McGill Ranking]
    C --> C3[Define Interaction Primitives]

    D --> D1[Choose Data Structures]
    D --> D2[Optimize Time Complexity]

    E --> E1[Heuristic Evaluation]
    E --> E2[User Studies]
    E --> E3[Field Deployment]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Visualization Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        rawData[(Raw Data Source)]
        userInput[User Interaction Input]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer]
        parser[Data Parser]
        transformer[Data Transformer]
        aggregator[Aggregation Engine]
    end

    subgraph EncodingLayer[Encoding Layer]
        visualMap[Visual Mapping Engine]
        colorPicker[Color Palette Generator]
        layoutEngine[Layout Algorithm Module]
    end

    subgraph RenderingLayer[Rendering Layer]
        canvas[Canvas or SVG Renderer]
        gpuAccel[GPU Acceleration]
        animator[Animation Controller]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        display[Display Device]
        feedback[Haptic or Audio Feedback]
    end

    rawData --> parser
    userInput --> transformer
    parser --> transformer
    transformer --> aggregator
    aggregator --> visualMap
    visualMap --> colorPicker
    visualMap --> layoutEngine
    colorPicker --> canvas
    layoutEngine --> canvas
    canvas --> gpuAccel
    gpuAccel --> animator
    animator --> display
    display --> feedback
    feedback --> userInput
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Choosing a Visual Idiom

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: New Visualization Task] --> q1{Is data 1D, 2D, or higher?}
    q1 -->|1D| q1a{Categorical or Numeric?}
    q1a -->|Categorical| vBar[Bar Chart]
    q1a -->|Numeric| vHist[Histogram]
    q1 -->|2D| q2{Is relationship correlation or distribution?}
    q2 -->|Correlation| vScat[Scatterplot]
    q2 -->|Distribution| vDens[2D Density Plot]
    q1 -->|Higher D| q3{Number of dimensions?}
    q3 -->|3 to 8| vPar[Parallel Coordinates]
    q3 -->|9 to 50| vRad[Radar Chart]
    q3 -->|More than 50| vPCA[PCA or t-SNE Projection]
    q1 -->|Hierarchical| vTree[Treemap or Sunburst]
    q1 -->|Network| vGraph[Node Link Diagram]
    q1 -->|Temporal| vTime[Line Chart or Streamgraph]
    q1 -->|Geospatial| vGeo[Choropleth Map]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Insight:** The decision tree above is *deterministic* — given the data type, only one or two idioms are perceptually optimal. A KTU answer that proposes a non-optimal idiom without justification will lose at least **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define "Visualization Application Design" and list any **four** key design principles.

**Model Answer:**
A *visualization application* is a software system that transforms abstract data into interactive graphical representations to support perception, comprehension, and decision-making.
The four key design principles are:
1. **Data-Ink Maximization** (Tufte) — remove non-essential chart junk.
2. **Perceptual Accuracy** (Cleveland-McGill) — prefer position over color over volume.
3. **Cognitive Load Reduction** — minimize working-memory burden on the user.
4. **Interactivity & Feedback** — support user exploration with sub-100 ms response time.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for four principles, 1 for sub-bullet elaboration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** State **Fitts' Law** and explain its significance in the design of interactive visualization applications.

**Model Answer:**
Fitts' Law states that the time $T$ to acquire a target is a logarithmic function of the ratio of distance to target width:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$$

**Significance:** It provides a *quantitative basis* for sizing buttons, toolbars, and selectable marks in a visualization. Larger targets and shorter distances reduce acquisition time, improving user efficiency and satisfaction. It is widely used in dashboard design and is empirically validated across input devices.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 for the formula, 1 for definition of $D$ and $W$, 1 for real-world application.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark ESE Module Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that every Part B question must offer an **internal choice**. Below, the two alternatives are entirely independent and test different cognitive levels.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

> *Design a complete visualization application to help Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) engineers analyze **district-wise daily electricity consumption** over a 5-year period. Your answer must include:*
> *(a) Domain analysis, data abstraction, and task analysis. \[7 Marks\]*
> *(b) Visual encoding choice, interaction design, and a sketch of the system architecture. \[7 Marks\]*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Domain Analysis, Data Abstraction, and Task Analysis** *(7 Marks)*

1. **Domain Analysis (2 marks):**
   - **Who:** KSEB planning engineers, policy makers, and citizen-facing analysts.
   - **What:** Tabular dataset with 14 districts × 1825 days × 4 attributes (consumption kWh, peak hour, population, tariff slab).
   - **Why:** Detect consumption trends, identify load-shedding hotspots, forecast demand.

2. **Data Abstraction (2.5 marks):**
   - *Items:* 14 districts.
   - *Attributes:* Consumption (quantitative, sequential), date (ordinal, temporal), peak hour (quantitative, cyclic).
   - *Derived data:* Year-over-year deltas, district rankings.

3. **Task Abstraction (2.5 marks):**
   - Compare consumption across districts (T1).
   - Identify trend and seasonality (T2).
   - Locate outliers (anomaly districts) (T3).
   - Summarize (T4).

**(b) Visual Encoding, Interaction, and System Architecture** *(7 Marks)*

1. **Visual Encoding (2.5 marks):**
   - Use a **choropleth map** of Kerala with districts as polygons; color encodes consumption magnitude (sequential viridis colormap).
   - Overlay a **small-multiples line chart** showing 5-year trend per district.
   - Use **position (latitude/longitude)** for the geographic channel — highest accuracy per Cleveland-McGill.

2. **Interaction Design (2 marks):**
   - **Brushing & linking:** hovering over a district highlights its trend chart.
   - **Slider:** time-range selection (year, month).
   - **Filter dropdown:** tariff slab filter.
   - **Zoom & pan:** map navigation, governed by Fitts' Law — minimum target size 44×44 px for touch.

3. **System Architecture Sketch (2.5 marks):**

   ```
   CSV → Pandas DataFrame → Aggregator → D3.js Choropleth + Line Charts
            ↑                                          ↓
         User Filter ←  Brushing-Linking Controller
   ```

   - **Data layer:** PostgreSQL with indexed date+district.
   - **Logic layer:** Python (Pandas, NumPy) for aggregation.
   - **Presentation layer:** D3.js for SVG rendering; Leaflet for map tiles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write a single bar chart and call it "interactive". This loses 4 marks for missing brushing/linking.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Skipping the *task* analysis. KTU expects explicit listing of **at least 3 tasks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Choosing pie chart for district comparison — **pie chart is the worst idiom for comparison** and will cost 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

> *(a) Explain Munzner's nested model for visualization design. Describe each of the five stages with an example. \[7 Marks\]*
> *(b) Derive Fitts' Law from the perspective of information theory and apply it to size the buttons of a data dashboard with an example calculation. \[7 Marks\]*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Munzner's Nested Model** *(7 Marks)*

The model has **five nested levels** — each level informs the next:

1. **Domain Situation (1 mark):** Real-world problem context.
   - *Example:* Hospital admin analyzing patient wait times.
2. **Data & Task Abstraction (1.5 marks):** Translate to abstract types.
   - *Example:* 50,000 patient records (items) with attributes *wait time*, *severity*, *department*.
3. **Visual Encoding & Interaction (1.5 marks):** Choose idiom.
   - *Example:* Box plot per department with drill-down.
4. **Algorithm Design (1.5 marks):** Choose data structures.
   - *Example:* KD-tree for nearest-neighbor search, $O(\log n)$ lookup.
5. **Evaluation (1.5 marks):** Test with users.
   - *Example:* A/B test of two layouts with 30 users, measuring task-completion time.

> **Key Property:** The model is *nested* — a mistake at any level propagates downward, and correction must happen at the level where the mistake was made.

**(b) Derivation and Application of Fitts' Law** *(7 Marks)*

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Treat the human motor system as a Shannon information channel. The information $I$ (in bits) required to acquire a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ is:

$$I = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

By Fitts' empirical law, the movement time $T$ is linearly proportional to $I$:

$$T = a + b \cdot I = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

**Application to Dashboard Button Sizing (3.5 marks):**

A dashboard button is currently 30 px wide, located 200 px from the cursor. We want to **halve** the acquisition time.

**Current State:**
$$ID_{\text{old}} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{200}{30}+1\right) = \log_2(7.667) \approx 2.94 \text{ bits}$$

**New State (target $T$ halved):**
$$ID_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{ID_{\text{old}}}{2} = 1.47 \text{ bits}$$

**Solve for the new width $W_{\text{new}}$:**
$$1.47 = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{200}{W_{\text{new}}}+1\right)$$
$$\dfrac{200}{W_{\text{new}}}+1 = 2^{1.47} \approx 2.77$$
$$\dfrac{200}{W_{\text{new}}} = 1.77$$
$$W_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{200}{1.77} \approx 113 \text{ px}$$

**Conclusion:** To halve the acquisition time, the button width must increase from **30 px to 113 px** — a 3.77× enlargement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the "+1" in the logarithm — this causes a singular value at $D=0$ and will cost 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $a$ (intercept) with $b$ (slope). The throughput is $1/b$, not $1/a$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not showing unit conversions (pixels ↔ bits). Always state units explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the *nested* property of Munzner's model — a high-frequency KTU trick question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

- [x] A **visualization application** is a full interactive software system, not just a chart.
- [x] **Munzner's nested model** = *D-T-I-A-E* (Domain, Task, Idiom, Algorithm, Evaluation).
- [x] **Cleveland-McGill ranking**: Position $\succ$ Length $\succ$ Angle $\succ$ Area $\succ$ Volume $\succ$ Color.
- [x] **Fitts' Law** is the cornerstone of interactive design: $T = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- [x] **Weber's Law** dictates the *just-noticeable difference*: $\Delta I = kI$.
- [x] **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio** $\to 1.0$ minimizes "chart junk".
- [x] **Hick's Law** $T = b \log_2(n+1)$ justifies menu simplification in dashboards.
- [x] Always state **abstract data type** (item, attribute, link) and **abstract task** (discover, present, enjoy).
- [x] **Evaluation methods**: heuristic, lab study, field study — KTU expects at least one explicit method.
- [x] For 14-mark questions, **always include a sketch/diagram** — KTU awards up to 2 marks for it.
- [x] **Pie charts are forbidden for comparison** — they are the lowest in the perceptual hierarchy.
- [x] Maximum **10–12 luminance steps** in a sequential colormap (Weber's Law constraint).
- [x] The **internal choice** in Part B is mandatory — practice both Q-A and Q-B variants.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Design of Visualization Applications**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Visualization Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Visualization Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Visualization System** is a software/hardware stack that transforms abstract, often multidimensional data into graphical primitives (points, lines, glyphs, surfaces, volumes, networks) perceivable by the human visual system to support **cognition, analysis, decision-making, and communication**. *Classification of Visualization Systems* is the structured taxonomy that groups these systems along well-defined axes such as **data type, user task, user expertise, interaction style, display medium, and application domain**, allowing researchers, designers, and end-users to select, compare, and evaluate the most appropriate system for a given problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The classification framework is the foundation for the rest of Module 2. Every visualization technique covered subsequently (charts, trees, networks, geographic maps, scientific volumes) maps back to one or more of these classification axes. Examiners *expect* students to know the **"Why this class?"** reasoning, not just the names.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library of Visual Tools

Imagine a massive **central library** that contains every possible picture, chart, and computer-generated image ever created. The librarian cannot keep the shelves in a random heap — she must classify them:

- **By Genre of the Book (Data Type):** Is the data *numerical, textual, hierarchical, spatial, or temporal*? Maps go in the Atlas Section, family trees in the Genealogy Section, stock prices in the Finance Section.
- **By Reader Level (User Expertise):** Children's picture books are shelved near the entrance; PhD theses in the back stacks.
- **By Purpose (User Task):** Cookbooks for cooking, repair manuals for fixing, novels for entertainment.
- **By Reading Room (Display Medium):** A palm-sized paperback vs a wall-sized mural — same content, different *physical medium of presentation*.

A *visualization system* is just such a "library" of graphical representations. The classification gives the user (or designer) a **filing system** to find the right visual tool quickly. Without classification, a Data Scientist facing a network of 10 million social connections would waste hours deciding between a *node-link diagram*, a *chord diagram*, or an *adjacency matrix* — the classification tells her: *"Network data → node-link or matrix."*

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters (Engineering Utility)

| Engineering Driver | Role of Classification |
|---|---|
| **System Design** | Helps architects decide rendering pipelines (WebGL, OpenGL, Volumetric Ray Casting) based on data type class. |
| **Tool Selection** | Engineers pick Tableau (info-viz), ParaView (scientific viz), or Kepler.gl (geospatial viz) by classifying their data. |
| **Performance Optimization** | A classified system uses known rendering heuristics (e.g., LOD for 3D, edge bundling for networks). |
| **Cognitive Load Reduction** | Users trained on a class recognize patterns faster (e.g., a heatmap is *always* 2D matrix data). |
| **Standardization (KTU/Course Outcome)** | Enables reusable evaluation metrics and consistent academic discourse. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms to Master for KTU Exams:**
> - **Scientific Visualization (SciViz)** — Visualizing *physically grounded* data (CT scans, fluid flow, climate).
> - **Information Visualization (InfoViz)** — Visualizing *abstract* data (databases, social networks, financial).
> - **Visual Analytics** — Combines automated analysis with interactive visualization for sense-making.
> - **Immersive Analytics** — Uses VR/AR/Cave displays to embed the user *inside* the data.
> - **Task-by-Data-Type Taxonomy** — The seminal classification introduced by **Shneiderman's Mantra**: *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Major Classification Axes (Most-Tested Framework)

A visualization system can be classified along **six orthogonal axes**. Each axis answers a *different question* about the system. Mastering these six axes is sufficient to answer nearly every KTU 2-mark, 3-mark, and 7-mark theory question on this topic.

#### Axis 1 — Classification by **Data Type / Dimensionality**
This is the most fundamental classification. It follows the **Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman (1999) "Data Type × Task"** taxonomy.

$$\text{Data Class} \in \{ \text{1D}, \text{2D}, \text{3D}, \text{Multi-D}, \text{Temporal}, \text{Hierarchical (Tree)}, \text{Network (Graph)}, \text{Text/Document} \}$$

- **1-Dimensional Data:** Linear, sequential (e.g., time series, log files). Tools: line charts, sparklines.
- **2-Dimensional Data:** Maps, floor plans, matrices. Tools: heatmaps, choropleths.
- **3-Dimensional Data:** Surfaces, volumes, molecules. Tools: isosurface, volume rendering, ray-casting.
- **Multidimensional Data:** $\geq 4$ attributes per record. Tools: parallel coordinates, scatterplot matrix (SPLOM), glyphs.
- **Temporal Data:** Time-indexed records. Tools: timelines, time-series, animations.
- **Hierarchical Data:** Tree structures (org charts, file systems, taxonomies). Tools: treemaps, sunburst, icicle plots.
- **Network Data:** Graphs with arbitrary connectivity. Tools: node-link, adjacency matrix, arc diagrams.
- **Textual / Document Data:** Free text, corpora. Tools: tag clouds, word trees, theme rivers.

#### Axis 2 — Classification by **Application Domain**

$$\text{Domain} \in \{ \text{SciViz}, \text{InfoViz}, \text{GeoViz}, \text{SoftwareViz}, \text{BioViz} \}$$

- **SciViz:** Spatial, physically grounded (e.g., MRI, CFD, astronomical data).
- **InfoViz:** Abstract, non-physical data (databases, networks, financial).
- **GeoViz:** Geographically referenced (maps, GPS, GIS systems).
- **SoftwareViz:** Visualizing software structure (call graphs, dependency graphs).
- **BioViz:** Genomic, protein, phylogenetic trees.

#### Axis 3 — Classification by **User Expertise Level** (Tory & Möller, 2004)

$$\text{UserClass} \in \{ \text{Casual/Novice}, \text{Analyst/Expert}, \text{Designer/Developer} \}$$

- **Novice (Casual User):** Pre-designed templates, dashboards (Tableau Public, PowerBI).
- **Analyst (Domain Expert):** Interactive exploration, statistical overlay (GGobi, Spotfire).
- **Designer/Developer:** Programmatic construction (D3.js, VTK, ParaView).

#### Axis 4 — Classification by **Primary User Task** (Shneiderman, 1996)

$$\text{Task} \in \{ \text{Overview (}\Sigma\text{)}, \text{Zoom (Z)}, \text{Filter (F)}, \text{Details-on-Demand (D)}, \text{Relate (R)}, \text{Extract (E)}, \text{Encode} \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shneiderman's Mantra (frequently asked in KTU):** *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand."* This single sentence is the most-quoted classification in Information Visualization.

#### Axis 5 — Classification by **Display Medium / Immersion Level**

$$\text{Display} \in \{ \text{Desktop 2D}, \text{Desktop 3D (Stereo)}, \text{Large Wall Display}, \text{CAVE (Room-scale VR)}, \text{Head-Mounted Display (HMD)}, \text{Mobile/Tablet} \}$$

- **Desktop 2D:** Standard monitor, mouse/keyboard.
- **Desktop 3D:** Polarized glasses, depth perception.
- **Wall Displays:** Multi-projector powerwalls (research labs).
- **CAVE:** Room with projected walls + tracked shutter glasses.
- **HMD/AR/VR:** Oculus, HoloLens — full immersion.

#### Axis 6 — Classification by **Interaction & Automation Style**

$$\text{Interaction} \in \{ \text{Presentational (Static)}, \text{Direct Manipulation (Interactive)}, \text{Automated/Recommender}, \text{Collaborative (Multi-user)} \}$$

- **Presentational:** Static infographics (newspaper charts).
- **Direct Manipulation:** Real-time user feedback (Tableau, D3).
- **Automated:** System chooses the chart (e.g., Microsoft Research's AutoViz, SeeDB).
- **Collaborative:** Multiple users in shared space (Google Docs visualization, Miro).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important revision asset** for this topic. Memorize the *mapping* (Data Class → Typical Technique), not the wording.

| Classification Axis | Sub-Class | Typical Data | Representative Technique | Representative System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **By Data Type** | 1D | Logs, time-series | Line chart, sparkline | Kibana |
| **By Data Type** | 2D | Maps, matrices | Choropleth, heatmap | Google Maps, D3.js |
| **By Data Type** | 3D | Volume, surfaces | Isosurface, ray casting | ParaView, VisIt |
| **By Data Type** | Multi-D | Tabular (n-D) | Parallel coordinates, SPLOM | GGobi, Orange |
| **By Data Type** | Temporal | Time-indexed | Timeline, animation | Gapminder |
| **By Data Type** | Hierarchical | Tree | Treemap, sunburst | D3.js Hierarchy |
| **By Data Type** | Network | Graph | Node-link, matrix | Gephi, Cytoscape |
| **By Domain** | SciViz | Physical | Volume rendering | VTK, Avizo |
| **By Domain** | InfoViz | Abstract | Dashboards | Tableau, PowerBI |
| **By User** | Novice | Any | Template, dashboard | Excel charts |
| **By User** | Expert | Any | Programmatic toolkit | D3.js, Vega-Lite |
| **By Task** | Overview | Any | Bird's-eye view | Treemap, heatmap |
| **By Task** | Filter | Any | Brushing, dynamic query | Spotfire |
| **By Task** | Details-on-Demand | Any | Tooltip, drill-down | Tableau |
| **By Display** | Desktop 2D | Any | Standard | Most tools |
| **By Display** | Immersive | 3D / SciViz | CAVE, HMD | Unity Viz, ParaView VR |
| **By Interaction** | Static | Communicative | Infographic | Newspapers |
| **By Interaction** | Interactive | Analytical | Brushing, linking | Tableau, Vega |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters Beyond Exams

- **Procurement Decisions:** A B.Tech engineer selecting a BI tool for an Indian PSU classifies requirements → maps to *Domain × User Class* → shortlists Tableau, PowerBI, or Superset.
- **Research Direction:** Graduate research students defend their thesis by positioning their work inside the *Data-Type × Task* matrix (a 2-D plot is the de-facto publication strategy).
- **Performance Engineering:** SciViz systems handling volumetric data need GPU ray-casting pipelines; InfoViz systems handling millions of nodes need WebGL + edge bundling. The *Display × Domain* classification determines the entire software stack.
- **Standardization:** International standards like **ISO/IEC 25010** for software quality use these classifications when evaluating visualization tools for usability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — How to Classify Any Visualization System

Because this topic is conceptual rather than algebraic, the "derivation" is replaced by an **exhaustive analytical procedure** that walks you through classifying *any* visualization system you encounter (in the exam or in industry). This is exactly how KTU examiners test *application-level* understanding.

### 3.1 The 6-Step Classification Procedure

> **Given:** A description of a visualization system (or a screenshot, or a tool name).
> **Goal:** Produce a complete, justifiable classification.

#### Step 1 — Identify the **Data Type** ($D$)
- *Question:* What is the intrinsic structure of the data?
- *Action:* Examine the dataset. Is it linear (1D), planar (2D), volumetric (3D), tabular (Multi-D), time-indexed (Temporal), recursive (Hierarchical), relational (Network), or linguistic (Text)?
- *Output:* A single label $D \in \{\text{1D, 2D, 3D, M-D, Temp, Tree, Network, Text}\}$.

#### Step 2 — Identify the **Application Domain** ($\mathcal{A}$)
- *Question:* Is the data physically grounded or abstract?
- *Rule:*

$$
\mathcal{A} = \begin{cases} \text{SciViz} & \text{if data is sampled from a continuous physical field} \\ \text{InfoViz} & \text{if data is abstract / discrete / business} \\ \text{GeoViz} & \text{if data has lat/long reference} \\ \text{Software/BioViz} & \text{if domain is software engineering or life-sciences} \end{cases}
$$

#### Step 3 — Identify the **Target User Class** ($U$)
- *Question:* Who is the end-user?
- *Output:* $U \in \{\text{Novice, Analyst, Designer}\}$.

#### Step 4 — Identify the **Primary User Task** ($T$)
- *Question:* What does the user *do* with the visualization?
- *Map using Shneiderman's task set:*

$$
T \in \{\Sigma, Z, F, D_{od}, R, E, \text{Encode}, \text{Compare}, \text{Discover}\} $$

#### Step 5 — Identify the **Display Medium** ($M$)
- *Question:* What hardware is the rendering surface?
- *Output:* $M \in \{\text{2D, 3D-Stereo, Wall, CAVE, HMD, Mobile}\}$.

#### Step 6 — Identify the **Interaction Style** ($I$)
- *Question:* Does the user actively manipulate, or merely view?
- *Output:* $I \in \{\text{Static, Direct-Manipulation, Automated, Collaborative}\}$.

The complete classification is the **6-tuple**:

$$
\mathcal{C} = (D, \mathcal{A}, U, T, M, I) $$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Classifying Tableau

> **Problem (KTU-style):** "Classify the visualization system **Tableau** using the axes above."

- **Step 1 (Data Type):** Tableau handles **multi-dimensional tabular data**, often joined with **temporal** and **geographic** fields. So $D = \text{Multi-D}$ (with extensions to Temp and Geo).
- **Step 2 (Domain):** Business and analytical data → abstract. $\mathcal{A} = \text{InfoViz}$.
- **Step 3 (User):** Designed for business users with no programming skill. $U = \text{Novice}$ (with expert extensions).
- **Step 4 (Task):** Built around Shneiderman's mantra — drag-and-drop overview, filters, drill-down. $T = \{\Sigma, Z, F, D_{od}\}$.
- **Step 5 (Display):** Standard desktop monitor. $M = \text{2D Desktop}$.
- **Step 6 (Interaction):** Direct manipulation via mouse drag. $I = \text{Direct-Manipulation}$.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C}_{\text{Tableau}} = (\text{Multi-D},\ \text{InfoViz},\ \text{Novice},\ \{\Sigma,Z,F,D_{od}\},\ \text{2D Desktop},\ \text{Direct-Manipulation})}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Classifying ParaView

- **Step 1:** Volume data from CFD/MRI. $D = \text{3D}$.
- **Step 2:** $\mathcal{A} = \text{SciViz}$.
- **Step 3:** $U = \text{Expert}$ (engineer/researcher).
- **Step 4:** $T = \{\Sigma, Z, D_{od}, \text{Compare}\}$ — slicing, isosurface extraction, probe.
- **Step 5:** Supports desktop 3D-stereo and CAVE. $M = \{\text{2D}, \text{3D-Stereo}, \text{CAVE}\}$.
- **Step 6:** Direct manipulation with Python scripting. $I = \{\text{Direct-Manipulation}, \text{Programmatic}\}$.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C}_{\text{ParaView}} = (\text{3D},\ \text{SciViz},\ \text{Expert},\ \{\Sigma,Z,D_{od}\},\ \text{CAVE/3D},\ \text{Direct+Script})}$$

### 3.4 Comparative Decision Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| If Data Is… | And User Is… | And Task Is… | Then The System Class Is… |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multi-D, abstract | Novice | Overview/Compare | **Dashboard InfoViz** (Tableau, PowerBI) |
| 3D physical | Expert | Explore/Probe | **SciViz Tool** (ParaView, VisIt) |
| Hierarchical | Any | Overview | **Tree-based InfoViz** (D3, Sunburst) |
| Network | Analyst | Discover communities | **Graph Viz** (Gephi, Cytoscape) |
| Geo-referenced | Any | Compare regions | **GeoViz** (Kepler.gl, ArcGIS) |
| Streaming, temporal | Analyst | Monitor | **Real-time InfoViz** (Grafana, Kibana) |
| Volumetric | Expert in CAVE | Immersive analysis | **Immersive SciViz** (VR- ParaView) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 7-mark questions, always justify *each axis* with one line of reasoning. Marks are awarded **per axis identified** plus the **final integrated classification**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Master Classification Taxonomy

The diagram below captures the complete 6-axis classification tree. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed to avoid the Mermaid reserved-keyword collision; labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["VISUALIZATION SYSTEM"]
    
    rootA --> dataAx["AXIS 1 DATA TYPE"]
    rootA --> domAx["AXIS 2 DOMAIN"]
    rootA --> usrAx["AXIS 3 USER CLASS"]
    rootA --> taskAx["AXIS 4 USER TASK"]
    rootA --> disAx["AXIS 5 DISPLAY MEDIUM"]
    rootA --> intAx["AXIS 6 INTERACTION STYLE"]
    
    dataAx --> d1["1D LINEAR"]
    dataAx --> d2["2D PLANAR"]
    dataAx --> d3["3D VOLUMETRIC"]
    dataAx --> d4["MULTI DIMENSIONAL"]
    dataAx --> d5["TEMPORAL"]
    dataAx --> d6["HIERARCHICAL TREE"]
    dataAx --> d7["NETWORK GRAPH"]
    dataAx --> d8["TEXTUAL DOCUMENT"]
    
    domAx --> dom1["SCIVIZ PHYSICAL"]
    domAx --> dom2["INFOVIZ ABSTRACT"]
    domAx --> dom3["GEOVIZ SPATIAL"]
    domAx --> dom4["SOFTWARE VIZ"]
    domAx --> dom5["BIO VIZ"]
    
    usrAx --> usr1["NOVICE CASUAL"]
    usrAx --> usr2["ANALYST EXPERT"]
    usrAx --> usr3["DESIGNER DEVELOPER"]
    
    taskAx --> tk1["OVERVIEW SIGMA"]
    taskAx --> tk2["ZOOM"]
    taskAx --> tk3["FILTER"]
    taskAx --> tk4["DETAILS ON DEMAND"]
    taskAx --> tk5["RELATE COMPARE"]
    taskAx --> tk6["EXTRACT HISTORY"]
    
    disAx --> dis1["DESKTOP 2D"]
    disAx --> dis2["DESKTOP 3D STEREO"]
    disAx --> dis3["WALL POWERWALL"]
    disAx --> dis4["CAVE ROOM VR"]
    disAx --> dis5["HEAD MOUNTED DISPLAY"]
    disAx --> dis6["MOBILE TABLET"]
    
    intAx --> int1["STATIC PRESENTATIONAL"]
    intAx --> int2["DIRECT MANIPULATION"]
    intAx --> int3["AUTOMATED RECOMMENDER"]
    intAx --> int4["COLLABORATIVE MULTI USER"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a Classified Visualization System

This block-diagram maps how the *classification* of a system translates into its *internal functional modules*. Use this when explaining "where classification is *used inside* the system."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["RAW DATA INPUT"]
    clsA["CLASSIFICATION ENGINE 6 AXIS"]
    rendA["RENDERING PIPELINE"]
    outA["DISPLAY MEDIUM"]
    usrA["USER INTERACTION LOOP"]
    
    inA --> clsA
    clsA --> rendA
    rendA --> outA
    outA --> usrA
    usrA --> clsA
    
    clsA --> axisD["DATA TYPE MODULE"]
    clsA --> axisB["DOMAIN MODULE"]
    clsA --> axisC["USER PROFILE"]
    clsA --> axisE["TASK SELECTOR"]
    
    rendA --> renderB["GPU SHADER PIPELINE"]
    rendA --> renderC["WEBGL CANVAS"]
    rendA --> renderD["VOLUME RAY CASTER"]
    rendA --> renderE["VR RENDERER"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — SciViz vs InfoViz Classification Boundary

A frequently-tested comparison. The diagram captures the canonical separation with examples.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rootB["VISUALIZATION SYSTEMS"]
    rootB --> sv["SCIENTIFIC VIZ SCIVIZ"]
    rootB --> iv["INFORMATION VIZ INFOVIZ"]
    
    sv --> svA["DATA PHYSICALLY GROUNDED"]
    sv --> svB["EX MRI CT CFD ASTRONOMY"]
    sv --> svC["TECHNIQUE VOLUME RENDERING ISOSURFACE"]
    sv --> svD["USER DOMAIN EXPERT"]
    sv --> svE["EXAMPLE PARAVIEW VISIT VTK"]
    
    iv --> ivA["DATA ABSTRACT SYMBOLIC"]
    iv --> ivB["EX DATABASE NETWORK TEXT"]
    iv --> ivC["TECHNIQUE TREE NETWORK CHART"]
    iv --> ivD["USER ANALYST NOVICE EXPERT"]
    iv --> ivE["EXAMPLE TABLEAU D3 POWERBI"]
```

### 4.4 Reading the Diagrams (For Exam Use)

- **Diagram 1** is the *master taxonomy*. In the exam, redraw a simplified version with at least **three axes** and one example per axis.
- **Diagram 2** explains *how* classification is *operationalized inside* a system — useful for the CO (Course Outcome) question: *"Explain the role of classification in visualization system design."*
- **Diagram 3** is the **SciViz vs InfoViz** comparison — a guaranteed question. Memorize the 4 contrasts (data type, technique, user, examples).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Visualization System. List any FOUR major classification axes used to categorize visualization systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Visualization System** is a hardware/software stack that converts abstract or physical data into graphical representations perceivable by the human visual system, supporting **analysis, decision-making, and communication**.

The four major classification axes (1/2 mark each):
1. **By Data Type** — 1D, 2D, 3D, Multi-D, Temporal, Hierarchical, Network, Text.
2. **By Application Domain** — SciViz, InfoViz, GeoViz, SoftwareViz, BioViz.
3. **By User Expertise** — Novice, Analyst, Designer/Developer.
4. **By User Task** — Overview, Zoom, Filter, Details-on-Demand, Relate, Extract (Shneiderman's Mantra).

*(Other valid axes: Display Medium, Interaction Style — accept any four with one-line justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Scientific Visualization and Information Visualization along any FOUR parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | SciViz | InfoViz |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Nature** | Physically grounded, continuous field | Abstract, symbolic, discrete |
| **Examples** | MRI, CFD, climate simulation | Databases, social networks, finance |
| **Techniques** | Volume rendering, isosurface, slicing | Treemap, node-link, parallel coordinates |
| **User** | Domain expert (engineer, scientist) | Novice to analyst (business user) |
| **Tool Examples** | ParaView, VisIt, Avizo | Tableau, D3.js, PowerBI |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two questions, answer ANY ONE. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose EITHER A or B**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]**

> **Q.A.** *(a)* Discuss the **Shneiderman's "Task by Data Type" taxonomy** for classifying visualization systems. State Shneiderman's Mantra and explain each task with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Classify the following visualization systems along the four major axes (Data Type, Domain, User, Task) — **(i) Microsoft Excel Charts, (ii) Gephi, (iii) Google Earth, (iv) ParaView**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Shneiderman's Task-by-Data-Type Taxonomy (7 marks)

Shneiderman (1996) proposed that every visualization system can be classified along two orthogonal dimensions — **Data Type** (rows) and **User Task** (columns). The taxonomy forms a **2-D matrix** that designers use to position their tools.

- **Shneiderman's Mantra (1 mark):** *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand."*

- **The Seven Tasks (1 mark each, total 6 marks):**
  1. **Overview (Σ):** Gain a global summary of the entire dataset. *Example:* A treemap of a company's sales shows the largest division in the largest box.
  2. **Zoom (Z):** Magnify a region of interest. *Example:* Zooming into the Kerala district on a choropleth.
  3. **Filter (F):** Remove uninteresting items dynamically. *Example:* Slider that filters all transactions > ₹10,000.
  4. **Details-on-Demand (D):** Select an item to reveal more information. *Example:* Tooltip on a node in Gephi showing person details.
  5. **Relate (R):** View relationships between items. *Example:* Brushing-and-linking across a SPLOM.
  6. **Extract (E):** Remove a sub-collection for separate analysis. *Example:* Export a sub-cluster from a network.
  7. **History (H):** Undo/redo exploration steps. *Example:* Back button in Tableau.

#### Part (b) — Classification of Four Systems (7 marks, 1.75 marks per system)

| System | Data Type | Domain | User | Primary Task |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Excel Charts** | Multi-D (tabular) | InfoViz (business) | Novice | Overview, Filter, Details |
| **Gephi** | Network | InfoViz (social) | Analyst/Expert | Overview, Filter, Relate |
| **Google Earth** | 2D + 3D + Temporal | GeoViz | Novice | Zoom, Filter, Overview |
| **ParaView** | 3D Volumetric | SciViz | Expert | Zoom, Details-on-Demand, Compare |

*(Justification in 2 lines per system for full marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]**

> **Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the **classification of visualization systems by display medium / immersion level**. Compare desktop 2D, CAVE, and HMD-based immersive systems with one real-world example each. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* With a neat diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a typical visualization system** and explain how the *classification engine* drives the *rendering pipeline*. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Classification by Display Medium (7 marks)

Visualization systems are classified by their **physical rendering surface**, which determines the perceptual cues (depth, parallax, field-of-view) available to the user.

- **Desktop 2D (2 marks):** Standard monitor, mouse/keyboard. *Example:* Tableau on a laptop. Limitation: no true depth perception; relies on shading/perspective.
- **Desktop 3D / Stereo (1.5 marks):** Polarized or active-shutter glasses deliver separate left/right eye images. *Example:* Medical imaging workstations. Adds *stereopsis* but user remains seated.
- **CAVE — Cave Automatic Virtual Environment (2 marks):** A room where 3–6 walls are rear-projected; head-tracking adjusts the perspective. *Example:* University of Illinois' Cube-3 CAVE used for architectural walkthroughs. Full *spatial immersion* with body movement.
- **HMD — Head-Mounted Display (1.5 marks):** Oculus Rift, HTC Vive, Microsoft HoloLens (AR). *Example:* NASA uses Meta Quest HMD for Mars terrain exploration. Highest immersion; supports AR overlays of real environments.

$$\text{Immersion Level} \propto (\text{Field of View}) \times (\text{Depth Cues}) \times (\text{Body Tracking}) $$

#### Part (b) — Functional Architecture (7 marks)

The typical architecture of a classified visualization system consists of **five functional blocks** connected in a closed loop:

1. **Data Ingestion Module (1 mark):** Reads raw data (CSV, HDF5, JSON, SQL).
2. **Classification Engine (2 marks):** Determines the 6-axis classification (D, A, U, T, M, I). This decision drives *all downstream choices*.
3. **Rendering Pipeline (2 marks):** Selects shaders (e.g., WebGL for 2D InfoViz, volume ray-caster for 3D SciViz, VR renderer for HMD).
4. **Display Output (1 mark):** Writes pixels to the chosen medium (monitor, wall, CAVE, HMD).
5. **User Interaction Loop (1 mark):** Captures mouse, touch, gesture, voice; feeds back into the Classification Engine to re-select appropriate view.

```
[ DATA ] → [ CLASSIFICATION ENGINE ] → [ RENDERING PIPELINE ] → [ DISPLAY ]
                ↑                                                            |
                └────────── [ USER INTERACTION ] ←────────────────────────────┘
```

The **Classification Engine is the brain** — it decides *what to render, how to render, and what task the user is currently doing*. Removing it (i.e., a non-classified system) leads to a "one-size-fits-all" tool that performs poorly on heterogeneous data.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing *Data Type* with *Domain*.** "Temporal data" is a *data type*; "SciViz" is a *domain*. A temporal visualization in astronomy is *Temporal Data + SciViz Domain* — these are independent axes. Many students merge them and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Quoting Shneiderman's Mantra without explaining it.** The mantra is worth 1 mark; the *seven tasks with examples* are worth 6 marks. Examiners want application, not memorization.
> 3. **Forgetting to justify the classification.** "Tableau is InfoViz" without saying *why* (abstract business data, novice user) earns only 0.5 of 1 mark.
> 4. **Treating classification as a one-axis concept.** Always use at least 3 axes (Data × Domain × User) for a 7-mark question; the 6-axis model is a complete answer.
> 5. **Spelling "Shneiderman" as "Schneiderman" / "Schniederman"** — these spelling errors are flagged by strict examiners and cost 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Drawing the Mermaid/Diagram without labels.** Diagrams must have *axis names* and *one example per node*. A bare tree earns 1 mark; a labelled tree earns 3.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **One-liner definition:** Visualization systems classify along **6 orthogonal axes** — *Data Type, Domain, User, Task, Display, Interaction*.
- **Most-tested axis:** *Data Type* (1D, 2D, 3D, Multi-D, Temporal, Tree, Network, Text) — appears in 80% of KTU questions.
- **Most-quoted line:** **Shneiderman's Mantra** — *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand."*
- **Two supreme categories:** **SciViz** (physical data) vs **InfoViz** (abstract data) — know the 4-way contrast.
- **Three user classes:** Novice, Analyst, Designer — choose your tool accordingly.
- **Six-axis tuple:** $\mathcal{C} = (D, \mathcal{A}, U, T, M, I)$ — write this in exams for instant full-credit structure.
- **Examples to memorize:** Tableau (InfoViz-Novice), ParaView (SciViz-Expert), Gephi (Network-Analyst), Google Earth (GeoViz-Novice), D3.js (Designer-toolkit), VTK (SciViz-Library).
- **Display progression:** 2D Desktop → 3D Stereo → Wall → CAVE → HMD. Immersion increases along this chain.
- **Frameworks to know (name-only):** Card–Mackinlay–Shneiderman (Data × Task), Tory & Möller (User), Shneiderman (Mantra), Keim (InfoViz + Visual Data Mining).
- **Avoid:** Static "chart-types" lists — KTU 2024 Scheme prefers *taxonomic* answers (axes + classification tuple).
- **Quick-exam answer template (7-mark):**
  1. State the 6 axes (1 mark).
  2. Explain 2 axes in depth with 1 example each (3 marks).
  3. Classify one real system using the 6-tuple (2 marks).
  4. Draw a simple labelled Mermaid tree (1 mark).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Data Visualization and Interpretation (PECPT745)**, the topic of **Interaction and Visualization Techniques** is formally defined as the combined discipline of *human-computer interaction (HCI) principles* and *graphical encoding strategies* that enable a user to *actively explore, query, manipulate, and interpret* complex datasets through visual representations.

According to the **Munzner Visualization Book** framework adopted in modern visualization curricula, this topic is bifurcated into two tightly coupled pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Interaction Techniques:** The set of *low-level user actions* (select, filter, brush, link, zoom, pan, drill) supported by the visualization system to manipulate what is shown and how it is shown.
>
> **Pillar 2 — Visualization Techniques:** The set of *idioms* (visual encodings such as scatterplot, treemap, parallel coordinates, choropleth) used to represent data along its spatial, temporal, hierarchical, network, multidimensional, or textual dimensions.

Mathematically, a visualization can be represented as a mapping function:

$$
V : D \times P \rightarrow G
$$

where $D$ is the dataset, $P$ is the set of interaction parameters (selected, filtered, highlighted), and $G$ is the resulting graphical output rendered on the display.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a visualization tool as a **car dashboard**:

- The **visualization techniques** are the **dials and gauges** themselves — the speedometer, fuel gauge, and tachometer. They are the *static encodings* designed to display specific types of information.
- The **interaction techniques** are the **steering wheel, accelerator, brake, headlights, and zoom button** — the controls that let the driver *interact with the environment*, *change the view*, *focus attention*, and *retrieve deeper information* (e.g., turning on high beams to see further into the dark).

Just as a skilled driver combines dials (encoding) and controls (interaction) to navigate a complex road, a data analyst combines visualization and interaction techniques to navigate a complex dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 2 of PECPT745 specifically emphasizes the **taxonomy of interaction techniques** (per Yi et al., 2007) and the **classification of visualization techniques** (per Munzner's "What-Why-How" framework) along data dimensions: *1D, 2D, 3D, temporal, multidimensional, trees, networks, and text*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

The following quantitative metrics are foundational to evaluating interaction and visualization techniques:

- **Data-Ink Ratio** (Tufte, **1983**) — proportion of ink devoted to actual data versus decorative elements. Target value should approach **1.0**.
- **Number of Distinct Visual Variables** used — Bertin identified **7** (position, size, shape, value, color, orientation, texture).
- **Shneiderman's Mantra** — a 3-step interaction guideline formalized in **1996**.
- **Cleveland-McGill Accuracy Hierarchy** — a **10-tier ranking** of perceptual tasks by human decoding accuracy (1984).

> [!NOTE]
> The standard KTU Board Examiner will look for these exact terminologies in student answers. Misnaming *Bertin's Variables* as "Shneiderman's Variables" is a common 2-mark deduction.
