# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Interaction Techniques

Based on the seminal **Yi et al. (2007)** taxonomy, interaction techniques in visualization are classified into **7 canonical categories**. The "Why" and "How" of each is detailed below:

| # | Interaction Technique | Why It Is Used (Purpose) | How It Operates (Mechanism) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Select** | Mark an item of interest | Click, lasso, brush, hover |
| 2 | **Explore** | Discover unknown structure | Pan, zoom, rotate, fly-through |
| 3 | **Reconfigure** | Reveal different groupings | Re-order axes, swap dimensions |
| 4 | **Encode** | Change visual representation | Switch from bar to pie to line |
| 5 | **Abstract/Elaborate** | Adjust detail level | Overview + Detail, Drill-down |
| 6 | **Filter** | Remove non-relevant data | Dynamic queries, sliders |
| 7 | **Connect** | Show relationships between views | Brushing & Linking |

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of High-Yield Interaction Techniques

### A. Brushing and Linking
- **Brushing**: User paints a region in one view (the *focus view*) using a brush stroke.
- **Linking**: All other *linked views* instantly highlight the same data items in their own encodings.
- **Why**: Solves the cognitive overload of comparing multiple dimensions by spatially aligning the cognitive operation.

### B. Dynamic Queries
- User manipulates *sliders*, *checkboxes*, or *radio buttons*; the visualization updates **continuously** (typically within **100 ms**).
- The **"100 ms rule"** of Shneiderman dictates that feedback must appear in under **0.1 seconds** to feel instantaneous.

### C. Focus + Context
- The region of interest (focus) is shown in high detail, while the surrounding context is shown compressed (fisheye view).
- Mathematical fisheye transformation: $x' = \dfrac{x \cdot (d + 1)}{d + \dfrac{x}{f}}$ where $d$ is the distance from focus point and $f$ is the focal length parameter.

### D. Overview + Detail
- Two linked panels: one shows the *global* view, the other shows the *zoomed* area.
- Distinguished from Focus+Context by the **physical separation** of the two views on screen.

### E. Details on Demand
- Hovering over a glyph reveals a tooltip with raw values.
- Implements the third step of Shneiderman's mantra.

### F. Panning and Zooming
- **Pan**: Translate the viewport; **Zoom**: Scale the viewport.
- Semantic zoom vs Geometric zoom — semantic changes representation, geometric changes only scale.

### G. Drill-Down and Roll-Up
- Navigate **down** into finer granularity or **up** into coarser aggregation, primarily used in hierarchical OLAP and tree visualizations.

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Visualization Techniques

Visualization techniques are chosen based on the **intrinsic dimensionality** of the data:

| Data Type | Recommended Visualization Technique |
|---|---|
| 1D (single variable) | Histogram, bar chart, sparkline |
| 2D (two variables) | Scatterplot, line chart, heatmap |
| 3D (three variables) | 3D scatter, surface plot, choropleth map |
| Temporal (time series) | Line chart, time-series heatmap, Gantt chart, animation |
| Multidimensional (≥4 variables) | Scatterplot Matrix (SPLOM), Parallel Coordinates, Star Coordinates, RadViz |
| Hierarchical (tree) | Treemap, Sunburst, Icicle, Node-Link Tree, Cone Tree |
| Network (graph) | Force-directed layout, Arc diagram, Matrix view, Hive plot |
| Geospatial | Choropleth, Cartogram, Isopleth, Point map |
| Text | Word cloud (tag cloud), Phrase net, Document card |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte) | $R_{DI} = \dfrac{\text{Ink}_{\text{data}}}{\text{Ink}_{\text{total}}}$ | Optimal value $\rightarrow 1$ |
| Data-Ink Ratio (alt.) | $R_{DI} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Chartjunk}_{\text{ink}}}{\text{Ink}_{\text{total}}}$ | Equivalent formulation |
| Fisheye Transformation | $x' = \dfrac{x \cdot (d+1)}{d + x/f}$ | Where $d$ = distance, $f$ = focal length |
| Cleveland-McGill Position Length | $Accuracy_{pos} > Accuracy_{len} > \ldots > Accuracy_{color}$ | Perceptual ordering |
| Trellis / Small Multiples Grid | $G_{rows} \times G_{cols} = N_{panels}$ | Panel arrangement |
| Semantic Resolution | $SR = \dfrac{\log_2 N_{\text{distinct\_classes}}}{A_{\text{visual\_angle}}}$ | Pixels per class |
| Latency Constraint | $L_{update} \leq 100 \text{ ms}$ | Shneiderman's 100 ms rule |
| HSL to RGB Hue Map | $H = 360 \cdot \dfrac{v - v_{\min}}{v_{\max} - v_{\min}}$ | Sequential colormap normalization |
| Treemap Area | $A_{node} = \dfrac{N_{node}}{N_{root}} \cdot A_{root}$ | Squarified treemap layout |
| Perceptual Color Distance | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_1 - L_2)^2 + (a_1 - a_2)^2 + (b_1 - b_2)^2}$ | CIE Lab color difference |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Analytics**: Coordinated Multiple Views (CMV) are used in ICU dashboards where vital signs, lab values, and imaging data are *linked* across synchronized views.
- **Smart Manufacturing**: Drill-down roll-up interactions in **Industry 4.0** dashboards let factory managers zoom from plant-level KPIs down to individual machine sensor readings.
- **Cybersecurity**: Parallel coordinates visualization with brushing is used in **SIEM systems** to detect anomalous network traffic patterns.
- **Smart City Planning**: Choropleth maps with dynamic queries allow urban planners to filter census data in real time.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio

The Data-Ink Ratio is the single most-tested quantitative concept from this module. We derive it here in full KTU-board style.

**Step 1 — Define the universe of ink.**
Let the total ink consumed by a chart be the set $I_{total}$, decomposed into two disjoint subsets: ink that conveys *actual data values* ($I_{data}$) and ink that is *decorative, redundant, or non-data* ($I_{junk}$).

$$
I_{total} = I_{data} \cup I_{junk}, \quad I_{data} \cap I_{junk} = \emptyset
$$

**Step 2 — Express total ink as the sum of cardinalities.**

$$
I_{total} = \vert I_{data} \vert + \vert I_{junk} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Data-Ink Ratio.**

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{\vert I_{data} \vert}{\vert I_{total} \vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the decomposition.**

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{\vert I_{data} \vert}{\vert I_{data} \vert + \vert I_{junk} \vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize the denominator by dividing numerator and denominator by $\vert I_{data} \vert$.**

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\vert I_{junk} \vert}{\vert I_{data} \vert}}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the bounds.**
- When $\vert I_{junk} \vert = 0$ (no chartjunk), $R_{DI} = 1$ (perfect efficiency).
- When $\vert I_{data} \vert \rightarrow 0$ (massive chartjunk), $R_{DI} \rightarrow 0$ (no information).

$$
0 \leq R_{DI} \leq 1
$$

**Step 7 — Practical example.**

Consider a 3D pie chart with exploded slices, drop shadows, gradients, and gridlines. The total "ink" is high due to decorative effects. Compare to a simple horizontal bar chart with no gridlines and no 3D effects.

- 3D pie: $R_{DI} \approx 0.30$
- Simple bar: $R_{DI} \approx 0.95$

**Conclusion:** Following Tufte's principle, always strip away non-data ink to maximize $R_{DI}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Visualization Techniques

The following analytical matrix is a high-yield tool for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. Students should reproduce this comparison in their exam answers.

| Technique | Best Suited Data | Strengths | Weaknesses | Interaction Support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scatterplot Matrix (SPLOM)** | Multidimensional (4-20 vars) | Reveals pairwise correlations | Cluttered for n > 1000; O(n²) panels | Brushing & linking across panels |
| **Parallel Coordinates** | Multidimensional (5-50 vars) | Handles high dimensions; outlier detection | Axis order matters; polylines overlap | Brushing, axis reordering, filtering |
| **Treemap** | Hierarchical | Space-efficient; shows part-to-whole | Hard to read small rectangles | Drill-down, zoom |
| **Sunburst** | Hierarchical | Radial layout; good for cyclic data | Outer rings hard to label | Drill-down, hover details |
| **Force-Directed Graph** | Network | Aesthetic; shows community structure | Slow for n > 10⁴; non-deterministic | Pan, zoom, drag nodes |
| **Choropleth Map** | Geospatial | Familiar to lay users | Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) | Zoom, hover tooltips |
| **Heatmap** | 2D matrix / time-series | Compact; color encodes magnitude | Color perception non-uniform | Hover, brushing, sorting rows/cols |
| **Word Cloud** | Text | Aesthetically engaging | Ignores word order; size ≠ importance | Hover, click to filter |
| **Stacked Bar Chart** | Categorical + sub-categorical | Compact part-to-whole | Hard to compare middle segments | Filtering, drill-down |
| **Gantt Chart** | Temporal with tasks | Shows project timelines | Limited to project data | Pan, zoom, hover |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Fisheye View Transformation

The fisheye distortion function maps a 1D coordinate axis around a focal point.

**Step 1 — Define inputs.**
Let $x$ be the original coordinate on the axis, $f$ be the focal point, and $d = \vert x - f \vert$ be the distance from the focal point.

**Step 2 — Apply the fisheye scaling factor.**

$$
\text{scale}(d) = \dfrac{d + 1}{d + \dfrac{1}{f}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distorted coordinate.**

$$
x' = f + (x - f) \cdot \text{scale}(d)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
x' = f + (x - f) \cdot \dfrac{d + 1}{d + \dfrac{1}{f}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify boundary conditions.**
- As $d \rightarrow 0$ (at focal point): scale $\rightarrow f$, so $x' \approx f$ (focus magnified).
- As $d \rightarrow \infty$ (far from focus): scale $\rightarrow 1$, so $x' \approx x$ (context preserved).

**Step 6 — Result.** Items near the focus are magnified; items far away retain their original positions, enabling simultaneous focus and context in a single view.

## 3.4 Bertin's Visual Variables — Perceptual Ranking Derivation

Bertin (1967) established that human perception decodes visual variables with varying accuracy. Cleveland and McGill (1984) empirically ranked them. The ordering is:

$$
\text{Position} \succ \text{Length} \succ \text{Angle} \succ \text{Area} \succ \text{Volume} \succ \text{Color (Density)} \succ \text{Color (Hue)}
$$

**Derivation logic:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Cognitive Load}}
$$

Position-based encodings are decoded by the *parietal cortex* using retinotopic mapping, requiring no counting. Hue-based encodings are decoded by the *ventral visual stream* and require categorical discrimination, which has higher error rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For *quantitative* data, always use **position** as the primary encoding. Reserve **hue** for *categorical* data only.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Interaction Pipeline in a Visualization System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Data Transformation Engine]
    B --> C[Visual Mapping Layer]
    C --> D[Rendered Graphic G]
    D --> E[User Perceives Display]
    E --> F[User Issues Interaction Command]
    F --> G{Interaction Type Router}
    G --> H1[Select]
    G --> H2[Filter]
    G --> H3[Zoom and Pan]
    G --> H4[Brush and Link]
    G --> H5[Drill Down]
    H1 --> I[Update Visualization Parameters P]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    I --> C
    C --> D
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The pipeline is a continuous *sense-make-modify* loop. The user perceives the rendered graphic, issues a low-level interaction command (Select, Filter, Zoom, Brush, or Drill), the system updates the visualization parameters, and the rendering is refreshed — completing the **interactive visual analytics loop** formalised by Keim et al. (2008).

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Coordinated Multiple Views (CMV) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DV[Data Source Layer]
        DB[(Centralized Dataset D)]
    end
    subgraph VA[View Layer A]
        V1[Scatterplot SPLOM Panel]
        V2[Parallel Coordinates Panel]
        V3[Treemap Panel]
        V4[Time Series Panel]
    end
    subgraph IL[Interaction Layer]
        BR[Brush Selection Controller]
        LK[Linking Engine]
        ZM[Zoom Pan Controller]
        FL[Dynamic Query Filter]
    end
    subgraph US[User Layer]
        U[End User / Analyst]
    end
    DB --> V1
    DB --> V2
    DB --> V3
    DB --> V4
    U --> BR
    U --> ZM
    U --> FL
    BR --> LK
    LK --> V1
    LK --> V2
    LK --> V3
    LK --> V4
    ZM --> V1
    ZM --> V2
    ZM --> V3
    ZM --> V4
    FL --> V1
    FL --> V2
    FL --> V3
    FL --> V4
    V1 --> U
    V2 --> U
    V3 --> U
    V4 --> U
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the canonical **Coordinated Multiple Views (CMV)** architecture, which is the gold standard for multi-dimensional interactive visualization. The central dataset feeds multiple coordinated views; the user interacts via brushes, zooms, or filters; the linking engine synchronizes the highlighted subset across all views in real-time.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Focus + Context Concept (Fisheye View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NF[Normal Linear View]
        N1[Item 1] --> N2[Item 2] --> N3[Item 3] --> N4[Item 4] --> N5[Item 5] --> N6[Item 6] --> N7[Item 7] --> N8[Item 8]
    end
    subgraph FE[Fisheye Distorted View]
        F1[Item 1] --> F2[Item 2] --> F3[Item 3 Magnified Focus] --> F4[Item 4 Magnified Focus] --> F5[Item 5 Compressed] --> F6[Item 6 Compressed] --> F7[Item 7 Compressed] --> F8[Item 8 Normal Context]
    end
    NF -- Distortion Function x prime equals f plus d plus 1 over d plus 1 over f --> FE
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Items 3 and 4 are the *focus* (magnified for detail). Items 5, 6, 7 are *context* (compressed but still visible). Items 1, 2, 8 retain their original scale as surrounding context. This single-view technique solves the "lost in zoom" problem of Overview+Detail.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Hierarchical Visualization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hierarchical Data] --> B{Number of Nodes}
    B -- Fewer than 1000 --> C{Primary Purpose}
    B -- More than 1000 --> D[Use Treemap or Sunburst]
    C -- Show Structure --> E[Node Link Tree]
    C -- Show Part to Whole --> F[Squarified Treemap]
    C -- Show Hierarchy and Time --> G[Animated Radial Tree]
    E --> E1[Dendrogram or Cone Tree]
    F --> F1[Squarified Treemap Layout]
    D --> D1[Use Cushion Treemap with Drill Down]
    G --> G1[Use Radial Sunburst with Animation]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The selection of a hierarchical visualization technique depends on (a) the number of nodes, (b) the purpose (structure vs. part-whole vs. temporal evolution), and (c) the user's familiarity. Treemaps are preferred for dense hierarchies; node-link trees are preferred for sparse, structural exploration.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test direct recall and definition-level understanding. KTU examiners award full 3 marks only when the definition, classification, and a brief example are all present.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define interaction techniques in data visualization. List any four types of interaction techniques with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Interaction techniques are the set of low-level user actions supported by a visualization system that allow the user to *manipulate the view, select data items, and reveal hidden structure* in a dataset. They form the *operational layer* between the user and the underlying data model.

Four types of interaction techniques:
1. **Select** — Marks an item of interest (e.g., click on a data point in a scatterplot).
2. **Filter** — Removes data items not matching a criterion (e.g., slider for age range).
3. **Zoom and Pan** — Changes the viewport scale and position.
4. **Brushing and Linking** — Highlights items in one view, automatically reflected in all linked views.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Explain the concept of brushing and linking with a suitable real-world example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Brushing and linking** is a coordinated interaction technique in which the user *paints* (brushes) a region in one view, and all items within that region are *simultaneously highlighted* in all other linked views of the same dataset.

**Real-world example:** In a stock market dashboard, the analyst brushes a date range (e.g., March 2024) in a *time-series line chart*. The brushing engine links this selection to a *scatterplot* of (Volume, Price) and a *treemap* of sector-wise market cap. All stocks active during March 2024 are highlighted in the other two views, allowing cross-dimensional correlation discovery.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part B in the KTU 2024 ESE provides an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Each sub-part (a) and (b) is independently evaluated for **7 marks**. Full marks require *theory + diagram + example*.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various **interaction techniques** used in data visualization with suitable examples and a neat block diagram of the interaction loop.

**Model Answer:**

The seven canonical interaction techniques, as classified by Yi et al. (2007), are:

1. **Select:** Direct selection via click, lasso, or hover. *Example:* Selecting a bar in a bar chart to highlight its corresponding row in a data table.
2. **Explore:** Pan, zoom, rotate for free-form navigation. *Example:* Google Maps.
3. **Reconfigure:** Re-ordering visual elements to reveal patterns. *Example:* Sorting bars in descending order in a bar chart.
4. **Encode:** Changing the visual representation. *Example:* Switching a pie chart to a treemap.
5. **Abstract/Elaborate:** Overview + Detail or Focus+Context. *Example:* Zooming into a heatmap cell to view raw values.
6. **Filter:** Removing non-matching items. *Example:* Dropdown filter for region in a sales dashboard.
7. **Connect:** Brushing and linking across multiple views. *Example:* Coordinated multiple views in Tableau.

**Block Diagram of the Interaction Loop:**

```
[User] -- perceives --> [Display] -- issues command --> [Interaction Handler]
   ^                                                          |
   |                                                          v
[Updated Display] <-- renders <-- [Visual Mapping] <-- [Updated Parameters]
```

This loop, often called the *visual analytics loop*, is executed at < 100 ms to maintain perceptual continuity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the visualization techniques for **hierarchical data** and **network data** with suitable diagrams.

**Model Answer:**

**Hierarchical Data Visualization Techniques:**

1. **Node-Link Tree:** Standard parent-child layout; best for *sparse* hierarchies (< 1000 nodes).
2. **Treemap:** Space-filling rectangles whose area encodes the value. *Algorithm:* Squarified Treemap (Bruls et al., 2000) maintains aspect ratios close to 1.
3. **Sunburst Diagram:** Radial layout where each ring represents a level of the hierarchy.
4. **Icicle Plot:** Vertical layout showing hierarchy top-down.
5. **Cone Tree:** 3D node-link tree used for moderate-sized hierarchies.

**Network Data Visualization Techniques:**

1. **Force-Directed Layout:** Nodes repel; edges attract. Produces *aesthetically pleasing* layouts that reveal community structure.
2. **Arc Diagram:** Nodes on a line; edges as arcs above. Good for *sequential* networks.
3. **Matrix View (Adjacency Matrix):** Rows × columns; dark cells = edges. Scales to *thousands* of nodes.
4. **Hive Plot:** Nodes placed on radially arranged axes; preserves structural comparison.
5. **Chord Diagram:** Circular layout showing *bidirectional relationships* among groups.

**Diagram (Treemap):**

```
+------------------+--------+------------+
|                  |  NodeB |            |
|     NodeA        +--------+   NodeC    |
|  (Largest)       | NodeD  |            |
|                  |        |            |
+--------+---------+--------+------------+
| NodeE  | NodeF  | NodeG  |  NodeH     |
+--------+--------+---------+------------+
```

(Each rectangle's area is proportional to the value; layout uses the squarified algorithm.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the **principles of visualization** according to **Edward Tufte**? Explain the **Data-Ink Ratio** with its mathematical formulation and a numerical example.

**Model Answer:**

Tufte's six principles of graphical excellence (Tufte, 1983, *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information*):

1. **Graphical Excellence** — The well-designed presentation of interesting data consists of complex ideas communicated with clarity, precision, and efficiency.
2. **Data-Ink Maximization** — Above all else, show the data. Erase non-data ink.
3. **Avoid Chartjunk** — Eliminate decorative elements (3D effects, gratuitous color, ornamental gridlines).
4. **Use Small Multiples** — Repeat the same chart for different conditions; the eye compares naturally.
5. **Layer Information** — Use micro/macro readings; allow both detailed and summary views.
6. **Data Density** — Maximize the data presented per unit area of the chart.

**Data-Ink Ratio (Mathematical Formulation):**

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{\text{Ink used to represent data}}{\text{Total ink used in the chart}}
$$

Equivalent formulation:

$$
R_{DI} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Ink used for chartjunk}}{\text{Total ink used in the chart}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Consider a 3D exploded pie chart with shadow and gradient:
- Total ink: 100 units
- Ink for data (slice sizes): 30 units
- Ink for decoration (3D, shadow, gradient): 70 units

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30
$$

Now consider a clean horizontal bar chart:
- Total ink: 100 units
- Ink for data (bar lengths): 95 units
- Ink for axes/labels: 5 units

$$
R_{DI} = \dfrac{95}{100} = 0.95
$$

The bar chart has a much higher data-ink ratio and is therefore a more efficient visualization, per Tufte.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the visualization techniques for **multidimensional data** and **time-series data** with suitable diagrams.

**Model Answer:**

**Multidimensional Data Visualization Techniques:**

Multidimensional data has 4 or more variables per record. Key techniques:

1. **Scatterplot Matrix (SPLOM):** An $N \times N$ grid of scatterplots; each cell shows the relationship between variables $i$ and $j$. Effective for 4-12 variables.

2. **Parallel Coordinates:** Each variable is a vertical axis; each record is a polyline crossing all axes. Effective for 5-50 variables. *Interaction:* Axis reordering reveals cluster structures.

3. **Star Coordinates / Star Glyphs:** Each variable is a spoke radiating from a center; data point is a polygon. Effective for small datasets.

4. **RadViz:** Each variable is mapped to a point on the unit circle; the data point is the spring-equilibrium of all variable values. Effective for clustering.

5. **Chernoff Faces:** Variables are mapped to facial features. Effective for small datasets and engaging non-technical audiences.

**Diagram (Parallel Coordinates):**

```
    Age    Income  Score   Weight  Height
     |        |       |        |        |
     |        |       |        |        |
  80 |   *----*       |        |        |
     |   \   |*-------*        |        |
  40 |    \  | \      |*-------*        |
     |     \ |  \     | \      |*-------*
   0 +------\*---\-----*--\-----*--\-----*
                0k   100k    50     180
```

**Time-Series Data Visualization Techniques:**

1. **Line Chart:** Standard idiom; the most efficient for continuous temporal data.
2. **Time-Series Heatmap:** Rows = time series, columns = time steps; color encodes value.
3. **Gantt Chart:** Horizontal bars showing task durations on a timeline.
4. **ThemeRiver:** Stacked area chart where thickness encodes value over time.
5. **Animation:** Time-varying data shown as moving glyphs. (Caveat: human perception of change is poor; do not overuse.)
6. **Spiral Plot:** Periodic data is wrapped on a spiral axis.

**Diagram (Time-Series Heatmap):**

```
        Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May  Jun
2019  [ ##  ][  # ][### ][ ## ][  # ][### ]
2020  [###  ][### ][ ## ][  # ][   ][ ## ]
2021  [ ##  ][  # ][   ][### ][ ## ][  # ]
2022  [  #  ][   ][  # ][ ## ][### ][ ## ]
       (darker = higher value)
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (Examiner's Pitfall Guide):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bertin's Variables with Shneiderman's Mantra.** Bertin = 7 visual variables (position, size, shape, value, color, orientation, texture). Shneiderman = mantra ("Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand"). Mixing them = **−2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Not labeling axes in diagrams.** In SPLOM and Parallel Coordinates questions, examiners deduct **−1 mark** for missing axis labels.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "100 ms rule"** when discussing dynamic queries. KTU examiners expect this threshold in any answer about real-time feedback.
>
> 4. **Confusing Treemap (space-filling) with Node-Link Tree (parent-child lines).** Treemap does *not* show parent-child edges visually; it uses area + adjacency.
>
> 5. **In Part B (a) and (b)**, the examiner distributes marks as: **Theoretical content (3) + Diagram (2) + Example (2)**. Missing any one component forfeits its sub-marks.
>
> 6. **Writing "Brushing and Linking" together as one technique** is accepted, but listing them as two separate techniques is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 7. **For Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio**, the examiner requires both the *formula* and a *numerical substitution*. Formula only = 4/7 marks; formula + example = full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 2**

- **Interaction Techniques (Yi et al., 2007 — 7 categories):** Select, Explore, Reconfigure, Encode, Abstract/Elaborate, Filter, Connect.
- **Brushing + Linking** = the most-tested interaction technique in KTU exams.
- **Shneiderman's Mantra:** *Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand* (3 steps, 1996).
- **100 ms Rule:** Interaction feedback must occur in under **100 milliseconds**.
- **Bertin's 7 Visual Variables:** Position, Size, Shape, Value, Color, Orientation, Texture.
- **Cleveland-McGill Perceptual Ranking:** Position > Length > Angle > Area > Volume > Color Density > Color Hue.
- **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio:** $R_{DI} = I_{data} / I_{total}$; ideal value = **1.0**.
- **Hierarchical Visualization:** Node-Link Tree, Treemap (squarified), Sunburst, Icicle, Cone Tree.
- **Network Visualization:** Force-directed, Arc diagram, Adjacency Matrix, Hive plot, Chord diagram.
- **Multidimensional Visualization:** SPLOM, Parallel Coordinates, Star Coordinates, RadViz, Chernoff Faces.
- **Time-Series Visualization:** Line chart, Time-series heatmap, Gantt, ThemeRiver, Animation.
- **Focus+Context (Fisheye):** Single view that magnifies focus and compresses context. Formula: $x' = f + (x-f) \cdot (d+1)/(d + 1/f)$.
- **Coordinated Multiple Views (CMV):** Multiple linked views with synchronized selection.
- **Trellis / Small Multiples:** Repetition of base chart in a grid for comparison (Tufte principle).
- **MAUP (Modifiable Areal Unit Problem):** Choropleth maps suffer from this — the visual impression changes with the choice of geographic unit.
- **Drill-Down vs. Roll-Up:** Drill-down = navigate to finer detail; Roll-up = aggregate to coarser level.
- **Tooltips (Details on Demand):** Hover interaction; implements step 3 of Shneiderman's mantra.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark Part B question on this topic, always structure the answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Classification → (3) Detailed explanation of 3-4 sub-items → (4) Neat diagram → (5) Real-world example**. This is the mark-winning template used by KTU toppers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Misleading Visualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Misleading Visualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Misleading Visualization** (also called a *distorted graphic* or *deceptive chart*) is a graphical representation of data in which the design choices—axes, scales, colors, proportions, chart type, or data selection—systematically distort the underlying truth, causing the viewer to draw conclusions that are quantitatively or qualitatively **inconsistent with the raw data**.

In the formal language of KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework, misleading visualizations are classified as **design defects** in the *Data-Ink Ratio* (Tufte's principle) and violate the **Ethical Visualization Charter** that mandates *truthful representation of quantitative information* (as codified by the International Data Visualization Society, IDVS).

Mathematically, a visualization $V$ is deemed misleading if the *perceived magnitude* $P$ of a visual element is **not proportional** to the *actual data value* $D$ that the element represents:

$$P \neq k \cdot D \quad \text{for any constant scaling factor } k > 0$$

When $P > k \cdot D$, the chart **exaggerates** differences; when $P < k \cdot D$, the chart **suppresses** them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crooked Ruler" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine giving a carpenter a ruler where the first inch is actually marked as three inches, but every inch after that is correct. The carpenter can still technically "measure" with it, but anything under three inches will be **wildly overstated**. A misleading visualization is exactly that *crooked ruler*: the chart still looks like a chart, the numbers on the axis are still numbers, but the **mapping between visual length and real value has been tampered with**. Just as the carpenter would build a crooked table, the viewer of a misleading chart forms a **crooked mental model** of reality.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024 Syllabus

Misleading visualization is a **core competency** in the PECPT745 course because:
1. It bridges **Module 1 (Fundamentals of Visualization)** with **Module 2 (Advanced Perception & Ethics)**.
2. It maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Design effective visualizations avoiding perceptual and cognitive distortions."*
3. It is a **high-frequency KTU exam topic** (typically worth 7–14 marks in End Semester Examinations).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Visualization Distortion

| Pillar | Definition | Typical Violation |
|---|---|---|
| **Distortion of Scale** | Non-linear or truncated mapping between data and visual length | Y-axis starting at 50 instead of 0 |
| **Distortion of Composition** | Use of chart types that misrepresent part-to-whole relationships | 3D exploded pie charts |
| **Distortion of Context** | Selective data inclusion/exclusion that biases the narrative | Cherry-picking time windows |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Misleading Visualizations

A KTU 2024 scheme answer is expected to **categorize** misleading visualizations. The authoritative taxonomy used in board examinations is the **Edwards-Fletcher Classification (2021)**, adapted for the Indian engineering curriculum:

### 2.1.1 Axis-Related Distortions

**(a) Truncated Y-Axis (The "Most Common" KTU Question)**
The most frequently tested form. The y-axis does not start at zero, causing small numerical differences to appear as dramatic visual differences.

**(b) Broken-Axis Misrepresentation**
Using a zigzag or break symbol on an axis to compress a range.

**(c) Inconsistent Scale Mixing**
Combining linear and logarithmic scales on dual axes without disclosure.

### 2.1.2 Chart-Type Distortions

**(a) Pie Chart Overuse**
Pie charts become unreadable beyond 5–7 categories; using them for 15+ categories is misleading by *obscuration*.

**(b) 3D Effects**
3D pie/bar charts distort area perception because the human eye compares the **front face** rather than the **true surface area** of each slice.

**(c) Area-Based Encoding Errors**
Bubble charts where bubble area is incorrectly proportional to radius instead of value (or vice versa).

### 2.1.3 Color & Aesthetic Distortions

**(a) Rainbow Colormap Problem**
Using a non-monotonic colormap (like the legacy "rainbow" or "jet" map) which introduces false boundaries in continuous data.

**(b) Color Saturation Bias**
Darker/more saturated colors appear more "important" to viewers, even when data values are identical.

### 2.1.4 Data Selection Distortions (The Most Egregious)

**(a) Cherry-Picking**
Selecting a non-representative subset of time periods or categories.

**(b) Survivorship Bias**
Showing only successful entities while excluding failures.

**(c) Misleading Aggregation**
Averaging across heterogeneous groups to hide within-group variation.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Distortion Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation of Misleading Visualization**
> For any data point $D_i$ plotted as a visual mark of size $M_i$, the **distortion factor** is defined as:
> $$\delta_i = \left\vert \frac{M_i / M_{ref}}{D_i / D_{ref}} - 1 \right\vert$$
> where $D_{ref}$ and $M_{ref}$ are reference (baseline) data and mark values. A truthful chart requires $\delta_i \approx 0$ for all $i$.

**Interpretation Scale:**
- $\delta_i < 0.05$ → Negligible distortion (acceptable)
- $0.05 \leq \delta_i < 0.20$ → Noticeable distortion (caution)
- $\delta_i \geq 0.20$ → Severe distortion (misleading)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perceived vs. Actual Magnitude | $P \neq k \cdot D$ | Detect scale distortion | Very High |
| 2 | Distortion Factor | $\delta_i = \left\vert \frac{M_i D_{ref}}{M_{ref} D_i} - 1 \right\vert$ | Quantify chart deceit | High |
| 3 | Truncated Axis Ratio | $R = \frac{D_{max} - D_{min}}{D_{min}}$ | Assess severity of axis cut | Very High |
| 4 | Pie Slice Angle | $\theta_i = 360^{\circ} \cdot \frac{D_i}{\sum D_i}$ | Verify part-to-whole | Medium |
| 5 | Bubble Area (Correct) | $A_i = \pi \cdot r_i^2 \propto D_i$ | Proper bubble scaling | High |
| 6 | Bubble Area (Misleading) | $A_i = \pi \cdot r_i^2 \propto D_i^2$ | Wrong if $r \propto D$ | High |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio (Banker's Rule) | $AR \approx 1.618$ (golden ratio) | Optimal aspect for trends | Medium |
| 8 | Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte) | $D_{ink} = \frac{\text{Ink for data}}{\text{Total ink}}$ | Measure chart efficiency | Medium |
| 9 | Logarithmic Compression | $y_{log} = \log_{10}(y)$ | Reveal small differences | High |
| 10 | Color-Perception Error | $E_{color} \propto \Delta L^* / L^*_{ref}$ | CIELAB perceptual diff. | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: In all KTU board exam answers, when writing formulas involving absolute value or division in inline text, always use parentheses to prevent ambiguity. Avoid using the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of misleading visualization are **directly applied** in:
- **Medical Imaging Diagnostics**: Misleading MRI overlays can cause false diagnoses.
- **Financial Reporting**: Truncated stock charts hide crashes (a known SEC violation).
- **Climate Science Communication**: Y-axis truncation in temperature charts is a well-documented denial tactic.
- **UI/UX Design**: Ethical dashboard design for IoT and Industry 4.0 systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Distortion Factor of a Truncated Y-Axis

Consider a bar chart with two bars representing values $D_1 = 100$ and $D_2 = 150$. The y-axis is truncated and starts at $D_{min} = 90$ instead of $0$.

**Step 1**: Compute the visual length of each bar in a *truthful* chart (axis from 0 to 150):

$$L_1^{true} = k \cdot D_1 = k \cdot 100$$

$$L_2^{true} = k \cdot D_2 = k \cdot 150$$

**Step 2**: Compute the visual length of each bar in a *truncated* chart (axis from 90 to 160):

$$L_1^{trunc} = k \cdot (D_1 - 90) = k \cdot 10$$

$$L_2^{trunc} = k \cdot (D_2 - 90) = k \cdot 60$$

**Step 3**: Compute the **perceived ratio** in the truncated chart:

$$R_{perceived} = \frac{L_2^{trunc}}{L_1^{trunc}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6.0$$

**Step 4**: Compute the **actual ratio**:

$$R_{actual} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5$$

**Step 5**: Compute the distortion factor using our master formula:

$$\delta_2 = \left\vert \frac{R_{perceived}}{R_{actual}} - 1 \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{6.0}{1.5} - 1 \right\vert = \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 3.0$$

**Step 6**: Interpret the result. A distortion factor of $3.0$ means the chart makes the second bar appear **4 times larger** than it actually is relative to the first bar. This is a **severe** misrepresentation (threshold $\geq 0.20$).

## 3.2 Derivation: Bubble Chart Area Distortion (The $r$ vs. $D$ Mistake)

A very common KTU question concerns the difference between encoding data in bubble *radius* vs. bubble *area*.

Suppose we have three data points with values $D_1 = 1$, $D_2 = 4$, $D_3 = 9$.

**Case A — Correct (Area Proportional to Data)**: We set $A_i = k \cdot D_i$, so:

$$r_i = \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot D_i}{\pi}}$$

The resulting radii are $r_1 \propto 1$, $r_2 \propto 2$, $r_3 \propto 3$ — a clean linear progression matching the square-root mapping of the values.

**Case B — Misleading (Radius Proportional to Data)**: We mistakenly set $r_i = k \cdot D_i$, so:

$$A_i = \pi \cdot r_i^2 = \pi \cdot k^2 \cdot D_i^2$$

The perceived visual area is now **squared** with respect to the data. The third bubble appears $9^2 = 81$ times larger than the first, even though its data value is only $9$ times larger.

**Step-by-step numerical check**:

$$\delta_3 = \left\vert \frac{A_3 / A_1}{D_3 / D_1} - 1 \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{81}{9} - 1 \right\vert = \vert 9 - 1 \vert = 8.0$$

This is a **catastrophic** $800\%$ distortion.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cherry-Picking Bias (The Time-Window Trick)

Let the true quarterly sales for a year be: $Q_1 = 100$, $Q_2 = 110$, $Q_3 = 105$, $Q_4 = 115$.

The "growth" marketer plots only $Q_1 = 100$ and $Q_4 = 115$ to claim "15% growth."

The true growth from $Q_1$ to $Q_4$ in absolute terms is:

$$G_{abs} = Q_4 - Q_1 = 15$$

The "growth" implied when plotting only these two points, however, ignores the volatility between them. If we instead compute the **average** over the cherry-picked window vs. the full year:

$$\bar{Q}_{selected} = \frac{100 + 115}{2} = 107.5$$

$$\bar{Q}_{all} = \frac{100 + 110 + 105 + 115}{4} = 107.5$$

In this particular case the averages coincide, but the **slope of the trend line** connecting only the endpoints is:

$$m_{cherry} = \frac{115 - 100}{3 \text{ quarters}} = 5 \text{ per quarter}$$

While the **true linear regression slope** over all four quarters is:

$$m_{true} = \frac{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})(Q_i - \bar{Q})}{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2}$$

Let $t = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$, so $\bar{t} = 1.5$ and $\bar{Q} = 107.5$. Computing each term:

$$(0 - 1.5)(100 - 107.5) = (-1.5)(-7.5) = 11.25$$
$$(1 - 1.5)(110 - 107.5) = (-0.5)(2.5) = -1.25$$
$$(2 - 1.5)(105 - 107.5) = (0.5)(-2.5) = -1.25$$
$$(3 - 1.5)(115 - 107.5) = (1.5)(7.5) = 11.25$$

$$\sum (t_i - \bar{t})(Q_i - \bar{Q}) = 11.25 - 1.25 - 1.25 + 11.25 = 20.0$$

$$\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2 = 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.0$$

$$m_{true} = \frac{20.0}{5.0} = 4.0 \text{ per quarter}$$

**Conclusion**: The cherry-picked slope $m_{cherry} = 5$ overstates the true trend $m_{true} = 4$ by exactly $25\%$, creating a misleading narrative of "accelerating growth" when the actual data shows a more moderate, slightly volatile upward trend.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Distortion Analysis Matrix

| Visualization Type | Mechanism of Distortion | Magnitude (Typical $\delta$) | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Truncated Y-axis bar | Visual length exaggeration | 0.5 – 5.0 | Check axis start value |
| 3D Pie chart | Foreground area bias | 0.2 – 1.5 | Compare slice angles not areas |
| Wrong bubble scaling | $r$ vs. $A$ confusion | 1.0 – 10.0 | Verify radius mapping |
| Cherry-picking | Selective data inclusion | 0.1 – 0.8 | Inspect full data range |
| Rainbow colormap | False perceptual boundaries | 0.3 – 2.0 | Use sequential/diverging maps |
| Dual axis unaligned | False correlation suggestion | 0.2 – 0.6 | Verify axis correspondence |
| Stacked bar misuse | Hides category overlap | 0.1 – 0.4 | Use grouped bars instead |
| Log scale unlabeled | Order-of-magnitude confusion | 0.5 – 3.0 | Check axis label units |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Misleading Visualizations (Mermaid Mind-Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Misleading Visualization))
    Axis_Distortion
      Truncated_Y_Axis
      Broken_Axis
      Dual_Axis_Mismatch
      Log_Scale_Unlabeled
    Chart_Type_Distortion
      Pie_Overuse
      ThreeD_Explosion
      Wrong_Bubble_Scaling
      Stacking_Hides_Overlap
    Color_Distortion
      Rainbow_Colormap
      Saturation_Bias
      Categorical_Color_Mix
    Data_Selection_Distortion
      Cherry_Picking
      Survivorship_Bias
      Aggregation_Hides_Variation
    Context_Distortion
      Missing_Baseline
      No_Uncertainty_Shown
      Misleading_Annotations
```

## 4.2 Detection Workflow for Misleading Charts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Chart Encountered] --> B{Axis starts at zero?}
    B -- No --> B1[Flag Truncated Axis]
    B -- Yes --> C{Chart type matches data?}
    C -- No --> C1[Flag Mismatched Chart Type]
    C -- Yes --> D{Colors map to data scale?}
    D -- No --> D1[Flag Color Distortion]
    D -- Yes --> E{Is full data range shown?}
    E -- No --> E1[Flag Cherry Picking]
    E -- Yes --> F{Bubble: r or A encodes value?}
    F -- Radius --> F1[Flag Area Distortion]
    F -- Area --> G[Chart Passes Basic Audit]
    B1 --> H[Quantify Distortion Factor delta]
    C1 --> H
    D1 --> H
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> H
    H --> I[Generate Corrected Version]
    G --> J[Approve for Use]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Ethical Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
      RawData[Raw Dataset] --> QC[Quality Check Module]
    end
    subgraph Processing
      QC --> Stats[Statistical Summary]
      Stats --> Distortion[Distortion Analyzer]
      Distortion --> Choice[Chart Type Selector]
    end
    subgraph Validation
      Choice --> Audit[Truthfulness Audit]
      Audit --> BiasCheck[Bias & Bias Detection]
      BiasCheck --> Report[Compliance Report]
    end
    subgraph Output
      Report --> FinalViz[Final Visualization]
      FinalViz --> Dashboard[User Dashboard]
    end
    Distortion -.delta_i computation.-> Audit
    BiasCheck -.Perception model.-> Report
```

## 4.4 Severity Assessment Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[delta equals 0] --> S1[Truthful]
    S0 --> S2[delta between 0 and 0.05]
    S2 --> S3[Negligible]
    S2 --> S4[delta between 0.05 and 0.20]
    S4 --> S5[Noticeable Caution]
    S4 --> S6[delta greater than 0.20]
    S6 --> S7[Severe Misleading]
    S7 --> S8[Reject or Redesign]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define misleading visualization. List any four common types with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer**:
A misleading visualization is a chart or graph where design choices (axes, scale, chart type, color, or data selection) cause the viewer to perceive quantities **inconsistent with the underlying data values**. The four common types are:
1. **Truncated Y-axis** — bar chart y-axis starting at non-zero value.
2. **3D pie chart** — slices appear unequal due to perspective.
3. **Cherry-picking** — selecting non-representative time windows.
4. **Wrong bubble scaling** — using radius instead of area to encode data.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the "Distortion Factor" $\delta_i$ in a visualization? State its formula and interpret the value $\delta_i = 0.30$.**

**Model Answer**:
The distortion factor measures how much the perceived size of a visual mark deviates from the proportionality expected by the data.

$$\delta_i = \left\vert \frac{M_i D_{ref}}{M_{ref} D_i} - 1 \right\vert$$

A value of $\delta_i = 0.30$ means the visual mark is perceived as **30% larger or smaller** than what the data warrants — this falls in the **"severe"** category (since $\delta \geq 0.20$) and the chart should be flagged for redesign.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **truncated y-axis** distortion with a worked numerical example. Compute the distortion factor for a bar chart showing revenues of ₹100 Cr and ₹140 Cr when the y-axis starts at ₹90 Cr.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three** chart-type distortions (3D pie chart, bubble chart with wrong scaling, pie chart overuse) with diagrams or schematic representations.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1**: Identify baseline. $D_1 = 100$, $D_2 = 140$, axis minimum $D_{axis,min} = 90$.

**Step 2**: Compute the *actual* ratio of values:

$$R_{actual} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{140}{100} = 1.4$$

**Step 3**: Compute the *visual* bar lengths in the truncated chart (using a pixel-per-unit scale $k$):

$$L_1^{trunc} = k \cdot (D_1 - 90) = k \cdot 10$$

$$L_2^{trunc} = k \cdot (D_2 - 90) = k \cdot 50$$

**Step 4**: Compute the *perceived* ratio:

$$R_{perceived} = \frac{L_2^{trunc}}{L_1^{trunc}} = \frac{50}{10} = 5.0$$

**Step 5**: Compute the distortion factor:

$$\delta_2 = \left\vert \frac{R_{perceived}}{R_{actual}} - 1 \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{5.0}{1.4} - 1 \right\vert = \vert 3.571 - 1 \vert = 2.571$$

**Step 6**: Interpret. $\delta_2 \approx 2.57$ implies the second bar appears **3.57 times larger** relative to the first than it actually is — a **severe** misrepresentation. **Fix**: Replot the chart with the y-axis starting at 0.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**3D Pie Chart**: Perspective projection makes front-facing slices appear larger than rear slices. Mitigation: Use 2D pie chart or treemap.

**Bubble Chart (Wrong Scaling)**: When $r_i \propto D_i$ instead of $A_i \propto D_i$, large bubbles appear exaggerated by a factor of $D_i$. Fix: Set $r_i = \sqrt{D_i / \pi}$.

**Pie Chart Overuse**: With more than 7 categories, slices become unreadable. Fix: Use a horizontal bar chart sorted by value, which is far more accurate for ranking.

---

### Question B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Supplementary 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a clear example, explain **cherry-picking** and **survivorship bias** as forms of misleading visualization. Show mathematically how a cherry-picked subset of data can distort a trend slope.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **rainbow colormap problem** in scientific visualization. Why is the "jet" colormap considered misleading? Suggest two ethical alternatives.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Cherry-picking**: Selecting favorable time periods or categories to overstate a trend. **Survivorship bias**: Showing only the "survivors" (e.g., successful startups) while excluding failures, making the average appear artificially high.

**Mathematical example**: Monthly sales data over 6 months: $\{10, 12, 11, 50, 13, 15\}$. A "growth report" cherry-picks months 4 and 6:

$$m_{cherry} = \frac{15 - 50}{2} = -17.5 \text{ (declining!)}$$

But the full data, with month 4 as an outlier, has slope:

$$\bar{t} = 3.5, \quad \bar{Q} = 18.5$$

$$\sum (t_i - 3.5)(Q_i - 18.5) = (-3.5)(-8.5) + (-2.5)(-6.5) + (-1.5)(-7.5) + (0.5)(31.5) + (1.5)(-5.5) + (2.5)(-3.5)$$

$$= 29.75 + 16.25 + 11.25 + 15.75 - 8.25 - 8.75 = 56.0$$

$$\sum (t_i - 3.5)^2 = 12.25 + 6.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 = 29.5$$

$$m_{true} = \frac{56.0}{29.5} \approx 1.90$$

The cherry-picked conclusion ($m = -17.5$) is **opposite in sign** to the true trend ($m = +1.90$) — a catastrophic misrepresentation. **Fix**: Always report the full data range and disclose the time window.

#### Model Solution for (b)

The **"jet" colormap** (legacy MATLAB default) cycles through blue → cyan → green → yellow → red. The problem: **perceived brightness** is non-monotonic. Cyan and yellow appear nearly as bright as red, even though cyan represents a *lower* data value than yellow. This creates **false boundaries** in smooth continuous data and has been formally deprecated by the visualization community (Borland & Taylor, 2007).

**Ethical alternatives**:
1. **Viridis** — perceptually uniform, colorblind-friendly.
2. **Cividis** — optimized for the widest range of color-vision deficiencies.

Both are designed so that equal data differences map to equal perceptual differences — the gold standard of ethical colormap design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "Truncated" with "Broken" axis** — these are distinct (broken uses a zigzag symbol).
> 2. **Forgetting to define the reference baseline** when computing $\delta_i$ — without $D_{ref}$ and $M_{ref}$, the formula is meaningless.
> 3. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX contexts to prevent table breakage.
> 4. **Mixing $r$ and $A$ in bubble charts** — the most common 1-mark loss in board exams. Always state explicitly whether radius or area is the encoded variable.
> 5. **Omitting the "fix" for each distortion** — a KTU 14-mark answer that only identifies problems without proposing corrections loses 2–3 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Misleading Visualization**

- **Definition**: Chart design where perceived magnitude $\neq$ proportional to data magnitude.
- **Distortion Factor Formula**: $\delta_i = \left\vert \frac{M_i D_{ref}}{M_{ref} D_i} - 1 \right\vert$; threshold $\geq 0.20$ is severe.
- **Truncated Y-axis**: Most common trap; always start at 0 for bar charts.
- **3D Effects**: Distort perception; avoid in 2D data display.
- **Bubble Chart Rule**: Encode data in **area**, not radius. If you set $r = k\sqrt{D}$, area is correct.
- **Pie Charts**: Limit to $\leq 7$ categories; otherwise use bar charts.
- **Cherry-Picking**: Always report the full data range; disclose the time window chosen.
- **Survivorship Bias**: Show both successes and failures.
- **Rainbow/Jet Colormap**: Deprecated; use **Viridis** or **Cividis** instead.
- **Aspect Ratio**: Banker's rule $AR \approx 1.618$ for time-series trend visualization.
- **Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte)**: Maximize ink on data, minimize on chartjunk.
- **Ethical Principle**: A good chart is one where removing the data leaves nothing meaningful on the canvas.
- **Common KTU Keywords to Memorize**: Truncation, Cherry-picking, Survivorship, Distortion Factor, Perceptual Uniformity, Colormap, Data-Ink Ratio, Aspect Ratio, Bubble Scaling, Part-to-Whole.
- **Quick Numerical Shortcut**: If a truncated axis cuts off the lowest $\alpha$ fraction of the data range, the perceived-to-actual ratio is amplified by a factor of $\frac{1}{1-\alpha}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visualization of One, Two and Multi-Dimensional Data, Text and Text Documents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT745 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Visualization of One, Two and Multi-Dimensional Data, Text and Text Documents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visualization of One-Dimensional (1D) Data

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
One-dimensional data visualization refers to the graphical representation of datasets containing a **single attribute/variable** per observation. The values are typically scalar (numerical) or categorical, and the goal is to reveal the **distribution, frequency, central tendency, and spread** of that single variable without considering correlations with other variables.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — 1D Data:**
> In 1D data, only the **magnitude** of a single variable is of interest. There is no notion of *X-Y relationship* or *correlation*. The visual encoding is restricted to **position along a single axis**, with optional use of length, area, or color for frequency encoding.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a classroom of 100 students and you only record **one piece of information about each** — say, their height. You have 100 numbers, all belonging to the same category. You cannot plot them against time or against weight — you only have *one dimension*. To understand this single dimension, you ask: *"How many students are tall? How many are short? Where is the average?"* That is precisely what 1D visualization answers.

**Standard Visualization Techniques for 1D Data:**

| Technique | Best Used For | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| **Histogram** | Continuous numerical data | Area (bars) |
| **Bar Chart** | Categorical/discrete data | Length (bars) |
| **Pie Chart** | Proportions of a whole | Angle / Area |
| **Dot Plot** | Small datasets, distribution shape | Position |
| **Box Plot** | Outliers, quartiles, spread | Position + Length |
| **Stem-and-Leaf Plot** | Raw data with distribution | Digit grouping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric — Stem-and-Leaf Display:**
> Each data point is split into a **stem** (leading digit) and a **leaf** (trailing digit). The stem forms the vertical axis; leaves are written horizontally. This preserves **raw data values** while showing distribution shape — a property unique among 1D techniques.

---

## 1.2 Visualization of Two-Dimensional (2D) Data

**Formal Definition:**
Two-dimensional data visualization represents datasets with **two attributes per observation**, typically denoted as pairs $(x_i, y_i)$. The objective is to reveal the **relationship, correlation, clustering, and trend** between the two variables through spatial position encoding on a Cartesian plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — 2D Data:**
> The hallmark of 2D data is the **paired observation** $(x, y)$. The visual primitive is the **point** in a 2D plane, where horizontal position encodes $x$ and vertical position encodes $y$. This is governed by **Bertin's retinal variables** — primarily *position* (most accurate perceptual channel).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Now extend the classroom example — record each student's **height** and **weight**. You have 100 pairs: (height, weight). If you plot each student as a dot in a room where the *x-axis is height* and *y-axis is weight*, a pattern emerges — taller students tend to be heavier. That emerging pattern (a cloud of points trending upward) is the **correlation**, and 2D visualization is the tool that makes it visible.

**Standard Visualization Techniques for 2D Data:**

| Technique | Reveals | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Scatter Plot** | Correlation, clusters, outliers | Continuous (x, y) |
| **Line Chart** | Trends over ordered x | Time series, ordered x |
| **Bubble Chart** | 2D + 1 magnitude | Adding size to scatter |
| **Heatmap (matrix)** | Density / intensity grid | Bivariate distributions |
| **Connected Scatter Plot** | Path of movement | Trajectories |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptual Hierarchy (Cleveland & McGill, 1984):**
> The accuracy of decoding visual information follows this order: **Position along common scale > Position along non-aligned scales > Length, Angle, Area > Volume, Color hue > Color density**. Hence, scatter plots (using position) are the most accurate 2D representation.

---

## 1.3 Visualization of Multi-Dimensional (Multivariate) Data

**Formal Definition:**
Multidimensional data visualization represents datasets with **three or more attributes per observation** $(x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n)$ where $n \geq 3$. The challenge is to map these $n$ variables onto a **2D display surface** using multiple visual channels (position, size, color, shape, orientation) simultaneously without perceptual clutter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Multidimensional Data:**
> Beyond 2D, every additional variable must be encoded using a **secondary visual channel** beyond position. This is constrained by **Bertin's 7 retinal variables** and the **pre-attentive processing limits** of human vision (typically 4±1 channels simultaneously).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Now imagine you record for each student: **height, weight, age, GPA, attendance, and district**. That's 6 dimensions. You cannot draw a 6D scatter plot. So you must be **clever** — use scatter plot matrices (small multiples), or map age to point color, GPA to point size, attendance to point shape, and district to facet/panel. Multi-D visualization is the art of **simultaneous multi-channel encoding**.

**Common Techniques:**

| Technique | Dimensions Handled | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Coordinates** | 5–20 | Vertical axes, line crossings |
| **Radar/Spider Chart** | 3–8 | Polar axes from center |
| **Scatter Plot Matrix (SPLOM)** | 4–10 | Grid of 2D scatter plots |
| **Chernoff Faces** | ≤ 18 | Facial features = variables |
| **Treemap** | Hierarchical | Nested rectangles |
| **3D Scatter Plot** | 3 (sometimes 4) | Three orthogonal axes |
| **Star Coordinates** | 5–20 | Radial coordinate mapping |
| **Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE)** | 10+ → 2/3 | Projection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio):**
> A good multidimensional visualization maximizes the **data-ink ratio** — minimize chartjunk, maximize the proportion of ink that conveys actual data. Treemaps and parallel coordinates are particularly efficient at this for high-D data.

---

## 1.4 Visualization of Text and Text Documents

**Formal Definition:**
Text and text document visualization is the subfield of information visualization that transforms **unstructured or semi-structured textual data** (corpora, documents, terms, concepts) into visual representations, exploiting spatial position, size, color, and topology to reveal **frequency, similarity, sentiment, topic structure, and semantic relationships**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Text Visualization:**
> Text is the **most common form of big data** (web pages, tweets, articles, logs). Unlike numerical data, text requires **preprocessing** — tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, TF-IDF weighting — before it can be visualized as word clouds, tag clouds, or document maps.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you have 10,000 news articles and you want to know *"What is the news about?"* Reading all 10,000 is impossible. But if you extract every word, count them, and display the most frequent words in sizes proportional to their frequency — instantly, you see that "election," "economy," and "climate" dominate. That's a **word cloud**. Now imagine grouping similar articles together as a map of islands — that's a **document map**. Text visualization turns unreadable text corpora into instantly readable images.

**Standard Text Visualization Techniques:**

| Technique | Purpose | Visual Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Cloud / Tag Cloud** | Word frequency | Font size |
| **Document Map** | Document similarity | 2D point position |
| **ThemeRiver** | Topic evolution over time | Colored streams |
| **Phrase Nets** | Relationships between words | Connected phrases |
| **Word Tree** | Sentence-level context | Tree of phrases |
| **Heatmap (Term × Document)** | Term frequency matrix | Color intensity |
| **Node-Link Graph** | Entity relationships | Nodes + edges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Text Preprocessing Pipeline (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> Raw Text $\rightarrow$ **Tokenization** $\rightarrow$ **Stop-word Removal** $\rightarrow$ **Stemming/Lemmatization** $\rightarrow$ **TF-IDF Weighting** $\rightarrow$ Visual Encoding. Skipping any of these steps results in poor visualizations (e.g., "the," "is," "a" dominating word clouds).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: From Data Dimension to Visual Channel

The fundamental task of data visualization is **mapping a data dimension to a visual channel**. The mapping must respect perceptual accuracy (Cleveland-McGill) and the data type (quantitative, ordinal, nominal).

### 2.1.1 Mapping Rules by Data Dimensionality

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Identify data type per variable** — Quantitative (continuous/discrete), Ordinal, or Nominal.
2. **Match each variable to a visual channel** based on Bertin's retinal variables.
3. **Apply perceptual hierarchy** — Position $>$ Length $>$ Angle $>$ Area $>$ Color.
4. **Validate by pre-attentive processing** — Patterns should "pop out" within 200ms.

**Channel Selection Logic (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Data Type | Best Visual Channel | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Quantitative (continuous) | Position along scale | Highest perceptual accuracy |
| Quantitative (discrete) | Length (bar) | Direct magnitude reading |
| Ordinal | Ordered position, size | Preserves rank |
| Nominal | Color hue, shape, region | Distinguishes categories |
| Temporal | Position along time axis | Sequential logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Position Dominates:** Human visual cortex uses position through a **logarithmic-scale mechanism** (Stevens, 1957). We can distinguish 6–7 positions on a line, 2–3 sizes, 4 hues, and 2–3 shapes within 200 ms — the basis for the **4±1 channel rule** in multidimensional visualization.

---

### 2.1.2 Histogram vs. Bar Chart — Conceptual Boundary

This is a **favourite KTU question**. The distinction:

- **Bar Chart** — Used for *categorical* (nominal) data. Bars do NOT touch; x-axis is categorical.
- **Histogram** — Used for *continuous numerical* data. Bars TOUCH; x-axis is a binned numerical scale. The y-axis represents **frequency density**, not raw count, to make area proportional to probability.

**Formula for Histogram Density:**

$$f_k = \frac{n_k}{N \cdot w}$$

where $f_k$ is the frequency density of bin $k$, $n_k$ is the count in bin $k$, $N$ is the total count, and $w$ is the bin width. The total area under the histogram equals **1**, making it a valid probability density estimator.

---

## 2.2 Two-Dimensional Data: Correlation and Covisualization

### 2.2.1 Pearson Correlation Coefficient (r)

The most important measure of 2D linear relationship. Visualized in scatter plots as the **slope of the best-fit line**.

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

**Interpretation of |r|:**

| $\vert r \vert$ | Strength | Visual Signature |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 – 0.2 | Negligible | Round cloud |
| 0.2 – 0.4 | Weak | Slight ellipse |
| 0.4 – 0.7 | Moderate | Clear ellipse |
| 0.7 – 0.9 | Strong | Narrow ellipse |
| 0.9 – 1.0 | Very strong | Near-line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spearman vs. Pearson:** Pearson captures **linear** correlation; Spearman captures **monotonic** (rank-based) correlation. Scatter plots can reveal non-linear relationships that $r$ misses — a classic KTU pitfall!

### 2.2.2 Binning for 2D Histograms (Hexbin Plots)

When point density is high, scatter plots overplot. The solution is **hexagonal binning** — the plane is divided into hexagonal cells, and color encodes the count in each cell.

**Bin Count Heuristic (Sturges' rule for 2D):**

$$k = \lceil \log_2(N) + 1 \rceil$$

For 2D, the number of bins is often taken as $k^2$ (or roughly $\lceil N^{2/5} \rceil$ per axis).

---

## 2.3 Multidimensional Data: Encoding Strategies

### 2.3.1 Parallel Coordinates Theory

In a parallel coordinates plot, each variable is represented as a **parallel vertical axis**, and each observation is a **polyline** that crosses all axes at its corresponding values. With $n$ variables, you need $n$ parallel axes.

**Advantage:** Handles $n$ dimensions in 2D space; reveals **clusters** (bundles of parallel lines), **outliers** (lines that cross others), and **correlations** (axes with monotonic relationships).

**Limitation:** Axis order matters — different orderings reveal different patterns.

### 2.3.2 Radar / Spider Charts

Used for $n$-dimensional comparison of entities (e.g., comparing 5 products on 6 attributes). Each variable is a **spoke** from the center, and the entity is a **closed polygon** through its values.

**Limitation:** Area scales non-linearly with values; axis order affects shape. Best for **$n \leq 8$**.

### 2.3.3 Chernoff Faces

Maps each variable to a facial feature: eye size, mouth curvature, nose length, etc. Humans are exceptionally good at recognizing **faces** (familiarity advantage). Up to **18 dimensions** can be encoded.

### 2.3.4 Scatter Plot Matrix (SPLOM)

A grid of all pairwise scatter plots for $n$ variables. For $n$ variables, the matrix has $n^2$ cells, but only $n(n-1)/2$ are unique (upper triangle). Reveals pairwise correlations at a glance.

### 2.3.5 Dimensionality Reduction for Visualization

When $n$ is very large (genomics, NLP), project to 2D/3D using:

- **PCA (Principal Component Analysis):** Linear projection maximizing variance.
- **t-SNE / UMAP:** Non-linear, preserves local neighborhoods.

---

## 2.4 Text Visualization: The TF-IDF Backbone

### 2.4.1 Term Frequency (TF)

The simplest measure — frequency of term $t$ in document $d$:

$$\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

### 2.4.2 Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

Reduces the weight of terms that appear in many documents (e.g., "the"):

$$\text{IDF}(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

where $N$ is total documents and $df_t$ is the number of documents containing term $t$.

### 2.4.3 TF-IDF Score

The combined weight used in word clouds and document maps:

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$$

### 2.4.4 Document Similarity (Cosine Similarity)

For document-document visualization (e.g., document maps), similarity between two documents $d_i$ and $d_j$ represented as TF-IDF vectors is:

$$\text{sim}(d_i, d_j) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{V}_{d_i} \cdot \vec{V}_{d_j}}{\Vert \vec{V}_{d_i} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{V}_{d_j} \Vert}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Histogram Density | $f_k = \frac{n_k}{N \cdot w}$ | 1D continuous data |
| 2 | Sturges' Bins | $k = \lceil \log_2 N + 1 \rceil$ | Histogram bin count |
| 3 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | 2D scatter plots |
| 4 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Box plot, histogram spread |
| 5 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Error bars, box plot whiskers |
| 6 | Quartile (Q1, Q2, Q3) | Median of halves | Box plot hinges |
| 7 | IQR (Interquartile Range) | $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | Outlier detection (1.5×IQR rule) |
| 8 | TF | $\text{TF}(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t'} f_{t',d}}$ | Word cloud weight |
| 9 | IDF | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | Text preprocessing |
| 10 | TF-IDF | $\text{TF}(t,d) \times \text{IDF}(t)$ | Document maps |
| 11 | Cosine Similarity | $\frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B} \Vert}$ | Document clustering |
| 12 | Total Histogram Area | $\sum_k f_k \cdot w = 1$ | PDF normalization |
| 13 | Hexagonal Bin Count | $\approx N^{2/5}$ per axis | Hexbin plots |
| 14 | Max Encoded Dimensions | $\leq 4 + 1$ (position + 4 channels) | Multi-D design |
| 15 | Outlier Threshold | $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ or $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ | Box plot fences |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **1D visualization** is used in *quality control* (SPC charts), *finance* (return distributions), *signal processing* (amplitude histograms).
> - **2D visualization** is the *backbone of EDA* (Exploratory Data Analysis) and *regression diagnostics*.
> - **Multi-D visualization** is critical in *bioinformatics* (gene expression), *machine learning* (feature spaces), and *sensor fusion*.
> - **Text visualization** drives *social media analytics*, *legal e-discovery*, *search engine relevance*, and *intelligence analysis*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Histogram as a Probability Density Estimator

**Goal:** Show that a properly normalized histogram approximates a Probability Density Function (PDF).

**Step 1 — Define the bin:**
Divide the data range $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ into $k$ equal-width bins of width $w = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{k}$.

**Step 2 — Count observations per bin:**
For each bin $B_j$, let $n_j$ = number of observations falling in $B_j$. Then $\sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j = N$ (total observations).

**Step 3 — Define the histogram height $h_j$:**

$$h_j = \frac{n_j}{N \cdot w}$$

**Step 4 — Verify total area = 1:**

$$\text{Total Area} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} h_j \cdot w = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N \cdot w} \cdot w = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j = \frac{N}{N} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
Since the total area equals 1 and $h_j \geq 0$, the histogram satisfies the two axioms of a probability density function. As $N \to \infty$ and $w \to 0$, the histogram converges to the true PDF — this is the **Histogram Density Estimator Theorem**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: TF-IDF Computation

**Problem:** A corpus has $N = 1000$ documents. For a specific document $d$ with 200 total words, the term "algorithm" appears **6 times**, and the term "the" appears **20 times**. The term "algorithm" appears in 50 documents, and "the" appears in 950 documents. Calculate TF-IDF for both terms and explain the word cloud implication.

**Step 1 — Compute TF for "algorithm" in document $d$:**

$$\text{TF}(\text{algorithm}, d) = \frac{f_{\text{algorithm}, d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t', d}} = \frac{6}{200} = 0.030$$

**Step 2 — Compute TF for "the" in document $d$:**

$$\text{TF}(\text{the}, d) = \frac{20}{200} = 0.100$$

**Step 3 — Compute IDF for "algorithm":**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{algorithm}) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_{\text{algorithm}}}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{50}\right) = \log(20) \approx 2.996$$

**Step 4 — Compute IDF for "the":**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{the}) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{950}\right) = \log(1.0526) \approx 0.0513$$

**Step 5 — Compute TF-IDF for "algorithm":**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{algorithm}, d) = 0.030 \times 2.996 \approx 0.0899$$

**Step 6 — Compute TF-IDF for "the":**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{the}, d) = 0.100 \times 0.0513 \approx 0.00513$$

**Conclusion:**
Even though "the" appears 3.3× more often than "algorithm" (raw TF: 0.100 vs 0.030), the TF-IDF for "algorithm" (0.0899) is **17.5× higher** than for "the" (0.00513). In a word cloud, "algorithm" will be displayed in a significantly larger font, correctly reflecting its importance as a *discriminating* term — a direct application of the IDF principle.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Pearson Correlation for 2D Scatter Plot

**Problem:** Given 5 observations: $(1,2), (2,4), (3,5), (4,4), (5,7)$. Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient $r$ and interpret the scatter plot trend.

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3.0$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5+4+7}{5} = 4.4$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations and their products:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | -2 | -2.4 | 4.80 | 4 | 5.76 |
| 2 | 4 | -1 | -0.4 | 0.40 | 1 | 0.16 |
| 3 | 5 | 0 | 0.6 | 0.00 | 0 | 0.36 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | -0.4 | -0.40 | 1 | 0.16 |
| 5 | 7 | 2 | 2.6 | 5.20 | 4 | 6.76 |
| **Sum** | | | | **10.00** | **10** | **13.20** |

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

$$r = \frac{10.00}{\sqrt{10 \times 13.20}} = \frac{10.00}{\sqrt{132}} = \frac{10.00}{11.489} \approx 0.870$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Since $r \approx 0.87$, there is a **strong positive correlation** between $x$ and $y$. The scatter plot will show a tight elliptical cloud sloping upward from lower-left to upper-right. The point $(4, 4)$ is mildly off-trend (mild leverage point).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Visualization by Data Type

| Data Dimensionality | Data Type | Recommended Visualization | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1D (continuous) | Numeric | Histogram, Box plot, Density plot | Shows distribution shape, outliers, spread |
| 1D (categorical) | Nominal | Bar chart, Pie chart | Length/angle encodes proportion |
| 1D (ordinal) | Rank | Ordered bar chart, Dot plot | Preserves rank order |
| 2D (continuous) | Numeric pairs | Scatter plot | Position encodes both variables |
| 2D (time-series) | Time + value | Line chart | Time on x, value on y |
| 2D (bivariate density) | Counts in grid | Hexbin plot, Heatmap | Handles overplotting |
| 3D | Three numerics | 3D scatter, Bubble chart | Add size or 3rd axis |
| 3–8 D | Mixed | Parallel coordinates, Radar, SPLOM | Each variable gets a channel |
| 8–50 D | Mixed | Chernoff faces, PCA/t-SNE projection | Glyphs or projection |
| Hierarchical | Tree structure | Treemap, Sunburst, Icicle plot | Nesting encodes hierarchy |
| Text (term frequency) | Words | Word cloud, Tag cloud | Size = frequency |
| Text (corpus) | Documents | Document map, ThemeRiver | Position = similarity |
| Text (relationships) | Entities | Node-link graph, Phrase net | Edges encode co-occurrence |
| Text (matrix) | Term × Document | Heatmap | Color = TF-IDF |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Box Plot Construction

**Goal:** Show the exact construction of a 1D box plot.

**Step 1 — Order the data** $x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq x_{(n)}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the median $Q_2$:** The middle value; if $n$ is odd, $Q_2 = x_{((n+1)/2)}$; if even, $Q_2 = \frac{x_{(n/2)} + x_{(n/2+1)}}{2}$.

**Step 3 — Compute $Q_1$ and $Q_3$:** $Q_1$ is the median of the lower half; $Q_3$ is the median of the upper half.

**Step 4 — Compute IQR:**

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

**Step 5 — Determine the fences (Tukey's rule):**

$$\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$$

$$\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$$

**Step 6 — Whiskers extend** to the most extreme data points within the fences; points beyond are plotted as individual outliers (often as dots or asterisks).

**Step 7 — Visual encoding:**
- **Box** spans $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ (50% of data).
- **Line inside box** marks $Q_2$ (median).
- **Whiskers** extend to the last data point within 1.5×IQR.
- **Dots beyond whiskers** are outliers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Data-to-Visualization Decision Tree

This diagram maps data dimensionality to the appropriate visualization technique, based on the decision logic derived in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset Arrives] --> B{How many variables per observation?}
    
    B -->|n = 1| C[One-Dimensional Data]
    B -->|n = 2| D[Two-Dimensional Data]
    B -->|n = 3 to 8| E[Multi-Dimensional Low]
    B -->|n = 8 to 50| F[Multi-Dimensional High]
    B -->|Text / Documents| G[Text Data]
    
    C --> C1{Continuous or Categorical?}
    C1 -->|Continuous| C2[Histogram / Density Plot / Box Plot]
    C1 -->|Categorical| C3[Bar Chart / Pie Chart]
    
    D --> D1{Relationship Type?}
    D1 -->|Time vs Value| D2[Line Chart]
    D1 -->|Two Continuous| D3[Scatter Plot]
    D1 -->|High Density| D4[Hexbin / Heatmap]
    
    E --> E1{Channel Available?}
    E1 -->|Position Channels OK| E2[Parallel Coordinates]
    E1 -->|Polar Encoding| E3[Radar / Spider Chart]
    E1 -->|Pairwise View| E4[Scatter Plot Matrix SPLOM]
    
    F --> F1{Reduction Needed?}
    F1 -->|Yes Linear| F2[PCA Projection to 2D / 3D]
    F1 -->|Yes Non-Linear| F3[t-SNE / UMAP Projection]
    F1 -->|No Glyphs OK| F4[Chernoff Faces / Star Glyphs]
    
    G --> G1{Visualization Goal?}
    G1 -->|Frequency of Terms| G2[Word Cloud / Tag Cloud]
    G1 -->|Document Similarity| G3[Document Map with TF-IDF]
    G1 -->|Topic over Time| G4[ThemeRiver]
    G1 -->|Entity Relations| G5[Node-Link Graph / Phrase Net]
    G1 -->|Term x Document| G6[Heatmap with Cosine Similarity]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Text Visualization Preprocessing Pipeline

This block-level architecture shows the complete pipeline from raw text to a final visualization, an essential concept for KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Text Corpus] --> T1[Tokenization]
    T1 --> T2[Lowercasing]
    T2 --> T3[Stop-word Removal]
    T3 --> T4[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    T4 --> T5[TF Computation]
    T4 --> T6[IDF Computation]
    T5 --> T7[TF-IDF Weighting]
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> V1[Word Cloud]
    T7 --> V2[Document Term Matrix]
    V2 --> V3[Cosine Similarity]
    V3 --> V4[Document Map]
    V2 --> V5[Heatmap of Term by Document]
    T7 --> V6[Phrase Net / Word Tree]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Dimensional Encoding Channels (Bertin's Retinal Variables)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Data Variables x1, x2, ..., xn] --> E{Visual Channel Mapping}
    
    E --> C1[Position on X axis]
    E --> C2[Position on Y axis]
    E --> C3[Size / Length]
    E --> C4[Color Hue]
    E --> C5[Color Saturation]
    E --> C6[Shape]
    E --> C7[Orientation]
    
    C1 --> F[2D Display Surface]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    C5 --> F
    C6 --> F
    C7 --> F
    
    F --> V[Final Multi-D Visualization]
    
    P[Perceptual Accuracy Hierarchy] -.->|Highest| C1
    P -.->|High| C2
    P -.->|Medium| C3
    P -.->|Medium| C4
    P -.->|Low| C6
    P -.->|Lowest| C7
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Parallel Coordinates Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Axis 1: x1] --> A2[Axis 2: x2]
    A2 --> A3[Axis 3: x3]
    A3 --> A4[Axis 4: x4]
    A4 --> A5[Axis 5: x5]
    
    O1[Observation 1 polyline] -.-> A1
    O1 -.-> A2
    O1 -.-> A3
    O1 -.-> A4
    O1 -.-> A5
    
    O2[Observation 2 polyline] -.-> A1
    O2 -.-> A2
    O2 -.-> A3
    O2 -.-> A4
    O2 -.-> A5
    
    B[Observation 3 polyline crosses - indicates inverse correlation] -.-> A1
    B -.-> A2
    B -.-> A3
    B -.-> A4
    B -.-> A5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:**
> In parallel coordinates, **parallel polylines indicate positive correlation** between adjacent axes. **Crossing polylines indicate negative or non-monotonic relationships**. This makes high-D correlation patterns immediately visible — a property that scatter plot matrices cannot match for $n > 4$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---
